abstract:
BACKGROUND: Compulsory admission to psychiatric hospitals may be distressing, disruptive to patients and families, and associated with considerable cost to the health service. Improved patient experience and cost reductions could be realised by providing cost-effective crisis planning services.

METHODS: Economic evaluation within a multi-centre randomised controlled trial comparing Joint Crisis Plans (JCP) plus treatment as usual (TAU) to TAU alone for patients aged over 16, with at least one psychiatric hospital admission in the previous two years and on the Enhanced Care Programme Approach register. JCPs, containing the patient's treatment preferences for any future psychiatric emergency, are a form of crisis intervention that aim to mitigate the negative consequences of relapse, including hospital admission and use of coercion. Data were collected at baseline and 18-months after randomisation. The primary outcome was admission to hospital under the Mental Health Act. The economic evaluation took a service perspective (health, social care and criminal justice services) and a societal perspective (additionally including criminal activity and productivity losses).FINDINGS: The addition of JCPs to TAU had no significant effect on compulsory admissions or total societal cost per participant over 18-months follow-up. From the service cost perspective, however, evidence suggests a higher probability (80%) of JCPs being the more cost-effective option. Exploration by ethnic group highlights distinct patterns of costs and effects. Whilst the evidence does not support the cost-effectiveness of JCPs for White or Asian ethnic groups, there is at least a 90% probability of the JCP intervention being the more cost-effective option in the Black ethnic group.

INTERPRETATION: The results by ethnic group are sufficiently striking to warrant further investigation into the potential for patient gain from JCPs among black patient groups.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine a hospital for people who need help with their mental health.  Sometimes, people have to go there against their will, which is upsetting for them and their families, and it's expensive.

Doctors tried a new plan called a "Joint Crisis Plan" (JCP).  It's like a special instruction manual written with the patient, explaining what helps them when they're feeling really bad, so they can avoid going to the hospital.

They compared people who used the JCP to people who didn't.  They looked at how much it cost and whether it helped people avoid being forced to go to the hospital.

Overall, the JCP didn't make a big difference in how many people were forced to go to the hospital.  But, surprisingly, it seemed to save money for the hospital and government *sometimes*.  It seemed to be especially helpful and cost-effective for Black patients.  Doctors want to study this more to understand why.

Title: Findings of a randomized controlled trial using limited transthoracic echocardiogram (LTTE) as a hemodynamic monitoring tool in the trauma bay.
Journal: Journal of Trauma and Acute Care Surgery
Spin in Abstract Title/Results: RESULTS: Focus on (+) modified treatment population (e.g., report per-protocol rather than intention to treat)
abstract:
BACKGROUND: This study evaluates the effect of limited transthoracic echocardiogram (LTTE) on the initial phase of resuscitation in trauma patients.

METHODS: All highest-level alert patients with at least one measurement of systolic blood pressure less than 100 mm Hg, a mean arterial pressure less than 60 mm Hg, and/or a heart rate greater than 120 beats per minute who arrived at the trauma bay (TB) were randomized to have either LTTE performed (LTTEp) or not performed (non-LTTE) as part of their initial evaluation. Time from TB to operating room, intravenous fluid administration, blood product requirement, intensive care unit admission, and mortality were examined in both groups.

RESULTS: A total of 240 patients were randomized. Twenty-five patients were excluded since they died upon arrival to the TB, leaving 215 patients in the study. Ninety-two patients were in the LTTEp group with 123 patients in the non-LTTE group. The LTTEp and non-LTTE groups were similar in age (38 years vs. 38.8 years, p = 0.75), Injury Severity Score (ISS) (19.2 vs. 19.0, p = 0.94), Revised Trauma Score (RTS) (5.5 vs. 6.0, p = 0.09), lactate (4.2 vs. 3.6, p = 0.14), and mechanism of injury (p = 0.44). LTTEp had less intravenous fluid than non-LTTE patients (1.5 L vs. 2.5 L, p < 0.0001) and less time from TB to operating room (35.6 minutes vs. 79.1 min, p = 0.0006). The rate of intensive care unit admission was 80.4% in the LTTEp group compared to 67.2% in the non-LTTE group (p = 0.04). Mortality rates were 11% in the LTTEp group and 19.5% in the non-LTTE group (p = 0.09).

CONCLUSION: The treatment with LTTE was not more effective than the comparator in patients with hypotensive trauma during the early phase of resuscitation.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine a hospital is trying to figure out the best way to help people who are badly hurt in accidents.  Some of these people have very low blood pressure and a fast heart rate.

They wanted to see if a quick heart ultrasound (called a "limited transthoracic echocardiogram" or LTTE – it's like a picture of their heart) would help doctors treat them faster and better.

They split the injured people into two groups: one group got the quick heart ultrasound, and the other group didn't.  They then watched to see how long it took to get them to surgery, how much fluid they needed, if they needed to go to the intensive care unit (ICU), and if they survived.

What they found was that the group who got the quick heart ultrasound needed less fluid and got to surgery faster.  A slightly higher percentage of the ultrasound group also went to the ICU, but the difference in survival rates wasn't big enough to say for sure that the ultrasound made a difference.

So, even though the ultrasound helped some things, the doctors concluded that it didn't make a big enough difference overall in how well the injured people did.

Journal: Journal of Trauma and Acute Care Surgery

BACKGROUND: We hypothesize that limited transthoracic echocardiogram (LTTE) is a useful tool to guide therapy during the initial phase of resuscitation in trauma patients.

METHODS: All highest-level alert patients with at least one measurement of systolic blood pressure less than 100 mm Hg, a mean arterial pressure less than 60 mm Hg, and/or a heart rate greater than 120 beats per minute who arrived to the trauma bay (TB) were randomized to have either LTTE performed (LTTEp) or not performed (non-LTTE) as part of their initial evaluation. Images were stored, and results were reported regarding contractility (good vs. poor), fluid status (empty inferior vena cava [hypovolemic] vs. full inferior vena cava [not hypovolemic]), and pericardial effusion (present vs. absent). Time from TB to operating room, intravenous fluid administration, blood product requirement, intensive care unit admission, and mortality were examined in both groups.

RESULTS: A total of 240 patients were randomized. Twenty-five patients were excluded since they died upon arrival to the TB, leaving 215 patients in the study. Ninety-two patients were in the LTTEp group with 123 patients in the non-LTTE group. The LTTEp and non-LTTE groups were similar in age (38 years vs. 38.8 years, p = 0.75), Injury Severity Score (ISS) (19.2 vs. 19.0, p = 0.94), Revised Trauma Score (RTS) (5.5 vs. 6.0, p = 0.09), lactate (4.2 vs. 3.6, p = 0.14), and mechanism of injury (p = 0.44). Strikingly, LTTEp had significantly less intravenous fluid than non-LTTE patients (1.5 L vs. 2.5 L, p < 0.0001), less time from TB to operating room (35.6 minutes vs. 79.1 min, p = 0.0006), higher rate of intensive care unit admission (80.4% vs. 67.2%, p = 0.04), and a lower mortality rate (11% vs. 19.5%, p = 0.09). Mortality differences were particularly evident in the traumatic brain injury patients (14.7% in LTTEp vs. 39.5% in non-LTTE, p = 0.03).

CONCLUSION: LTTE is a useful guide for therapy in hypotensive trauma patients during the early phase of resuscitation.

Imagine a hospital is trying to help people who were hurt in accidents.  Some of these people are really, really sick and their blood pressure is low.

Doctors wanted to test a special kind of quick ultrasound (like a super-fast picture of the heart) called LTTE to see if it helps them decide how to treat these patients faster.  They split the sick people into two groups: one group got the LTTE ultrasound, and the other group didn't.

They looked at things like how much fluid the patients needed, how long it took to get them to surgery, if they needed intensive care, and if they lived or died.

What they found was amazing! The group that got the LTTE ultrasound needed less fluid, got to surgery faster, and had a better chance of surviving, especially those with head injuries.

So, the doctors think this quick ultrasound is a really helpful tool to help save lives when people are seriously hurt.

Title: No difference in blood loss between posterior-cruciate-ligament-retaining and posterior-cruciate-ligament-stabilized total knee arthroplasties.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
PURPOSE: Posterior-cruciate-ligament-retaining (PCR) and posterior-cruciate-ligament-stabilized (PS) arthroplasties are two common practices in total knee arthroplasty (TKA). This study aimed to compare the postoperative blood loss and need for blood transfusion between the PCR and PS techniques in cemented TKA.

METHODS: In this prospective, randomized study, 100 patients diagnosed with primary knee osteoarthritis were randomly assigned to either the PCR group (Group I) or the PS group (Group II). The exclusion criteria were rheumatological joint disease, previous knee surgery, anticoagulant therapy, and hypertension. There were no significant differences in age, body mass index, and gender between the groups. The primary outcome was the total measured blood loss volume. Haemoglobin and haematocrit levels were recorded preoperatively and on postoperative days 1, 3, and 5. Postoperative suction drainage and blood transfusion volumes were also recorded.

RESULTS: There were no statistically significant differences in haemoglobin or haematocrit levels between the groups on postoperative days 1, 3, and 5. There were also no statistically significant differences in the total measured blood loss volume, postoperative drainage amounts, or transfusion rates between the groups. Safety data, including reasons for withdrawals and treatment discontinuation, were not reported.

CONCLUSION: The PS technique was not more effective than the PCR technique in reducing perioperative blood loss or the need for postoperative blood transfusion in patients undergoing cemented TKA.

Imagine your knee is a hinge.  Sometimes, that hinge gets worn out and hurts (like in osteoarthritis).  Doctors can replace the worn-out parts with new ones – that's called a total knee replacement (TKA).

There are two ways to do this replacement: the PCR way and the PS way.  Both are like slightly different ways of putting the new knee parts in.

Doctors wanted to see if one way caused less bleeding after the surgery.  They studied 100 people getting knee replacements.  50 got the PCR way, and 50 got the PS way.

They measured how much blood each person lost.  Guess what?  They found that there wasn't much difference in blood loss between the two ways!  So, it doesn't seem like one way is better than the other at reducing bleeding.

Title: Intra-articular bupivacaine reduces postoperative pain and meperidine use after total hip arthroplasty: a randomized, double-blind study.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract: One hundred patients receiving unilateral total hip arthroplasty (THA) were randomized to receive an intra-articular injection of 300mg bupivacaine or normal saline after completion of surgery. Pain scores of the bupivacaine group were significantly lower than those of the control group the first 12hours postoperatively (all, P<0.001). A significantly lower dose of meperidine was used in the study group than in the control group the first 24hours postoperatively (median, 25 vs. 45mg, P<0.001). Nineteen patients in the study group required meperidine the first day after surgery, as compared to 45 patients in the control group. We conclude that intra-articular injection of bupivacaine after THA reduces pain and meperidine use in the first 12hours after surgery.

Imagine 100 people got new hip joints.  Half of them got a special shot (bupivacaine) in their hip after the surgery, and the other half got a regular salt water shot (this is the "control group," like a comparison).

So, the special shot helped reduce pain and the need for strong pain medicine after hip surgery.

Title: Effect of surgical closing in total knee arthroplasty at flexion or extension: a prospective, randomized study.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
Purpose: The aim of this study was to evaluate the effect of knee position during wound closure (flexed vs. extended) in total knee arthroplasty on knee strength and function.

Methods: In a prospective, randomized, double-blind trial, 29 patients were divided into two groups: for Group 1 patients, surgical closing was performed with the knee extended, and for Group 2 patients, the knee flexed at 90°. All patients were treated with the same anesthesia method, surgical team, surgical technique, prosthesis type, and rehabilitation process. The primary outcome was the American Knee Society Score values. Isokinetic muscle strength measurements of both knees in flexion and extension were taken using 60° and 180°/s angular velocity.

Results: No significant difference in the mean American Knee Society Score values and knee flexion degrees was observed between the two groups. Safety data, including reasons for withdrawals and treatment discontinuation, were not reported.

Conclusion: The treatment of knee flexion during wound closure was not more effective than knee extension in patients undergoing total knee arthroplasty.

Imagine your knee is broken and needs a new, artificial one put in – that's called a total knee arthroplasty.  Doctors wanted to see if it mattered whether the knee was bent (flexed) or straight (extended) when the surgery was finished and the wound was closed.

They had two groups of people getting this surgery.  One group had their knees straight when the wound was closed, and the other group had their knees bent.  They made sure everything else about the surgery was exactly the same for both groups.

After the surgery, they checked how strong the patients' knees were and how well they could bend.  They found that it didn't make any difference whether the knee was bent or straight during the closing of the wound – both groups got equally strong and bendy knees.

So, the doctors concluded that it doesn't matter if the knee is bent or straight when the surgeon closes the wound after this type of knee surgery.

Title: Effect of surgical closing in total knee arthroplasty at flexion or extension: a prospective, randomized study.

abstract:
Purpose:В The aim of this study was to evaluate the effect of knee position during wound closure (flexed vs. extended) in total knee arthroplasty on knee strength and function, as determined by knee society scores and isokinetic testing of extensor and flexor muscle groups. 
Methods:В In a prospective, randomized, double-blind trial, 29 patients were divided in two groups: for Group 1 patients, surgical closing was performed with the knee extended, and for Group 2 patients, the knee flexed at 90В°. All the patients were treated with the same anaesthesia method, surgical team, surgical technique, prosthesis type, and rehabilitation process. American Knee Society Score values and knee flexion degrees were recorded. Isokinetic muscle strength measurements of both knees in flexion and extension were taken using 60В° and 180В°/s angular velocity. The peak torque and total work values, isokinetic muscle strength differences, and total work difference values were calculated for surgically repaired and healthy knees.
 Results:В No significant difference in the mean American Knee Society Score values and knee flexion degrees was observed between the two groups. However, using isokinetic evaluation, a significant difference was found in the isokinetic muscle strength differences and total work difference of the flexor muscle between the two groups when patients were tested at 180В°/s. Less loss of strength was detected in the isokinetic muscle strength differences of the flexor muscle in Group 2 (-4.2%) than in Group 1 (-23.1%). 
Conclusion:В For patients undergoing total knee arthroplasty, post-operative flexor muscle strength is improved if the knee is flexed during wound closure.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is broken and needs a new part, like a robot knee!  Doctors did a study to see if the way they close up the wound after surgery makes a difference in how strong the knee gets afterward.

They had two groups of people:

Title: Effectiveness of sustained stretching of the inferior capsule in the management of a frozen shoulder.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
Background: Physiotherapy treatment of frozen shoulder is varied, but most lack specific focus on the underlying disorder, which is the adhered shoulder capsule. Although positive effects were found after physiotherapy, the recurrence and prolonged disability of a frozen shoulder are major factors to focus on to provide the appropriate treatment.

Questions/purposes: We wished to study the effectiveness of a shoulder countertraction apparatus on ROM, pain, and function in patients with a frozen shoulder and compare their results with those of control subjects who received conventional physiotherapy.

Methods: A total of 100 participants were randomly assigned to an experimental group and a control group, with each group having 50 participants. The control group received physiotherapy and the experimental group received countertraction and physiotherapy. The total treatment time was 20 minutes a day for 5 days per week for 2 weeks. The outcome measures used were goniometer measurements, VAS, and the Oxford Shoulder Score.

Results: Improvements were seen in the scores for shoulder flexion (94.1Ў ± 19.79Ў at baseline increased to 161.9Ў ± 13.05Ў after intervention), abduction ROM (90.4Ў ± 21.18Ў at baseline increased to 154.8Ў ± 13.21Ў after intervention), and pain (8.00 ± 0.78 at baseline decreased to 3.48 ± 0.71 after intervention) in the experimental group. Sixty percent of the participants (n = 30) were improved to the fourth stage of satisfactory joint function according to the Oxford Shoulder Score in the experimental group compared with 18% (n = 9) in the control group (p < 0.001).

Conclusions: Incorporating shoulder countertraction along with physiotherapy improves shoulder function compared with physiotherapy alone for the treatment of a frozen shoulder. Additional studies are needed focusing on this concept to increase the generalizability of the counter-traction apparatus in various groups.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is stiff and hurts a lot – that's a "frozen shoulder."  Doctors often help with exercises (physiotherapy), but sometimes it doesn't get better completely.

Scientists wanted to see if a special machine (a countertraction apparatus) could help *in addition* to the exercises.  They had 100 people with frozen shoulders.  Half got just the exercises, and half got the exercises *plus* the machine.

They measured how much people could move their arms, how much pain they had, and how well their shoulder worked.  The group that used the machine *and* the exercises got much better! They could move their arms more, had less pain, and their shoulders worked much better overall.

So, using the machine along with exercises seems to be a better way to treat frozen shoulders than just exercises alone.  But they need to do more tests to be completely sure it works for everyone.

Title: Is the newest fibrin sealant an effective strategy to reduce blood loss after total knee arthroplasty? A randomized controlled study.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract: An RCT was conducted to ascertain whether, compared to control management, topical application of a novel fibrin sealant (Evicel, J&J) in patients undergoing primary TKA reduces peri-operative blood loss. Sixty-two patients were randomized to receive topical application of Evicel (N = 31) or not (N = 31). The primary outcome was total peri-operative blood loss. The mean total blood loss was 1.9L (± 0.7) in the control group and 1.8L (± 0.5) in the treatment group (P = 0.4). The transfusion rate was 32.3% in the control group and 25.8% in the treatment group (P = 0.5). The treatment with Evicel was not more effective than control management in reducing peri-operative blood loss in patients undergoing primary TKA.
plain_language_summary:

Half the people got the sticky stuff put on their knee after the surgery (the treatment group), and half didn't (the control group).

They measured how much blood each person lost.  The people who got the sticky stuff lost a tiny bit less blood on average (1.8 liters vs 1.9 liters), but the difference was so small it wasn't really important.  It was also about the same number of people in each group who needed a blood transfusion.

The study showed that the sticky stuff didn't really help reduce blood loss during this type of surgery.

Title: Arthroscopic Bankart repair using knot-tying versus knotless suture anchors: is there a difference?
Journal: Arthroscopy: The Journal of Arthroscopic & Related Surgery
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint, Other
abstract:
Purpose: To compare the clinical outcome between the use of knotless sutures versus knot-tying sutures in arthroscopic Bankart repairs.

Methods: Between January 2007 and January 2011, 87 patients who underwent arthroscopic Bankart repair with the use of knot-tying suture anchors or knotless suture anchors were evaluated, with 45 patients in the knot-tying suture group and 42 patients in the knotless group. Patients were assigned to either group, with odd-numbered patients going to the knot-tying suture arm and even-numbered patients assigned to the knotless arm. Outcomes included the Constant score, the visual analog scale (VAS) score, patient satisfaction score, and range of motion in forward flexion and external rotation with the arm in adduction. Redislocations or subluxations with the 2 techniques was also studied.

Results: Both groups showed a statistically significant improvement between the preoperative and postoperative VAS scores and Constant scores. In the knot-tying suture group, the VAS score improved from 2.5 ± 2.3 to 0.7 ± 0.5 (P < .05) and the Constant score improved from 64 ± 7 to 92 ± 10 (P < .05). In the knotless group, the VAS score improved from 2.8 ± 2.5 to 0.9 ± 0.6 (P < .05), and the Constant score improved from 62 ± 6 to 89 ± 9 (P < .05). The patient satisfaction scores were 6.9 and 7.1 for the knot tying and knotless groups, respectively. No statistically significant differences were found when comparing the outcomes between the 2 groups. The change in the range of forward flexion and external rotation was also similar in the 2 groups. There was also no difference in recurrence or redislocation rates.

Conclusions: Both the knot-tying and knotless suture anchors groups showed statistically significant and similar improvement in VAS and Constant scores. Both anchors provided reasonable outcomes. The knotless suture anchor is a good alternative to knot-tying suture anchors so that arthroscopic Bankart repairs can be performed without knot tying.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is hurt and needs fixing.  Doctors can use two ways to sew it back together:  one uses knots (like tying your shoelaces), and the other doesn't need knots.

This study looked at 87 people who needed their shoulders fixed.  Half got the knot-tying stitches, and half got the knotless stitches.  Doctors checked how well their shoulders got better using different tests (like how much pain they had and how well they could move their arm).

The good news is that *both* groups got much better!  Whether they used knots or not, people's shoulders healed about the same.  There wasn't a big difference between the two ways of sewing.

So, the study showed that the knotless stitches are a good option – they work just as well as the stitches with knots, and they're easier to use.

Imagine your shoulder is like a ball and socket, and sometimes the ball (your upper arm bone) pops out of the socket.  Doctors can fix this with a surgery called an arthroscopic Bankart repair.  They use tiny stitches to hold everything in place.

Title: External rotation immobilization for primary shoulder dislocation: a randomized controlled trial.
Spin in Abstract Title/Results: Focus on (+) modified treatment population (e.g., report per-protocol rather than intention to treat)
abstract:
Background: The traditional treatment for primary anterior shoulder dislocations has been immobilization in a sling with the arm in a position of adduction and internal rotation. Recent data have suggested recurrent instability may be reduced with immobilization in external rotation after primary shoulder dislocation.

Methods: Sixty patients younger than 35 years of age with primary, traumatic, anterior shoulder dislocations were randomized (concealed, computer-generated) to immobilization with either an internal rotation sling (n = 29) or an external rotation brace (n = 31) at a mean of 4 days after closed reduction (range, 1-7 days). Patients with large bony lesions or polytrauma were excluded. The two groups were similar at baseline. Both groups were immobilized for 4 weeks with identical therapy protocols thereafter. Blinded assessments were completed by independent observers for a minimum of 12 months (mean, 25 months; range, 12-43 months). Recurrent instability was defined as a second documented anterior dislocation or multiple episodes of shoulder subluxation severe enough for the patient to request surgical stabilization. Ten patients (17%, five from each group) were lost to follow-up. Reported compliance with immobilization in both groups was 80%.

Results: There was no difference in the rate of recurrent instability between groups: 10 of 27 patients (37%) with the external rotation brace versus 10 of 25 patients (40%) with the sling redislocated or developed symptomatic recurrent instability (p = 0.41). WOSI scores were not different between groups (p = 0.74), and the difference in ASES scores approached statistical significance (p = 0.05), but the magnitude of this difference was small and of uncertain clinical importance.

Conclusions: The treatment with immobilization in external rotation was not more effective than immobilization in internal rotation in patients with primary anterior shoulder dislocation.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder pops out of its socket (that's a dislocation).  Doctors used to always keep it in a sling with your arm tucked in close to your body (internal rotation).  This study wanted to see if it's better to keep your arm pointing slightly outwards (external rotation) instead.

They took 60 young people whose shoulders popped out and split them into two groups. One group had their arms held in close (internal rotation), the other had their arms held slightly out (external rotation).  Both groups kept their arms still for 4 weeks.

After a year or more, they checked to see if anyone's shoulder popped out again.  They found that about the same number of people in both groups had problems again – it didn't matter which way their arm was held during the healing.  So, keeping the arm pointing outwards didn't seem to help prevent the shoulder from popping out again.

Title: External rotation immobilization for primary shoulder dislocation: a randomized controlled trial.

abstract:
Background: The traditional treatment for primary anterior shoulder dislocations has been immobilization in a sling with the arm in a position of adduction and internal rotation. However, recent basic science and clinical data have suggested recurrent instability may be reduced with immobilization in external rotation after primary shoulder dislocation.

Questions/purposes: We performed a randomized controlled trial to compare the (1) frequency of recurrent instability and (2) disease-specific quality-of-life scores after treatment of first-time shoulder dislocation using either immobilization in external rotation or immobilization in internal rotation in a group of young patients.

Methods: Sixty patients younger than 35 years of age with primary, traumatic, anterior shoulder dislocations were randomized (concealed, computer-generated) to immobilization with either an internal rotation sling (n = 29) or an external rotation brace (n = 31) at a mean of 4 days after closed reduction (range, 1-7 days). Patients with large bony lesions or polytrauma were excluded. The two groups were similar at baseline. Both groups were immobilized for 4 weeks with identical therapy protocols thereafter. Blinded assessments were completed by independent observers for a minimum of 12 months (mean, 25 months; range, 12-43 months). Recurrent instability was defined as a second documented anterior dislocation or multiple episodes of shoulder subluxation severe enough for the patient to request surgical stabilization. Validated disease-specific quality-of-life data (Western Ontario Shoulder Instability index [WOSI], American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons evaluation [ASES]) were also collected. Ten patients (17%, five from each group) were lost to followup. Reported compliance with immobilization in both groups was excellent (80%).

Results: With the numbers available, there was no difference in the rate of recurrent instability between groups: 10 of 27 patients (37%) with the external rotation brace versus 10 of 25 patients (40%) with the sling redislocated or developed symptomatic recurrent instability (p = 0.41). WOSI scores were not different between groups (p = 0.74) and, although the difference in ASES scores approached statistical significance (p = 0.05), the magnitude of this difference was small and of uncertain clinical importance.

Conclusions: Despite previous published findings, our results show immobilization in external rotation did not confer a significant benefit versus sling immobilization in the prevention of recurrent instability after primary anterior shoulder dislocation. Further studies with larger numbers may elucidate whether functional outcomes, compliance, or comfort with immobilization can be improved with this device.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder pops out of its socket (that's a shoulder dislocation).  Doctors used to always keep it in a sling with your arm tucked in close to your body (internal rotation).  

This study wanted to see if it's better to keep your arm pointing outwards (external rotation) instead.  They took 60 young people with this problem and split them into two groups: one group had their arm held in close, the other had it held outwards.  Both groups were kept still for 4 weeks.

After a year or more, they checked to see if the shoulder popped out again.  They also checked how well the shoulder worked and how much pain people had.

The results?  It didn't really matter which way they held the arm – about the same number of people in both groups had their shoulder pop out again.  There wasn't a big difference in how well their shoulders worked either.

So, even though some people thought holding the arm outwards would be better, this study didn't prove it.  They might need to do a bigger study to be sure.

Title: A comparison of suprascapular nerve block and procedural sedation analgesia in shoulder dislocation reduction.
Journal: The American Journal of Emergency Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim equivalence/non-inferiority versus control for a (-) endpoint
abstract:
OBJECTIVES: Dislocation of the shoulder joint is one of the most common dislocations. The reduction procedure is a painful procedure. In this study, 2 different treatment groups were compared for pain control during shoulder dislocation reduction. It was aimed to evaluate the differences between the groups in reduction, success, length of hospital stay, complications, side effects, patient-physician satisfaction, and ease of application.

METHODS: The study was planned to be prospective and randomized. As procedural sedation analgesia (SA), titration of ketamine 1 to 2 mg/kg was administered intravenously to group 1. Suprascapular nerve block (SNB) was applied under ultrasound guidance (USG) to group 2. Conformity to normal distribution of variables was examined with the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test. The П‡2 test and Fisher test were used to evaluate differences between the groups in categorical variables and the Mann-Whitney U test, and a value of P<.05 was accepted as statistically significant.

RESULTS: The study comprised a total of 41 patients; 20 in the group 1 and 21 in the group 2. No statistically significant difference was determined between the groups in terms of age (P=.916), sex (P=.972), reduction success (P=.540), and patient-physician satisfaction (P=.198). The time spent in the emergency department (ED) by patients in the SA group was signficantly longer compared with the SNB group. No side effects were observed in the SNB group.CONCLUSIONS: Suprascapular nerve block, which can be easily applied under USG in the ED, can be evaluated as a good alternative to SA in the reduction of shoulder dislocations.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your arm pops out of its socket in your shoulder – that's a shoulder dislocation.  It's pretty common, and putting it back in (reduction) hurts!

Doctors wanted to see which of two ways of reducing pain during this procedure worked better.

* **Group 1:** Got medicine through an IV to help them relax and feel less pain.
* **Group 2:** Got a special shot near their shoulder to numb the area.

They studied 41 people total.  They found that the shot (Group 2) worked just as well as the IV medicine (Group 1) at putting the shoulder back in place and making people happy with the treatment.  The shot group also didn't have any side effects, and they left the emergency room faster!

So, the doctors think the shot is a good way to reduce pain when putting a dislocated shoulder back in place because it's easy, fast, and works well.

Title: Arthroscopic Repair of Full-Thickness Rotator Cuff Tears With and Without Acromioplasty: Randomized Prospective Trial With 2-Year Follow-up.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Acromioplasty is commonly performed during arthroscopic rotator cuff repair, but its effect on short-term outcomes is debated.

PURPOSE: To report the short-term clinical outcomes of patients undergoing arthroscopic repair of full-thickness rotator cuff tears with and without acromioplasty.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 2.

METHODS: Patients undergoing arthroscopic repair of full-thickness rotator cuff tears were randomized into acromioplasty or nonacromioplasty groups. The Simple Shoulder Test (SST), American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons (ASES) score, Constant score, University of California-Los Angeles (UCLA) score, and Short Form-12 (SF-12) health assessment were collected along with physical examination including range of motion and dynamometer strength testing. Intraoperative data including tear size, repair configuration, and concomitant procedures were recorded. Follow-up examination was performed at regular intervals up to 2 years. Preoperative imaging was reviewed to classify the acromial morphologic type, acromial angle, and lateral acromial angulation.

RESULTS: A total of 114 patients were initially enrolled in the study, and 95 (83%; 43 nonacromioplasty, 52 acromioplasty) were available for a minimum 2-year follow-up. There were no significant differences in baseline characteristics, including number of tendons torn, repair configuration, concomitant procedures, and acromion type and angles. Within groups, there was a significant (P < .001) improvement in all functional outcome scores from preoperatively to all follow-up time points, including 2 years, for the nonacromioplasty and acromioplasty groups (ASES score: 55.1-91.5, 48.8-89.0; Constant score: 48.3-75.0, 51.9-78.7, respectively). There were no significant differences in functional outcomes between nonacromioplasty and acromioplasty groups or between subjects with different acromial features at any time point.

CONCLUSION: The results of this study demonstrate no difference in clinical outcomes after rotator cuff repair with or without acromioplasty at 2 years postoperatively.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is like a ball and socket, and the ball (your upper arm bone) needs strong ropes (muscles called the rotator cuff) to hold it in place.  Sometimes these ropes tear.  Doctors can fix them with a small surgery.

This study looked at whether it's better to also do a little extra surgery on the shoulder bone (called acromioplasty) during the rope repair.  They had two groups of people: one group got the extra bone surgery, and one didn't.

They checked how well people's shoulders worked after two years.  Guess what?  It didn't matter if they had the extra bone surgery or not! Both groups got better, and they got equally better.  So, the extra surgery doesn't seem to help much in the long run.

Imagine your shoulder is like a ball and socket, and the rotator cuff is like a group of strong rubber bands holding the ball in place.  Sometimes those rubber bands tear.  Doctors can fix them with a small surgery.

This study was about whether it's better to do a little extra surgery called "acromioplasty" at the same time.  Acromioplasty is like smoothing out a bony part of the shoulder.

Doctors wanted to see if smoothing that bone (acromioplasty) helped people's shoulders get better faster after the main surgery to fix the torn rubber bands.  They compared two groups: one group got the acromioplasty, and one group didn't.

They checked how well people's shoulders worked after two years.  Guess what?  It didn't matter whether they got the extra smoothing surgery or not!  Both groups got better about the same.  So, the extra surgery wasn't really necessary.

Title: Effects of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) on early recovery after total knee arthroplasty (TKA): a randomized, double-blind, controlled study.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract: Functional exercise after total knee arthroplasty (TKA) is necessary. However, it may be a difficult and painful process for the patient. Desirable methods of relieving the patient's pain are worth exploring. Oral supplement of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is a potential option. In the present study, we decide to investigate whether short-term administration of ATP benefits patients undergoing TKA. A total of 244 subjects were randomized to receive 120mg ATP or placebo each day for 4weeks. Significant differences in quadriceps strength, pain scores at postoperative days 7, 14, 21, and 28 and total opioid consumption were detected. It follows that oral supplement of ATP could benefit patients recovering from TKA.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is really, really hurt and needs a new one – like a robot knee!  That's what "total knee arthroplasty" (TKA) means.

After getting a new knee, it's important to do exercises to make it strong again. But that can be tough and hurt a lot.

Doctors wanted to see if giving people a special medicine called ATP could help reduce the pain and make the exercises easier.  They gave some people the medicine and others a fake medicine (a placebo) for four weeks.

They found that the people who got the ATP medicine had stronger leg muscles, less pain, and needed less pain medicine.

So, it seems like this ATP medicine might help people recover faster and easier after getting a new knee!

Title: Effects of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) on early recovery after total knee arthroplasty (TKA): a randomized, double-blind, controlled study.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is broken and needs a new part, like a robot part!  Doctors did that for a bunch of people (244!).  They wanted to see if giving them a special medicine (ATP) would help their leg get stronger faster after the surgery.

They gave half the people the medicine and half got a fake medicine (placebo – it looks like the real thing but doesn't do anything).  They checked how strong their leg muscles were and how much pain they had for a month.

Guess what? The special medicine didn't make their legs stronger or reduce their pain any more than the fake medicine did.  So, it didn't seem to help!

Title: Antiemetic use for nausea and vomiting in adult emergency department patients: randomized controlled trial comparing ondansetron, metoclopramide, and placebo.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage
abstract:
STUDY OBJECTIVE: We compare efficacy of ondansetron and metoclopramide with placebo for adults with undifferentiated emergency department (ED) nausea and vomiting.

METHODS: A prospective, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial was conducted in 2 metropolitan EDs in Melbourne, Australia. Eligible patients with ED nausea and vomiting were randomized to receive 4 mg intravenous ondansetron, 20 mg intravenous metoclopramide, or saline solution placebo. Primary outcome was mean change in visual analog scale (VAS) rating of nausea severity from enrollment to 30 minutes after study drug administration. Secondary outcomes included patient satisfaction, need for rescue antiemetic treatment, and adverse events.

RESULTS: Of 270 recruited patients, 258 (95.6%) were available for analysis. Of these patients, 87 (33.7%) received ondansetron; 88 (34.1%), metoclopramide; and 83 (32.2%), placebo. Baseline characteristics between treatment groups and recruitment site were similar. Mean decrease in VAS score was 27 mm (95% confidence interval [CI] 22 to 33 mm) for ondansetron, 28 mm (95% CI 22 to 34 mm) for metoclopramide, and 23 mm (95% CI 16 to 30 mm) for placebo. Satisfaction with treatment was reported by 54.1% (95% CI 43.5% to 64.5%), 61.6% (95% CI 51.0% to 71.4%), and 59.5% (95% CI 48.4% to 69.9%) for ondansetron, metoclopramide, and placebo, respectively; rescue medication was required by 34.5% (95% CI 25.0% to 45.1%), 17.9% (95% CI 10.8% to 27.2%), and 36.3% (95% CI 26.3% to 47.2%), respectively. Nine minor adverse events were reported.

CONCLUSION: Reductions in nausea severity for this adult ED nausea and vomiting population were similar for 4 mg intravenous ondansetron, 20 mg intravenous metoclopramide, and placebo. There was a trend toward greater reductions in VAS ratings and a lesser requirement for rescue medication in the antiemetic drug groups, but differences from the placebo group did not reach significance. The majority of patients in all groups were satisfied with treatment.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a really bad tummy ache with lots of throwing up.  Doctors wanted to see if two medicines, ondansetron and metoclopramide, helped people feel better than just getting a sugar pill (placebo).

They gave these medicines to grown-ups in a hospital emergency room who had bad nausea and vomiting.  They measured how much better people felt using a scale (like a ruler to measure pain).

They found that all three – the two medicines and the sugar pill – helped about the same amount!  The medicines *might* have helped a tiny bit more, but not enough to say for sure.  Most people felt okay with whatever they got.  There were also very few side effects.

So, the study showed that for this type of tummy trouble, those medicines aren't much better than a sugar pill.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine a science experiment to see what helps with throwing up.  Doctors wanted to know if two medicines, ondansetron and metoclopramide, were better than nothing (a placebo, which is like a sugar pill) at helping people who felt sick to their stomach in a hospital emergency room.

They gave some people ondansetron, some metoclopramide, and some the placebo.  They then asked everyone how sick they felt before and after taking the medicine.

They found that the medicine ondansetron didn't actually help people feel any better than the placebo did.  Both ondansetron and metoclopramide helped a little, but not significantly more than the placebo.  Almost everyone felt a little better after 30 minutes, regardless of what they took.  And almost nobody had any bad side effects.

So, the experiment showed that for this type of sickness, ondansetron wasn't a very helpful medicine.

Title: Comparison of local infiltration of analgesia and sciatic nerve block in addition to femoral nerve block for total knee arthroplasty.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage
abstract: We conducted a prospective randomized controlled trial to test the null hypothesis that there is no difference between sciatic nerve block (SNB) and local infiltration of analgesia (LIA) regarding postoperative analgesia after total knee arthroplasty (TKA), when administrated in addition to femoral nerve block (FNB). Forty-six patients scheduled for TKA were randomized into two groups: concomitant administration of FNB and SNB or FNB and LIA. Average pain scores during the first 21days after surgery were similar in the two groups and remained at low level. There was no significant difference in the need for adjuvant analgesics, patient satisfaction level, the time to achieve rehabilitation goals, and length of hospital stay. The LIA offers a potentially safer alternative to SNB as an adjunct to FNB.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine doctors are testing two different ways to help people feel less pain after knee replacement surgery.  They're comparing two types of pain medicine injections: one called SNB and the other called LIA, both used *in addition* to another injection called FNB.

They had 46 people get knee replacements and randomly split them into two groups.  One group got FNB and SNB, the other got FNB and LIA.

They checked how much pain each group had for 21 days.  They also looked at things like if they needed extra pain medicine, how happy they were with their pain relief, how quickly they got better, and how long they stayed in the hospital.

Guess what?  Both groups felt about the same amount of pain, and there wasn't much difference in any of the other things they checked.

The doctors think LIA might be a better choice because it's possibly safer than SNB, even though both worked pretty well.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're building a really awesome LEGO castle, but building it hurts your knee!  Doctors wanted to see which way of numbing your knee after surgery would work best to stop the pain.

They tried two ways:

1. **Numbing the whole leg (like a big, long LEGO):** This is like a sciatic nerve block (SNB).
2. **Numbing just the knee (like a small LEGO):** This is like local infiltration of analgesia (LIA).

Both ways also used another numbing shot in a different part of the leg (FNB).

They tested this on people getting knee replacement surgery (TKA).  They found that both ways worked about the same!  The pain was similar, they needed the same extra pain medicine, and they felt equally happy with the results.  It took them the same amount of time to get better and go home.

So, the doctors learned that numbing the whole leg isn't any better than just numbing the knee, when you already have another numbing shot.

Title: The effect of a single dose of preemptive pregabalin administered with COX-2 inhibitor: a trial in total knee arthroplasty.
abstract: We sought to compare a group (Group L) (n=21) of patients that underwent total knee arthroplasty and received a single preoperative dose of pregabalin combined with a COX-2 inhibitor with a control group (Group C) (n=20) that only received a COX-2 inhibitor. The primary outcome was acute postoperative pain intensity. Analgesic consumption and functional recovery were also evaluated. Mean cumulative fentanyl consumption during the first 48 hours was lower in Group L than in Group C (P<0.05). The pain scores at rest were lower in Group L at 6 and 12 hours after surgery (P<0.05). No significant intergroup difference was noted in functional recovery. The treatment with pregabalin was not more effective than the COX-2 inhibitor alone in patients with total knee arthroplasty regarding functional recovery.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine two groups of people getting knee surgery.

* **Group L:** Got a special medicine (pregabalin) *plus* another medicine (COX-2 inhibitor) before surgery.
* **Group C:** Only got the COX-2 inhibitor before surgery.

Doctors wanted to see which group had less pain after surgery.  They measured:

* **Pain:** How much pain each person felt.
* **Medicine for pain:** How much pain medicine (fentanyl) each person needed.
* **How well they could move:** How quickly they could walk and move around again.

They found that Group L (the group that got the extra medicine) had:

* Less pain after surgery.
* Needed less pain medicine in the first two days.

But, surprisingly, both groups got better at moving around at about the same speed.  The extra medicine didn't help them move better.

So, the extra medicine helped with pain, but not with getting better at moving.

Title: The effect of a single dose of preemptive pregabalin administered with COX-2 inhibitor: a trial in total knee arthroplasty.
abstract: We sought to compare a group (Group L) (n=21) of patients that underwent total knee arthroplasty and received a single preoperative dose of pregabalin combined with a COX-2 inhibitor with a control group (Group C) (n=20) that only received a COX-2 inhibitor in terms of (1) acute postoperative pain intensity, (2) analgesic consumption, and (3) functional recovery. Mean cumulative fentanyl consumption during the first 48 hours was lower in Group L than in Group C (P<0.05). The pain scores at rest were lower in Group L at 6 and 12 hours after surgery (P<0.05). No significant intergroup difference was noted in functional recovery. The addition of pregabalin led to an additive reduction in early postoperative pain and analgesic consumption.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine two groups of people who had surgery on their knees.

* **Group L:**  These people got a special medicine (pregabalin) *plus* another medicine (COX-2 inhibitor) before their surgery.

* **Group C:** These people only got the COX-2 inhibitor medicine before surgery.

The doctors wanted to see which group had less pain and needed less pain medicine after surgery.

They found that:

* **Less Pain Medicine:** Group L (the group that got both medicines) needed less pain medicine in the first two days after surgery.
* **Less Pain:** Group L also had less pain at 6 and 12 hours after surgery.
* **Same Recovery:** Both groups recovered about the same speed.

So, adding the pregabalin medicine seemed to help reduce pain and the need for pain medicine right after the knee surgery.

Title: The effect of knee extensor open kinetic chain resistance training in the ACL-injured knee.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
PURPOSE: To investigate the effect of different loads of knee extensor open kinetic chain resistance training on anterior knee laxity and function in the ACL-injured (ACLI) knee.

METHODS: Fifty-eight ACLI subjects were randomised to one of three (12-week duration) training groups. The STAND group trained according to a standardised rehabilitation protocol. Subjects in the LOW and HIGH group trained as did the STAND group but with the addition of seated knee extensor open kinetic chain resistance training at loads of 2 sets of 20 repetition maximum (RM) and 20 sets of 2RM, respectively. Anterior knee laxity and measurements of physical and subjective function were performed at baseline, 6 and 12 weeks. Thirty-six subjects were tested at both baseline and 12 weeks (STAND n = 13, LOW n = 11, HIGH n = 12).

RESULTS: The LOW group demonstrated a reduction in 133 N anterior knee laxity between baseline and 12 weeks testing when compared to the HIGH and the STAND groups (p = 0.009). Specifically, the trained-untrained knee laxity decreased an average of approximately 5 mm in the LOW group while remaining the same in the other two groups.

CONCLUSION: Twelve weeks of knee extensor open kinetic chain resistance training at loads of 2 sets of 20RM led to a reduction in anterior knee laxity in the ACLI knee. This reduction in laxity does not appear to offer any significant short-term functional advantages when compared to a standard rehabilitation protocol. These results indicate that knee laxity can be decreased with resistance training of the thigh muscles.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is like a hinge.  Sometimes, injuries can make that hinge loose and wobbly.  This study looked at whether exercising the leg muscles could help tighten that wobbly knee.

Scientists divided people with wobbly knees (because of a specific knee injury) into three groups:

* **Group 1 (STAND):** Did regular exercises.
* **Group 2 (LOW):** Did regular exercises *plus* some extra exercises to strengthen their leg muscles with lighter weights, doing many repetitions.
* **Group 3 (HIGH):** Did regular exercises *plus* some extra exercises to strengthen their leg muscles with heavier weights, doing fewer repetitions.

After 12 weeks, they checked how wobbly everyone's knees were.  They found that the group with lighter weights and many repetitions (LOW) had less wobbly knees than the other two groups.  However, all groups were about the same in terms of how well their knees worked in everyday activities.

So, the study showed that a specific type of leg exercise with lighter weights can help make a wobbly knee less wobbly, even if it doesn't necessarily make the knee *function* better right away.

Scientists wanted to see if doing special exercises could make the loose knee hinge tighter. They had three groups of people with loose knees:

* **Group 1 (STAND):** Did regular exercises.
* **Group 2 (LOW):** Did regular exercises PLUS some extra exercises to strengthen their leg muscles (but not too hard).

Title: Electrothermal arthroscopic capsulorrhaphy: old technology, new evidence. A multicenter randomized clinical trial.
Journal: Journal of Shoulder and Elbow Surgery
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim equivalence/non-inferiority versus control for a (-) endpoint, Claim benefit based only on efficacy, ignore safety (or vice versa)
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Radiofrequency technology for shoulder instability was rapidly adopted despite limited clinical evidence and a poor understanding of its indications. Reports of serious adverse events followed, leading to its abandonment. This paper presents findings from a multicenter randomized clinical trial evaluating the safety and efficacy of electrothermal arthroscopic capsulorrhaphy (ETAC) compared with open inferior capsular shift (ICS) and reviews the role of randomized trials in adopting new technology.

METHODS: Patients (>14В years) diagnosed with multidirectional instability or multidirectional laxity with anteroinferior instability and failed nonoperative treatment were enrolled. Patients with bone lesions or labral, biceps anchor, or full-thickness rotator cuff tears were excluded intraoperatively. Outcomes included Western Ontario Shoulder Instability Index, function and recurrent instability at 2 years postoperatively, and surgical times.

RESULTS: Fifty-four subjects (mean age, 23В years; 37 women) were randomized to ETAC (nВ =В 28) or open ICS (nВ =В 26). The groups were comparable at baseline, except for external rotation at the side. At 2 years postoperatively, there were no statistically or clinically significant differences between groups for the Western Ontario Shoulder Instability Index (PВ =В .71), American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons score (PВ =В .43), Constant score (PВ =В .43), and active range of motion. Recurrent instability was not statistically different (ETAC,В 2; open,В 4; PВ =В .41). ETAC (23В minutes) was significantly shorter than open ICS (59В minutes) (PВ <В .01) surgery. Three subjects (1 ETAC, 2 open) had stiff shoulders.CONCLUSIONS: At 2 years postoperatively, quality of life and functional outcomes between groups were not clinically different. ETAC had fewer complications and episodes of recurrence compared with open surgery. This evidence reinforces the need to critically evaluate new technology before widespread clinical use.

This study was to test a *different* way to fix wobbly shoulders:  two types of surgery. One is a new, less invasive surgery using heat (ETAC), and the other is an older, more invasive surgery (open ICS).  They wanted to see which one was better and safer.

They studied a bunch of people with wobbly shoulders. After two years, both surgeries worked about the same!  People in both groups felt about the same, and their shoulders were about as stable.  The new surgery (ETAC) was much faster, and had fewer problems.

The important lesson is:  Just because something is new and sounds cool doesn't mean it's the best.  Doctors need to carefully test new treatments before everyone starts using them.

Title: Less deep vein thrombosis due to transcutaneous fibular nerve stimulation in total knee arthroplasty: a randomized controlled trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
PURPOSE: The purpose of this study is to evaluate the clinical efficacy and safety of transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) in patients during total knee arthroplasty (TKA).

METHODS: Ninety patients undergoing primary TKA were randomly allocated to the TENS or control group. In the TENS group, electrical stimulation of the common fibular nerve, which produced a brisk dorsiflexion of the ankle, was performed for the operated leg during surgery. In the control group, no electrical stimulation was applied. Serum D-dimer and soluble fibrin monomer complex (SFMC) levels were measured before surgery, immediately after surgery, and on post-operative day (POD) 1. Ultrasonography was performed on POD 1.

RESULTS: Immediately after surgery, D-dimer and SFMC levels were lower in the TENS group compared with the control group. The incidence of DVT was 11% (five cases) in the TENS group and 31% (14 cases) in the control group (p = 0.02). There were no adverse effects related to TENS.CONCLUSIONS: The treatment with TENS was not more effective than the comparator in patients undergoing total knee arthroplasty.

Imagine your knee is broken and needs a new one, like a robot part!  Doctors did a study to see if using a little electric zap (called TENS) on the leg during the surgery would help.  They had two groups of people getting new knees: one group got the zap, the other didn't.

They checked blood tests to see how much clotting (like when you get a cut and it scabs over) was happening.  The zap group had less clotting right after surgery.  They also had fewer blood clots in their legs afterwards (only 11% got them, compared to 31% in the group without the zap).

But even though the zap seemed to help with clotting, the doctors said overall, the zap didn't make a big difference in how well the new knee surgery went.  And importantly, the zap didn't hurt anyone.

Title: Less deep vein thrombosis due to transcutaneous fibular nerve stimulation in total knee arthroplasty: a randomized controlled trial.

abstract:
PURPOSE: It has been known for years that deep vein thrombi (DVT) start to develop during total joint arthroplasty. Previously, we reported effective prevention of venous stasis by transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS). It is hypothesized that TENS might be a thromboprophylactic tool for the limb undergoing surgery. The purpose of this study is to clarify the clinical efficacy and safety of TENS in patients during total knee arthroplasty (TKA).

METHODS: Ninety patients undergoing primary TKA were involved and randomly allocated to the TENS or control group. In the TENS group, electrical stimulation of the common fibular nerve, which produced a brisk dorsiflexion of the ankle, was performed for the operated leg during surgery. In the control group, no electrical stimulation was applied. Serum D-dimer and soluble fibrin monomer complex (SFMC) levels were measured before surgery, immediately after surgery, and post-operative day (POD) 1. Ultrasonography was performed on POD 1.

RESULTS: Immediately after surgery, D-dimer and SFMC levels of each group were significantly lower in the TENS group compared with control (p < 0.05). The incidence of DVT was 11 % (five cases) in the TENS group while 31 % (14 cases) in control (p = 0.02). There were no adverse effects related to TENS.CONCLUSIONS: TENS during TKA showed significant effects on preventing DVT. Sustaining muscle pump activation during surgery prevented not only venous stasis, but also hypercoagulability of blood. Intraoperative TENS is a safe and novel strategy against early post-operative thromboembolism, which is difficult to be completed through existing prophylaxis after total joint arthroplasty.

Imagine your leg needs a new knee joint.  Doctors sometimes use a special machine to help prevent blood clots from forming during the surgery.  This machine uses gentle electrical zaps to make your leg muscles move a little, like they're pumping blood.

This study tested that machine on 90 people getting new knee joints.  Half the people got the zaps (the TENS group), and half didn't (the control group).  They checked their blood for signs of blood clots before, right after, and the day after surgery.

The results showed that the people who got the zaps had *way* fewer blood clots than the people who didn't.  The zaps seemed to help keep the blood flowing and prevent clots from forming.  And best of all, the zaps didn't hurt or cause any problems!

So, the study showed that this machine is a safe and helpful way to prevent blood clots during knee surgery.

Title: Fluoroscopy and imageless navigation enable an equivalent reconstruction of leg length and global and femoral offset in THA.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Restoration of biomechanics is a major goal in THA. The effect of imageless navigation compared with intraoperative fluoroscopy is unclear.

METHODS: In this prospective study, a consecutive series of 125 patients were randomized to either navigation-guided or fluoroscopy-controlled THA using sealed, opaque envelopes. All patients received the same cementless prosthetic components through an anterolateral MIS approach while they were in a lateral decubitus position. Leg length, global or total offset (representing the combination of femoral and acetabular offset), and femoral offset differences were restored using either navigation or fluoroscopy. Postoperatively, residual leg length and global and femoral offset discrepancies were analyzed on magnification-corrected radiographs of the pelvis by an independent and blinded examiner using digital planning software. Accuracy was defined as the relative postoperative difference between the surgically treated and the unaffected contralateral side for leg length and offset, respectively; precision was defined as the absolute postoperative deviation of leg length and global and femoral offset regardless of lengthening or shortening of leg length and offset throughout the THA. All analyses were performed per intention-to-treat.

RESULTS: Analyzing the relative accuracy of leg length restoration, we found a mean difference of 0.2 mm (95% CI, -1.0 to +1.4 mm; p = 0.729) between fluoroscopy and navigation, 0.2 mm (95% CI, -0.9 to +1.3 mm; p = 0.740) for global offset, and 1.7 mm (95% CI, +0.4 to +2.9 mm; p = 0.008) for femoral offset. For the absolute precision of leg length and global and femoral offset equalization, there was a mean difference of 1.7 ± 0.3 mm (p < 0.001) between fluoroscopy and navigation. The biomechanical reconstruction with a residual leg length and global and femoral offset discrepancy less than 5 mm and less than 8 mm, respectively, succeeded in 93% and 98%, respectively, in the navigation group and in 54% and 95%, respectively, in the fluoroscopy group.CONCLUSIONS: The treatment with imageless navigation was not more effective than intraoperative fluoroscopy in patients undergoing MIS THA with respect to the accuracy and precision of reconstructing leg length and global and femoral offset.

Imagine your leg is like a building made of blocks.  Sometimes, if someone hurts their hip really badly, they need a new hip joint (like replacing a broken block).  Doctors call this a THA (Total Hip Arthroplasty).

This study was about two ways doctors can make sure the new hip joint is put in perfectly:

* **Using a special computer system (navigation):**  Like using a blueprint to build with LEGOs.
* **Using X-ray pictures (fluoroscopy):** Like taking pictures while you build to make sure everything is straight.

The doctors wanted to see which way was better at making sure the leg was the same length and the hip joint was in the right place.  They studied 125 people who got new hip joints.

They found that:

* For most things, both ways were pretty much the same.  The computer and the X-rays did a similar job of making the leg the right length and the hip joint in the right spot.
* Only one small part of the hip joint placement was better with the computer.

So, the study showed that using a computer wasn't necessarily *better* than using X-rays for this type of surgery.  Both methods worked pretty well.

METHODS: In this prospective study a consecutive series of 125 patients were randomized to either navigation-guided or fluoroscopy-controlled THA using sealed, opaque envelopes. All patients received the same cementless prosthetic components through an anterolateral MIS approach while they were in a lateral decubitus position. Postoperatively, residual leg length and global and femoral offset discrepancies were analyzed on magnification-corrected radiographs of the pelvis by an independent and blinded examiner using digital planning software. Accuracy was defined as the relative postoperative difference between the surgically treated and the unaffected contralateral side for leg length and offset, respectively; precision was defined as the absolute postoperative deviation of leg length and global and femoral offset regardless of lengthening or shortening of leg length and offset throughout the THA. All analyses were performed per intention-to-treat.

RESULTS: Analyzing the relative accuracy of leg length restoration we found a mean difference of 0.2 mm (95% CI, -1.0 to +1.4 mm; p = 0.729) between fluoroscopy and navigation, 0.2 mm (95 % CI, -0.9 to +1.3 mm; p = 0.740) for global offset and 1.7 mm (95 % CI, +0.4 to +2.9 mm; p = 0.008) for femoral offset. For the absolute precision of leg length and global and femoral offset equalization, there was a mean difference of 1.7 В± 0.3 mm (p < 0.001) between fluoroscopy and navigation. plain_language_summary:
Imagine you're building a really tiny, perfect LEGO tower.  Doctors sometimes have to do something similar with people's hips – they replace a hip joint (THA means Total Hip Arthroplasty).  They want to make sure the new hip is exactly the right length and in the perfect position.

This study compared two ways doctors can do this:

* **Using a special computer system (navigation):** Like having a super-precise LEGO instruction manual.
* **Using X-ray pictures (fluoroscopy):** Like checking your LEGO tower with a regular ruler.

The doctors wanted to see which method was better at making the new hip the perfect size and position.  They did this by measuring how close the new hip was to the old, good hip.

They found that both methods were pretty good!  The computer system was slightly better at getting the *femoral offset* (one part of the hip's position) exactly right, but overall, both were very similar in how accurately they placed the new hip.  The computer system was also better at keeping the new hip within a very small margin of error.

So, the main takeaway is that both ways of doing the hip replacement are pretty accurate, and the computer system might be a tiny bit better, but not by much.

Title: Weighted versus uniform dose of tranexamic acid in patients undergoing primary, elective knee arthroplasty: a prospective randomized controlled trial.
abstract: Abstract: In a prospective, randomized, double-blinded, controlled study (25 controls), TA was infused parenterally before tourniquet release in two study groups. Group 1 (n = 20) received a 1 g dose, and group 2 (n = 20) received a 20 mg/kg dose. There was no significant difference between groups 1 and 2 with intra-operative, post-operative, and total blood loss. Two blood transfusions were given to one patient in the weighted group, compared to 19 transfusions (10 patients) in the control group. The treatment with a 1-g dose was not more effective than a 20 mg/kg dose in patients with intra-operative, post-operative, and total blood loss.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine scientists were testing a new medicine (let's call it "TA") to see if it helps reduce blood loss during surgery.  They did a study with three groups of people having surgery:

* **Group 1:** Got a certain amount (1 gram) of the medicine before the surgery.
* **Group 2:** Got a different amount of the medicine (based on their weight) before the surgery.
* **Control Group:** Didn't get the medicine.

They carefully measured how much blood each person lost during and after surgery.  

The important finding is that the medicine didn't seem to make much difference.  Both groups that got the medicine lost about the same amount of blood, and it wasn't less than the group that didn't get the medicine.  In fact, the group that *didn't* get the medicine needed many more blood transfusions!

So, the study showed that this medicine (TA) at either dose tested, didn't help reduce blood loss during surgery.

Title: Weighted versus uniform dose of tranexamic acid in patients undergoing primary, elective knee arthroplasty: a prospective randomized controlled trial.
abstract: In a prospective, randomized, double-blinded, controlled study (25 controls), TA was infused parenterally before tourniquet release in two study groups. Group 1 (n = 20) received a 1 g dose, and group 2 (n = 20) received a 20 mg/kg dose. There was no significant difference between groups 1 and 2 with intra-operative, post-operative and total blood loss. Both groups 1 and 2 exhibited significant improvements in intra-operative, post-operative, and total blood loss compared to the control group (P < 0.05). Two blood transfusions were given to one patient in the weighted group, compared to 19 transfusions (10 patients) in the control group. * **Group 2:** Got a different amount of medicine (based on their weight) before surgery.

So, the scientists concluded that the 1-gram dose of the medicine is just as good as the other dose, and both are really helpful in reducing blood loss during surgery.  They only needed a small amount of the medicine to get a big effect.

Title: Randomized controlled trial of intravenous antivenom versus placebo for latrodectism: the second Redback Antivenom Evaluation (RAVE-II) study.
Spin in Abstract Title/Results: RESULTS: Selectively focus on (+) within-group comparison for primary endpoint
abstract:
STUDY OBJECTIVE: Latrodectism is the most important spider envenomation syndrome worldwide. There remains considerable controversy over antivenom treatment. We aimed to investigate whether antivenom resulted in resolution of pain and systemic effects in patients with latrodectism who received standardized analgesia.

METHODS: In a multicenter randomized placebo-controlled trial of redback spider antivenom for latrodectism, 224 patients (>7 years) with a redback spider bite and severe pain, with or without systemic effects, were randomized to receive normal saline solution (placebo) or antivenom after receiving standardized analgesia. The primary outcome was a clinically significant reduction in pain 2 hours after trial medication compared with baseline. A second primary outcome for the subgroup with systemic features of envenomation was resolution of systemic features at 2 hours. Secondary outcomes were improved pain at 4 and 24 hours, resolution of systemic features at 4 hours, administration of opioid analgesics or unblinded antivenom after 2 hours, and adverse reactions.

RESULTS: Two hours after treatment, 26 of 112 patients (23%) from the placebo arm had a clinically significant improvement in pain versus 38 of 112 (34%) from the antivenom arm (difference in favor of antivenom 10.7%; 95% confidence interval -1.1% to 22.6%; P=.10). Systemic effects resolved after 2 hours in 9 of 41 patients (22%) in the placebo arm and 9 of 35 (26%) in the antivenom arm (difference 3.8%; 95% confidence interval -15% to 23%; P=.79). There was no significant difference in any secondary outcome between antivenom and placebo. Acute systemic hypersensitivity reactions occurred in 4 of 112 patients (3.6%) receiving antivenom.

CONCLUSION: The addition of antivenom to standardized analgesia in patients with latrodectism did not significantly improve pain or systemic effects.

They did a study where some people with bad spider bites got the antivenom, and others got a fake medicine (like a saltwater solution).  Everyone also got regular pain medicine.

They checked how much better the pain was after two hours.  It turned out that the antivenom didn't really make a big difference in how much the pain went away compared to the fake medicine.  The antivenom also didn't help much with other bad effects from the bite.

So, the study showed that for these spider bites, the special antivenom medicine wasn't much better than just using regular pain medicine.

Title: Nebulized fentanyl vs intravenous morphine for ED patients with acute limb pain: a randomized clinical trial.
Journal: The American Journal of Emergency Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim equivalence/non-inferiority versus control for a (-) endpoint
abstract:
Objective  
This study was designed to compare the effectiveness of nebulized fentanyl with intravenous (IV) morphine on the management of acute limb pain.

Methods  
This was a placebo-controlled, double-blind randomized clinical trial. Ninety emergency department patients with moderate to severe pain aged 15 to 50 years were block randomized and enrolled in this study. Forty-seven patients in the experimental group received nebulized fentanyl (4 μg/kg) and IV normal saline as placebo, and the remaining 43 patients in the control group received IV morphine (0.1 mg/kg) and nebulized normal saline as placebo. All participants' pain scores were assessed by Numerical Rating Scale before and after intervention at 5-, 10-, 15-, 30-, 45-, and 60-minute intervals. Patients' vital signs and possible adverse effects were recorded. 

Results  
The mean initial pain score in the experimental group was 8.7 and 8.4 in the control group (P = .1). Pain relief in both groups after 5 and 10 minutes was similar (P = .72). Pain management in both groups was successful, with more than a 3-point reduction in the Numerical Rating Scale. Patient satisfaction in both groups was similar. No adverse effects were reported in the experimental group.

Conclusion  
The treatment with nebulized fentanyl was not more effective than IV morphine in patients with acute limb pain in terms of pain reduction.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine doctors wanted to see which medicine is better at relieving really bad leg pain: a breathing treatment (like a special inhaler) with a medicine called fentanyl, or a shot of morphine.

They did a test with 90 people with bad leg pain.  They randomly split them into two groups.  One group got the fentanyl breathing treatment, and the other got the morphine shot.  Neither the patients nor the doctors knew who got which medicine (that's the "double-blind" part).

They checked everyone's pain levels before and after the medicine.  They found that both medicines worked about the same – they both helped reduce the pain a lot.  Nobody in the breathing treatment group got sick from the medicine.

So, the doctors concluded that the breathing treatment and the shot were equally good at getting rid of the leg pain.  Neither one was better than the other.

Title: Compression stockings in ankle sprain: a multicenter randomized study.
Journal: The American Journal of Emergency Medicine

abstract:
OBJECTIVES: Ankle sprain is a frequently encountered traumatic injury in emergency departments and is associated with important health expenses. We tested the hypothesis that compression stockings speed up recovery from ankle sprain.

METHODS: Recent (<48 hours) cases of ankle sprain without other traumatic injury in patients aged between 18 and 55 years were included. Patients were randomly allocated to placebo Jersey or class II compression stockings (Venoflex; Thuasne, Levallois-Perret, France). The primary end point was the time to recovery of normal painless walking without requirement for analgesic drug.

RESULTS: We randomized 126 patients and analyzed 117 patients (60 in the placebo group and 57 in the compression group). The median time to normal painless walking was not significantly decreased (P = .16). No significant differences were observed in pain, analgesic consumption, and bimalleolar and midfoot circumferences. No safety issue was reported.CONCLUSIONS: The treatment with compression stockings was not more effective than placebo in patients with ankle sprain.

They took 126 people who had recently twisted their ankles.  They split them into two groups: one group wore the special socks, and the other group wore regular socks (a placebo – like a pretend treatment).

They timed how long it took each person to walk normally again without pain and needing medicine.

Guess what?  The special socks didn't make a difference!  It took about the same amount of time for both groups to get better.  So, those special socks don't seem to help heal twisted ankles faster.
