Title: Double-Row Repair Lowers the Retear Risk After Accelerated Rehabilitation.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Recently, an accelerated rehabilitation protocol after rotator cuff (RC) repair has been proposed for patients at risk of postoperative stiffness.

PURPOSE: To investigate whether double-row (DR) repair provides better clinical outcomes and a lower retear rate compared with single-row (SR) configurations in patients undergoing early accelerated mobilization.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 1.

METHODS: A total of 58 patients were randomized to undergo either SR or DR repair. After 2 years of follow-up, 12 men and 13 women (mean age, 61.8 years; range, 52-67 years) in the SR group and 15 men and 10 women (mean age, 58.9 years; range, 51-69 years) in the DR group were evaluated. The primary outcome was the retear rate, assessed by magnetic resonance imaging of the shoulder at 2-year follow-up. The clinical evaluation was based on the modified University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) shoulder score and range of motion (ROM) measurements.

RESULTS: Magnetic resonance arthrography showed a lower full-thickness retear rate for the DR group than for the SR group (8% vs 24%, respectively; P < .05). At both 6-month and 2-year follow-up, there was no statistically significant difference in terms of the rate of stiffness in the SR and DR groups (8% vs 12% and 0% vs 0%, respectively; P > .05). No clinical differences were recorded regarding the UCLA score (SR group: mean, 32.6 [range, 30-35]; DR group: mean, 33.3 [range, 29-35]; P = .61) and ROM.

CONCLUSION: The treatment with double-row repair was not more effective than single-row repair in patients with rotator cuff tears in terms of clinical outcomes.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is like a ball and socket, and the muscles around it are like strong ropes holding the ball in place.  Sometimes those ropes (called rotator cuffs) tear.  Doctors can fix them with surgery, either using one row of stitches (single-row) or two rows (double-row).

This study looked at whether using two rows of stitches was better than one.  They wanted to see if it healed better and prevented the rope from tearing again.  They followed people for two years after surgery.

They found that the two-row stitch method did a slightly better job of preventing the rope from tearing again.  But, surprisingly, both methods worked about the same in terms of how well the shoulder moved and how much pain people had.  So, while two rows might be a little better at preventing re-tears, it doesn't necessarily mean it's a much better overall fix.

Title: Double-Row Repair Lowers the Retear Risk After Accelerated Rehabilitation.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Recently, an accelerated rehabilitation protocol after rotator cuff (RC) repair has been proposed for patients at risk of postoperative stiffness.

PURPOSE: To investigate, in patients undergoing early accelerated mobilization, whether double-row (DR) repair provides better clinical outcomes and a lower retear rate compared with single-row (SR) configurations.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 1.

METHODS: A total of 58 patients were randomized to undergo either SR or DR repair. After 2 years of follow-up, 12 men and 13 women (mean age, 61.8 years; range, 52-67 years) in the SR group and 15 men and 10 women (mean age, 58.9 years; range, 51-69 years) in the DR group were evaluated. To assess the retear rate, magnetic resonance imaging of the shoulder was performed at 2-year follow-up. The clinical evaluation was based on the modified University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) shoulder score and range of motion (ROM) measurements.

RESULTS: Magnetic resonance arthrography showed a significantly lower full-thickness retear rate for the DR group than for the SR group (8% vs 24%, respectively; P < .05). Conversely, at both 6-month and 2-year follow-up, there was no statistically significant difference in terms of the rate of stiffness in the SR and DR groups (8% vs 12% and 0% vs 0%, respectively; P > .05). No clinical differences were recorded regarding the UCLA score (SR group: mean, 32.6 [range, 30-35]; DR group: mean, 33.3 [range, 29-35]; P = .61) and ROM.

CONCLUSION: In selected patients at a high risk of shoulder stiffness and therefore necessitating accelerated postoperative rehabilitation, DR repair of the RC could lower retear rates.

Imagine your shoulder is like a ball and socket, and the muscles and tendons that hold it together are like strong ropes.  Sometimes those "ropes" (called rotator cuffs) get torn.  Doctors can fix them with surgery, using either single-row stitches (like one rope) or double-row stitches (like two ropes).

This study looked at whether using two rows of stitches is better.  They had two groups of adults with torn shoulder "ropes." One group got single-row stitches, the other got double-row stitches.  After two years, they checked to see if the ropes had re-torn and how well the shoulders moved.

They found that the double-row stitches were better at preventing the "ropes" from tearing again.  But surprisingly, both groups had about the same amount of stiffness afterwards.  So, if someone is at risk of a stiff shoulder after surgery, double-row stitches might be a better choice to keep the shoulder from re-tearing.

Title: Dietary fat restriction increases fat taste sensitivity in people with obesity.

abstract:
Objective: This study assessed the effect of a 6-week low-fat (LF) or portion control (PC) diet matched for weight loss on fat taste thresholds in people with overweight/obesity.

Methods: Participants (n = 53) completed a randomized dietary intervention and consumed either a LF diet (25% fat) or PC diet (33% fat) for 6 weeks. Fat taste thresholds (lowest detectable fat concentration) were assessed at baseline and week 6.

Results: Consumption of a LF diet (n = 26) and PC diet (n = 27) reduced participants' weight, with no significant differences between groups (LF, -2.9%, PC, -2.7%). Both diets resulted in a decrease in fat taste thresholds. The ability to perceive different fat concentrations in foods was increased after the LF diet only; however, food preference did not change on either diet.

Conclusions: The treatment with a low-fat diet was not more effective than a portion control diet in patients with overweight/obesity in increasing fat taste sensitivity.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine a science experiment to see if changing what people eat helps them taste fat better.  

They had two groups of grown-ups who were a little overweight.  One group ate a low-fat diet (not much fat in their food), and the other group ate a normal amount of fat but controlled how much they ate (portion control).  Both groups ate this way for six weeks.

At the beginning and end of the six weeks, they tested how well each person could taste fat in food.

Both groups lost a similar amount of weight.  Both groups got a little better at tasting fat, but only the group that ate the low-fat diet got *significantly* better at it.  Neither group changed what foods they liked or disliked.

So, the experiment showed that while both diets helped people lose weight and taste fat a little better, the low-fat diet was slightly better at making people more sensitive to the taste of fat.  But it wasn't a huge difference.

Title: Dietary fat restriction increases fat taste sensitivity in people with obesity.

abstract:
Objective: Individuals with obesity may be less sensitive to the taste of fat, and it is hypothesized that this is due to excess dietary fat intake. This study assessed the effect of a 6-week low-fat (LF) or portion control (PC) diet matched for weight loss on fat taste thresholds, fat perception, and preference in people with overweight/obesity.

Methods: Participants (n = 53) completed a randomized dietary intervention and consumed either a LF diet (25% fat) or PC diet (33% fat) for 6 weeks. Fat taste thresholds (lowest detectable fat concentration), fat perception (discrimination ability), preference, and anthropometry were assessed at baseline and week 6.

Results: Consumption of a LF diet (n = 26) and PC diet (n = 27) reduced participants' weight (P < 0.001), with no significant differences between groups (LF, -2.9%, PC, -2.7%). Both diets resulted in a decrease in fat taste thresholds (P = 0.014), and the effect tended to be stronger in the LF diet vs. the PC diet (P = 0.060). The ability to perceive different fat concentrations in foods was increased after the LF diet only (P = 0.017); however, food preference did not change on either diet.

Conclusions: A PC and LF diet both increase fat taste sensitivity in people with overweight/obesity, with the strongest effect after the LF diet.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your taste buds are like little sensors for different flavors, especially the taste of fatty foods.  Some people who are overweight or obese might not notice the taste of fat as much as others.  Scientists think this is because they eat a lot of fatty foods.

This study tested two different diets on people who were overweight or obese:

* **Low-Fat Diet:**  This diet had less fat (only 25% of the calories came from fat).
* **Portion Control Diet:** This diet had a bit more fat (33% of calories from fat), but people carefully controlled how much they ate.

Both groups lost a similar amount of weight after 6 weeks.  The interesting part is that after following *both* diets, people became better at tasting fat!  They could tell the difference between foods with different amounts of fat better than before.  This improvement was even bigger for the people on the low-fat diet.  However, neither diet changed how much people *liked* fatty foods.

In short, eating less fat helped people's taste buds become more sensitive to fat, even if they still enjoyed fatty foods.

Title: A randomized controlled clinical and radiological trial about outcomes of navigation-assisted TKA compared to conventional TKA: long-term follow-up.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
PURPOSE: This prospective randomized study compared navigation-assisted total knee arthroplasty (TKA) and conventional TKA, reporting clinical and radiological outcomes at a follow-up of 9 years. The purpose of this study was to compare the clinical and radiological outcomes for patients who underwent navigation-assisted TKA or conventional TKA after long-term follow-up.

METHODS: A total of 80 patients (88 knees) were available for physical and radiological examination 9 years after TKA. Clinical outcomes were evaluated using HSS, WOMAC, and KS function and pain scores. Radiological outcomes of component loosening and its survivorship during the 9-year follow-up were also evaluated.

RESULTS: There were no significant differences in clinical outcomes between the two groups. In terms of radiological outcomes, the navigation group had fewer alignment outliers (7.3% vs 20%, p = 0.006). The survival rate was slightly better in the navigation group than in the conventional group without statistical significance (best-case scenario 100% vs 95.3%, n.s., worst-case scenario 95.6% vs 88.4%, n.s.).

CONCLUSION: The treatment with navigation-assisted TKA was not more effective than conventional TKA in patients with total knee arthroplasty regarding clinical outcomes.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is broken and needs a new part, like a robot part!  Doctors did a study to see if using a special computer-guided tool (navigation-assisted TKA) to put in the new knee part was better than the usual way (conventional TKA).

They followed 80 people for 9 years after their knee surgery. They checked how well their knees worked (clinical outcomes) and looked at X-rays to see if the new parts were staying in place (radiological outcomes).

They found that both ways of doing the surgery worked about the same in terms of how well the knee worked.  The computer-guided way *did* seem to make the new knee part fit a little better in some cases, but not enough to be a big deal.

So, the study showed that the fancy computer tool didn't make a huge difference in the long run.  Both ways of doing the knee surgery worked pretty well.

PURPOSE: Well balanced knees with good alignment are essential for a well-functioning TKA with long survival of its implants. This prospective randomized study comparing navigation-assisted TKA and conventional TKA reported the clinical and radiological outcomes at a follow-up of 9В years. The purpose of this study was to compare the clinical and radiological outcomes for patients who underwent navigation-assisted TKA or conventional TKA after long-term follow-up.

METHODS: A total of 80 patients (88 knees) were available for physical and radiological examination 9В years after TKA. Clinical outcomes were evaluated using HSS, WOMAC, and KS function and pain scores. And radiological outcomes of the component loosening and its survivorship during 9-year follow-up were also evaluated.

RESULTS: There were no significant differences in the field of clinical outcomes between the two groups. In terms of radiological outcomes, the navigation group had fewer alignment outliers (7.3 vs 20В %, pВ =В 0.006). Although the clinical outcomes showed no differences between the two groups, the survival rate was slightly better in the navigation group than in the conventional group without statistical significance (best-case scenario 100 vs 95.3В %, n.s., worst-case scenario 95.6 vs 88.4В %, n.s.).

CONCLUSION: Navigation-assisted TKA produced better alignment outcomes and better survival rates than conventional instruments although some of the differences were not statistically significant.

Imagine your knee is a hinge.  This study looked at two ways to replace a broken knee hinge (called a TKA, which stands for total knee arthroplasty).  One way used a special computer guide (navigation-assisted), and the other was the usual way (conventional).

They followed 80 people for 9 years to see which way worked better.  They checked how well the new knee worked and how well it stayed in place using X-rays.

The study found that the computer-guided way made the new knee straighter and last a little longer, but the difference wasn't huge.  Both ways worked pretty well overall.  So, while the computer-guided way might be slightly better, it's not a super big deal.

Title: Is There a Benefit for Liposomal Bupivacaine Compared to a Traditional Periarticular Injection in Total Knee Arthroplasty Patients With a History of Chronic Opioid Use?
abstract:
Background: Postoperative pain after total knee arthroplasty (TKA) poses a major challenge. It delays mobilization, increases opioid consumption and side effects, and lengthens hospitalization. This challenge multiplies when treating an opioid-dependent population. We examined whether a novel suspended release local anesthetic, liposomal bupivacaine (LB) would improve pain control and decrease opioid consumption after TKA compared to a standard periarticular injection in opioid-dependent patients.

Methods: Thirty-eight patients undergoing TKA were randomly assigned to receive either a periarticular injection (PAI) with LB (n = 20) or with a standard PAI (including a combination of ropivacaine, clonidine, Toradol, Epinepherine, and saline; n = 18) as part of a multimodal pain management approach. All periarticular injections were done by a single surgeon. Perioperative treatment was similar between groups. Postoperative information regarding pain level was evaluated by a pain visual analog scale score. Postoperative opioid consumption was recorded.

Results: After controlling baseline narcotic usage before surgery, no differences were found between groups in daily postoperative narcotic usage (P = .113), average daily pain score (P = .332), or maximum daily pain score (P = .881). However, when examining pain levels separately for each day, pain visual analog scale scores were reported higher in post operative day 1 in the LB group (P = .033).

Conclusions: LB was not found to be superior to standard PAI in opioid-dependent patients undergoing TKA. This patient population continues to present a challenge even with modern multimodal pain protocols.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine someone gets a new knee joint (that's a total knee arthroplasty, or TKA).  It hurts a lot afterwards!  Doctors try to help with pain medicine, but it's especially hard for people who already take a lot of pain medicine.

Doctors wanted to test a new type of pain medicine (liposomal bupivacaine, or LB) to see if it worked better than the usual medicine. They gave the new medicine to some people getting new knees and the usual medicine to others.

They watched to see how much pain medicine everyone needed and how much pain they had.  They found that the new medicine didn't actually help more than the usual medicine.  Both groups still had pain, and needed about the same amount of pain medicine.  So, the new medicine wasn't better for people who already take a lot of pain medicine.  Helping these people with pain after surgery is still a big problem.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is really, really hurt and needs to be replaced with a new one – that's a "total knee arthroplasty" (TKA).  After surgery, the knee is usually very painful.

Doctors wanted to see if a new type of medicine (liposomal bupivacaine, or LB) could help reduce this pain after knee replacement surgery compared to the usual medicine they use.  They gave the new medicine to some people and the usual medicine to others.

They checked how much pain each group had and how much pain medicine they needed.  Guess what?  The new medicine didn't work any better than the usual medicine at reducing pain or the need for other pain relievers.  So, the new medicine isn't better for this type of surgery.

Title: Conventional En Masse Repair Versus Separate Double-Layer Double-Row Repair for the Treatment of Delaminated Rotator Cuff Tears.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim equivalence/non-inferiority versus control for a (-) endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: The rotator cuff tendon is known to exert a shear force between the superficial and deep layers. Owing to this characteristic, separate repair of delaminated rotator cuff tears has been introduced for the restoration of the physiological biomechanics of the rotator cuff. However, whether conventional en masse repair or separate repair is superior is controversial in terms of outcomes.

PURPOSE: To compare clinical outcomes between conventional en masse repair and separate double-layer double-row repair for the treatment of delaminated rotator cuff tears.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled study; Level of evidence, 2.

METHODS: Between August 2007 and March 2014, a total of 82 patients who underwent arthroscopic rotator cuff repair of a delaminated tear were enrolled and randomized into 2 groups. In group 1 (n = 48), arthroscopic conventional en masse repair was performed. In group 2 (n = 34), separate double-layer double-row repair was performed. The primary outcome was the American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons score. The Constant score, Simple Shoulder Test score, and visual analog scale (VAS) score for pain and range of motion (ROM) were also assessed before surgery; at 3, 6, and 12 months after surgery; and at the last follow-up visit. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed at 12 months postoperatively to examine the retear rate and pattern.

RESULTS: There was no significant difference between groups in the preoperative demographic data, including patient age, sex, symptom duration, tear size, and functional scores (P > .05). The mean follow-up period was 25.9 ± 1.2 months. No significant differences in functional scores and ROM were found between the 2 groups at each time point. Eight (17%) of 48 patients in group 1 and 6 (18%) of 34 patients in group 2 showed retears on MRI at 12-month follow-up (P > .05).

CONCLUSION: The treatment with conventional en masse repair was not more effective than separate double-layer double-row repair in patients with delaminated rotator cuff tears.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is like a bouncy ball with layers.  Sometimes, those layers tear.  Doctors have two ways to fix these tears:

1. **En masse repair:**  They stitch the whole bouncy ball back together at once.
2. **Separate repair:** They carefully stitch each layer of the bouncy ball separately.

Doctors wanted to see which way is better. They studied 82 people with torn shoulders.  Half got the first type of repair, and half got the second.  They checked how well their shoulders worked after the surgery.

The result?  Both ways of fixing the tear worked about the same!  It didn't matter which method the doctors used; the shoulders got better in both groups.

BACKGROUND: The rotator cuff tendon is known to exert a shear force between the superficial and deep layers. plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is like a kite.  The rotator cuff is a strong group of muscles and tendons that help you lift your arm and move it around. Sometimes, these tendons can tear, like a rip in the kite's fabric.  This tear is called a "delaminated rotator cuff tear," meaning it's a tear that separates into layers.

Doctors tried two ways to fix these tears:

1. **En masse repair:**  Like patching the whole kite at once.
2. **Separate double-layer repair:** Like carefully patching each layer of the kite separately.

Doctors wanted to see which way was better. They studied 82 people with these tears.  Half got the first type of repair, and half got the second.  They checked how well their shoulders worked and how much pain they had for a year.

The results showed that *both* ways of fixing the tear helped people feel better and move their arms better.  However, the people who had the separate repair (method 2) had less pain in the first year after surgery.  Both groups had about the same number of tears that came back.

So, both methods work well, but the separate repair might be a little better at reducing pain.

Title: Comparison of Treatments for Superior Labrum-Biceps Complex Lesions With Concomitant Rotator Cuff Repair: A Prospective, Randomized, Comparative Analysis of Debridement, Biceps Tenotomy, and Biceps Tenodesis.
Journal: Arthroscopy: The Journal of Arthroscopic & Related Surgery
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage, Claim benefit based on (+) subgroup analysis
abstract:
PURPOSE: To compare the clinical outcomes in patients with concomitant superior labrum-biceps complex (SLBC) lesions and rotator cuff tears who underwent arthroscopic rotator cuff repair, according to 3 different treatment methods (simple debridement, biceps tenotomy, or biceps tenodesis) for the SLBC lesions.

METHODS: One hundred twenty patients who underwent arthroscopic rotator cuff repair with SLBC lesions (biceps partial tears <50%, partial pulley lesions, and type II SLAP lesions) were enrolled in this prospective comparative study and randomly assigned to 1 of 3 treatment groups (simple debridement [Deb], biceps tenotomy only [BTo], or biceps tenodesis with one suture anchor [BTd]). Patients with isolated subscapularis tears or osteoarthritis were excluded. Finally, 86 patients (Deb in 28, BTo in 27, and BTd in 31) were analyzed (mean follow-up, 22.1 ± 7.72 months; mean age, 58.98 ± 7.8 years). The primary outcome was pain. Functional, clinical, and radiologic outcomes, as well as the strength index of elbow flexion and forearm supination, were also analyzed.

RESULTS: Pain, range of motion, and functional scores improved postoperatively in all 3 groups, with no significant differences across groups. At the 6-month follow-up, 8 patients reported cramping pain (5 Deb patients, 2 BTo patients, and 1 BTd patient). Bicipital groove tenderness improved in the BTo group postoperatively (P = .006). The Popeye deformity was noted in 10 patients (37.0%) in the BTo group, 8 (25.8%) in the BTd group, and 2 (7.1%) in the Deb group (P = .029). On radiologic examination, the presence of tenodesis of the biceps tendons on the bicipital groove showed low agreement with the presence of the Popeye deformity (observed in 13 of 20 patients with the deformity, 65%). On strength index analysis, the BTo group showed lower forearm supination strength (0.877 ± 0.44) compared with that in the other groups (1.29 ± 0.525 in the Deb group and 1.12 ± 0.451 in the BTd group, P = .049).CONCLUSIONS: The treatment methods did not show significant differences in improving pain and function in patients with SLBC lesions and rotator cuff tears. The treatment A was not more effective than comparator B in patients with these conditions.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is like a ball and socket, and some of the parts inside got hurt (torn).  Doctors wanted to see which way was best to fix it.

They had three ways to fix the hurt parts:

1. **Clean up the mess:**  Like cleaning up a spill – they just cleaned the damaged area.
2. **Cut the rope:** They cut a small rope-like tendon (it's like a tiny muscle).
3. **Tie the rope:** They tied the rope back in place.

They tried these three ways on lots of grown-ups with the same shoulder problem.  They checked how much pain the grown-ups had, how well they could move their arms, and how strong their arms were after the surgery.

Guess what?  All three ways worked about the same!  Everyone felt less pain and could move their arms better.  However, cutting the rope sometimes made a little bump show up (like Popeye's arm!), and made their arm a little weaker.

So, the doctors learned that while all three ways helped, cutting the rope might have some small downsides.  The best way to fix the shoulder depends on the specific situation and what the doctor thinks is best for the patient.

Imagine your shoulder is like a ball and socket, and some of the parts inside got a little torn.  Doctors were trying to fix these tears in grown-ups' shoulders using a tiny camera and tools (arthroscopic surgery).

They tried three different ways to fix a specific part called the biceps tendon (it helps you bend your arm):

1. **Simple cleanup (Debridement):**  Just cleaning up the torn bits.
2. **Biceps tendon cut (Tenotomy):** Cutting the tendon.
3. **Biceps tendon reattachment (Tenodesis):**  Sewing the tendon back in a different place.

They wanted to see which way worked best.  They fixed the main shoulder tear in everyone, but used one of these three methods for the biceps tendon.

They found that all three ways helped people feel better and move their arms better.  However:

* **Cleanup** was the safest, with the least chance of a weird bulge in the arm (Popeye arm!). It also kept arm-twisting strength the best.
* **Cutting the tendon** helped with a specific type of shoulder pain, but sometimes caused that Popeye arm bulge and made twisting weaker.
* **Reattaching the tendon** was somewhere in between.

So, the best way to fix the biceps tendon depends on the person and what kind of problems they have.  The doctors learned that there's no single "best"

Title: Effects of a mindfulness-based weight loss intervention in adults with obesity: A randomized clinical trial.
abstract:
Objective: To determine whether adding mindfulness-based eating and stress management practices to a diet-exercise program improves weight loss.

Methods: In this study, 194 adults with obesity were randomized to a 5.5-month program with or without mindfulness training and identical diet-exercise guidelines. Intention-to-treat analyses with multiple imputation were used for missing data. The primary outcome was 18-month weight change.

Results: Estimated effects comparing the mindfulness to control arm showed weight loss at 12 months, -1.9 kg (95% CI: -4.5, 0.8; P = 0.17), and 18 months, -1.7 kg (95% CI: -4.7, 1.2; P = 0.24), which were not statistically significant. Changes in fasting glucose at 12 months were -3.1 mg/dl (95% CI: -6.3, 0.1; P = 0.06), and 18 months, -4.1 mg/dl (95% CI: -7.3, -0.9; P = 0.01). Changes in triglyceride/HDL ratio at 12 months were -0.57 (95% CI: -0.95, -0.18; P = 0.004), and 18 months, -0.36 (95% CI: -0.74, 0.03; P = 0.07). Estimates for other metabolic risk factors, including waist circumference, blood pressure, and C-reactive protein, were not statistically significant.

Conclusions: The treatment with mindfulness was not more effective than the comparator in patients with obesity for weight change.
plain_language_summary:

They had a group of grown-ups who were overweight.  Half the group got a diet and exercise plan, and the other half got the same diet and exercise plan *plus* lessons on paying attention to their food and how to handle stress.

After a year and a half, they weighed everyone again.  The group that got the extra lessons on paying attention and stress didn't lose significantly more weight than the group that only did the diet and exercise.  So, the extra lessons didn't seem to make a big difference in weight loss.

However, the group with the extra lessons *did* show some small improvements in other health things like blood sugar and cholesterol, although some of these improvements weren't completely certain.

In short:  The study showed that while mindfulness and stress management might help with some health markers, it didn't seem to help people lose significantly more weight.

Title: Effects of a mindfulness-based weight loss intervention in adults with obesity: A randomized clinical trial.
abstract:
Objective: To determine whether adding mindfulness-based eating and stress management practices to a diet-exercise program improves weight loss and metabolic syndrome components.

Methods: In this study 194 adults with obesity were randomized to a 5.5-month program with or without mindfulness training and identical diet-exercise guidelines. Intention-to-treat analyses with multiple imputation were used for missing data. The primary outcome was 18-month weight change.

Results: Estimated effects comparing the mindfulness to control arm favored the mindfulness arm in (a) weight loss at 12 months, -1.9 kg (95% CI: -4.5, 0.8; P = 0.17), and 18 months, -1.7 kg (95% CI: -4.7, 1.2; P = 0.24), though not statistically significant; (b) changes in fasting glucose at 12 months, -3.1 mg/dl (95% CI: -6.3, 0.1; P = 0.06), and 18 months, -4.1 mg/dl (95% CI: -7.3, -0.9; P = 0.01); and (c) changes in triglyceride/HDL ratio at 12 months, -0.57 (95% CI: -0.95, -0.18; P = 0.004), and 18 months, -0.36 (95% CI: -0.74, 0.03; P = 0.07). Estimates for other metabolic risk factors were not statistically significant, including waist circumference, blood pressure, and C-reactive protein.

Conclusions: Mindfulness enhancements to a diet-exercise program did not show substantial weight loss benefit but may promote long-term improvement in some aspects of metabolic health in obesity that requires further study.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine a science experiment to see if being mindful (paying close attention to your body and feelings) while dieting and exercising helps people lose more weight.

They had almost 200 grown-ups who were overweight.  Half did a regular diet and exercise plan. The other half did the same diet and exercise plan, but also practiced mindfulness – like paying attention to how their food tastes and how their body feels.

They checked their weight and other health things (like blood sugar and cholesterol) after 12 and 18 months.

The results showed that mindfulness didn't make a huge difference in how much weight people lost.  However, it *might* have helped a little with some other health things, like blood sugar levels, over the long term (18 months).  The scientists aren't completely sure yet, so they want to do more experiments.  Basically, being mindful while dieting and exercising might be helpful for health in other ways, even if it doesn't always lead to more weight loss.

Title: The Effect of Perioperative Corticosteroids in Total Hip Arthroplasty: A Prospective Double-Blind Placebo Controlled Pilot Study.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Focus on another objective (e.g., trial is (-) but they say they accomplish some goal that they did not prespecify)
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Surgery produces a rapid rise in interleukin 6 (IL-6) which may increase the risk of deep vein thrombosis and medical complications. Perioperative corticosteroids suppress IL-6 release in patients undergoing total knee arthroplasty. This study evaluates the effects of a perioperative corticosteroid regimen on IL-6 formation, thrombogenesis, fibrinolysis, and clinical outcomes in patients undergoing unilateral, uncemented, total hip arthroplasty.

METHODS: Twenty-seven patients (14 placebo and 13 study) were enrolled in this randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. The study group received 20 mg of prednisone orally followed by 2 doses of intravenous hydrocortisone, each 8 hours apart. Blood was drawn at several time points for IL-6, prothrombin fragment 1.2, and plasmin-alpha-2-antiplasmin complex, a marker of fibrinolysis. In-hospital visual analog pain (visual analog scale) scores, patient-controlled analgesia use, and ability to climb stairs were recorded.

RESULTS: Mean serum IL-6 levels at 6 and 24 hours postoperatively were significantly lower for the study group, whereas serum prothrombin fragment 1.2 and plasmin-alpha-2-antiplasmin were not statistically different at any study time point. Average pain scores were similar (P > .05), but study group experience less severe pain (P < .01) and less patient-controlled analgesia (PВ = .02). At 3 months, 4 patients in the placebo and 1 patient in the study group had difficulty going up and down staircases (PВ = .08).

CONCLUSION: The use of corticosteroids was associated with a statistically significant decrease in IL-6 at 6 and 24 hours postoperatively but did not affect thrombogenic markers. The study group had improved postoperative analgesia and a trend toward improved functional outcome at 3 months postoperatively.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your body is like a LEGO castle.  Sometimes, when you have surgery (like building a really big LEGO castle), your body makes a special chemical called IL-6.  Too much IL-6 can be like having too many wobbly LEGO pieces – it can cause problems.

Doctors wanted to see if giving patients a special medicine (like super-strong LEGO glue) called corticosteroids before and after hip replacement surgery could stop the body from making so much IL-6.

They did a test with two groups of people: one group got the corticosteroid medicine (the "study group"), and the other group got a fake medicine (the "placebo group").  They checked their blood to see how much IL-6 they had.

They found that the group who got the corticosteroid medicine had much less IL-6 after surgery.  This means the "LEGO glue" helped keep things stable!  The medicine also helped them have less pain and be able to climb stairs better after surgery.

So, the study showed that this special medicine can help reduce the wobbly LEGO pieces (IL-6) after hip surgery, leading to less pain and a quicker recovery.

Title: The Use of a Closed-Suction Drain in Revision Knee Arthroplasty May Not Be Necessary: A Prospective Randomized Study.
abstract:
BACKGROUND: The benefit of suction drains (SD) for the first 24-48 hours following joint replacement surgery is controversial. We aimed to determine if there is any difference in the early outcome of revision TKA when performed with or without SD.

METHODS: 83 cases indicated for revision knee arthroplasty were randomized to receive (42) or not receive (41) a deep intra-articular drain. First-stage revisions for treating periprosthetic infection were excluded. The primary outcome assessed was total blood loss. Secondary outcomes included the number of transfusions, fever, and wound complication rate at 24 months follow-up.

RESULTS: There were no significant differences in demographic factors, wound complications, and infection rate 24 months after surgery in either group. Average blood loss was 1856ml and 1533ml for the drain and no-drain groups, respectively (P value=0.0470). The need for transfusion was less in the no-drain group with an average of 0.15 unit/patient compared to an average of 0.37 unit/patient for the drain group (P value=0.0432).

CONCLUSION: The treatment with suction drains was not more effective than without drains in patients undergoing revision knee arthroplasty.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is like a really complicated LEGO castle, and it needs a repair (a "revision knee arthroplasty").  Doctors sometimes use a little tube called a suction drain to suck up extra blood and fluid after the repair.  But some doctors aren't sure if it's really helpful.

This study looked at 83 people who needed knee repairs.  Half got the suction drain, half didn't.  They wanted to see if the drain made a difference in how much blood was lost, if people needed blood transfusions, or if there were any problems with the wound.

They found that the drain didn't really help!  People who didn't have the drain actually lost less blood and needed fewer blood transfusions.  There weren't any other big differences between the two groups.

So, the study suggests that using the suction drain after this type of knee surgery might not be necessary.

Title: The Use of a Closed-Suction Drain in Revision Knee Arthroplasty May Not Be Necessary: A Prospective Randomized Study.
abstract:
BACKGROUND: The benefit of suction drains (SD) for the first 24-48 hours following joint replacement surgery is controversial. We aimed to determine if there is any difference in the early outcome of revision TKA when performed with, or without SD.

METHODS: 83 cases indicated for revision knee arthroplasty were randomized to receive (42) or not receive (41) a deep intra-articlular drain. First-stage revisions for treating periprosthetic infection were excluded. Patients were statistically compared for demographic parameters, early complications and early knee functional outcome. The assessed outcomes included total blood loss, number of transfusions, fever and wound complication rate at 24 months follow-up. In addition, the change in knee society score at 12 weeks postoperatively was compared between the groups.

RESULTS: There were no significant difference in demographic factors, wound complications, knee scores at 12 weeks and infection rate 24 months after surgery in either group. Title: Amiodarone, Lidocaine, or Placebo in Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest.
Spin in Abstract Title/Results: RESULTS: Focus on (+) modified treatment population (e.g., report per-protocol rather than intention to treat)
abstract:
Background: Antiarrhythmic drugs are used commonly in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest for shock-refractory ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia, but without proven survival benefit.

Methods: In this randomized, double-blind trial, we compared parenteral amiodarone, lidocaine, and saline placebo, along with standard care, in adults who had nontraumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, shock-refractory ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia after at least one shock, and vascular access. Paramedics enrolled patients at 10 North American sites. The primary outcome was survival to hospital discharge; the secondary outcome was favorable neurologic function at discharge. The per-protocol (primary analysis) population included all randomly assigned participants who met eligibility criteria and received any dose of a trial drug and whose initial cardiac-arrest rhythm of ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia was refractory to shock.

Results: In the per-protocol population, 3026 patients were randomly assigned to amiodarone (974), lidocaine (993), or placebo (1059); of those, 24.4%, 23.7%, and 21.0%, respectively, survived to hospital discharge. The difference in survival rate for amiodarone versus placebo was 3.2 percentage points (95% confidence interval [CI], -0.4 to 7.0; P=0.08); for lidocaine versus placebo, 2.6 percentage points (95% CI, -1.0 to 6.3; P=0.16); and for amiodarone versus lidocaine, 0.7 percentage points (95% CI, -3.2 to 4.7; P=0.70). Neurologic outcome at discharge was similar in the three groups. There was heterogeneity of treatment effect with respect to whether the arrest was witnessed (P=0.05); active drugs were associated with a survival rate that was significantly higher than the rate with placebo among patients with bystander-witnessed arrest but not among those with unwitnessed arrest. More amiodarone recipients required temporary cardiac pacing than did recipients of lidocaine or placebo.

Conclusions: Overall, neither amiodarone nor lidocaine resulted in a significantly higher rate of survival or favorable neurologic outcome than the rate with placebo among patients with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest due to initial shock-refractory ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your heart suddenly stops beating correctly.  Doctors call this cardiac arrest. Sometimes, even after giving the heart a strong electric shock (like in the movies!), it still doesn't beat properly.  

Doctors wanted to see if giving certain medicines (amiodarone and lidocaine) after the shock would help people survive. They did a big experiment where they gave some people one medicine, some people another, and some people just a harmless saltwater solution (placebo).

They followed these people to see if they survived and if they were okay afterwards.  

The results showed that the medicines didn't really help people survive any more than the saltwater solution.  It was pretty much the same for everyone.  Only if someone was seen having a heart attack by another person (a bystander-witnessed arrest) did the medicine seem to help a little, but not enough to be sure.

So, the experiment showed that these medicines aren't very helpful for this type of heart problem after an electric shock.

Imagine your heart is a drum, beating to keep you alive.  Sometimes, the drum goes crazy – beating too fast or erratically (that's called ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia).  Doctors use a shock (like a jolt) to try and restart it to a normal beat.

This study was testing if giving certain medicines (amiodarone and lidocaine) *after* the shock helped people survive.  They compared these medicines to a saltwater solution (placebo – it doesn't actually do anything medically).  They gave these to people whose hearts weren't beating normally even *after* the shock.

Title: No difference between fixed- and mobile-bearing total knee arthroplasty in activities of daily living and pain: a randomized clinical trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
Purpose: Until now, there are no definitive conclusions regarding functional differences related to middle- and long-term everyday activities and patient pain following implantation of mobile- and fixed-platform tibial prostheses. The aim of this study was to determine whether there are middle-term differences in knee function and pain in patients undergoing fixed- and mobile-bearing total knee arthroplasty (TKA).

Methods: Eligible patients were randomized into two groups: the first group received TKA implantation with a fixed tibial platform (group A); the second group received TKA with a mobile tibial platform (group B). Patients were followed up (2 years), and their symptoms and limitations in daily living activities were evaluated using the Knee Outcome Survey-Activities of Daily Living Scale (ADLS), in addition to pain evaluation assessed using the pain visual analogue scale (VAS).

Results: There were no significant differences in function and symptoms in the ADLS and VAS between the study groups.

Conclusion: The type of platform used in TKA (fixed vs. mobile) does not change the symptoms, function or pain of patients 2 years post-surgery. Although mobile TKAs may have better short-term results, at medium- and long-term follow-up they do not present important clinical differences compared with fixed-platform TKAs. This information is important so that surgeons can choose the most suitable implant for each patient.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is broken and needs a new part, like a robot knee!  Doctors use two types of robot knees: one that moves a lot (mobile) and one that doesn't move as much (fixed).

This study wanted to see if one type of robot knee was better than the other after a couple of years.  They gave some people the mobile knee and some the fixed knee.  Then they checked how well people could move and how much pain they had.

Guess what?  After two years, there was no big difference! Both types of robot knees worked about the same.  So, even though one might feel better right away, in the long run, it doesn't really matter which type the doctor chooses.  The doctor can pick whichever one is best for that person's specific needs.

Title: The Efficacy and Safety of Combination of Intravenous and Topical Tranexamic Acid in Revision Hip Arthroplasty: A Randomized, Controlled Trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
Background: Revision total hip arthroplasty (THA) is associated with substantial blood loss and a high probability of blood transfusion in the perioperative period. This study aimed to evaluate the efficacy and safety of combination of intravenous (IV) and topical tranexamic acid (TXA) in revision THA.

Methods: Eighty-four consecutive patients undergoing revision THA were randomized into combined group and IV-TXA group. Patients in the combined group were given intravenously 15 mg/kg TXA as a preoperative, and topical TXA solution was applied at a concentration of 3 g TXA per 100-mL saline during the different procedure points. Patients in the IV-TXA group were given intravenously 15 mg/kg TXA alone. The primary outcome was total blood loss.

Results: The mean total blood loss was lower in the combined group than the IV-TXA group. One deep vein thrombosis and 4 calf muscular vein thrombosis in the combined group and 3 calf muscular vein thrombosis in the IV-TXA group were detected by Doppler ultrasound. No pulmonary embolism was observed, and no significant differences were found in other complications between the 2 groups.

Conclusion: The treatment with combined administration of IV and topical TXA was not more effective than IV-TXA alone in patients undergoing revision THA.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your hip is broken and needs a really big repair, like replacing a whole part of it.  This is called a hip replacement surgery.  Sometimes, these surgeries cause a lot of bleeding.  Doctors want to stop that bleeding.

Doctors tried a medicine called TXA to see if it could help reduce bleeding during these big hip surgeries.  They gave it to some people in two different ways:  some got it through an IV (like a needle in their arm), and others got it through an IV *and* also directly on the surgery area.

They wanted to see which way worked better at stopping the bleeding.  They found that giving the medicine through the IV alone worked just as well as giving it both through the IV and directly on the surgery area.  So, giving it both ways didn't make a big difference.  Both ways were pretty safe too; there weren't many problems.

Background: Revision total hip arthroplasty (THA) is associated with substantial blood loss and a high probability of blood transfusion in the perioperative period. plain_language_summary:
Imagine your leg bone is broken really badly and needs a big surgery to replace the hip joint.  This surgery is called a "revision total hip arthroplasty" (that's a mouthful!).  Sometimes, these surgeries cause a lot of bleeding.

They found that the group who got the TXA both ways (IV and on the wound) bled much less and needed fewer blood transfusions (getting extra blood from someone else).  The extra TXA didn't seem to cause any more problems like blood clots in their legs.

Title: The Effect of Cell Dose on the Early Magnetic Resonance Morphological Outcomes of Autologous Cell Implantation for Articular Cartilage Defects in the Knee: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Although autologous chondrocyte implantation (ACI) has been established as a standard treatment for large full-thickness cartilage defects, the effect of different doses of autologous chondrocyte products on structural outcomes has never been examined.

HYPOTHESIS: In ACI, the dose level may have an influence on medium-term magnetic resonance morphological findings after treatment.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 1.

METHODS: A total of 75 patients who underwent ACI using a pure, autologous, third-generation matrix-associated ACI product were divided into 3 groups representing different doses: 3 to 7 spheroids/cm(2), 10 to 30 spheroids/cm(2), and 40 to 70 spheroids/cm(2). Magnetic resonance imaging was performed at 1.5, 3, 6, and 12 months after ACI and was evaluated by the magnetic resonance observation of cartilage repair tissue (MOCART) score and the Knee injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS).

RESULTS: MOCART scores showed improvements after 3 months, with slight dose dependence, and further improvement after 12 months, although without significant dose dependence. The mean MOCART scores after 3 months (0 = worst, 100 = best) were 59.8, 64.5, and 64.7 for the low-, medium-, and high-dose groups, respectively, and 62.9 for all patients; at 12 months, these were 74.1, 74.5, and 68.8 for the respective dose groups and 72.4 for all patients. Several MOCART items (surface of repair tissue, structure of repair tissue, signal intensity of repair tissue, subchondral bone, and synovitis) showed a more rapid response with the medium and high doses than with the low dose, suggesting a potential dose relationship. No significant correlation between the MOCART (overall and subscores) with clinical outcomes as assessed by the overall KOOS was detected at 3- and 12-month assessments.

CONCLUSION: This study reveals a trend toward earlier recovery after treatment with higher spheroid doses in terms of better defect filling for full-thickness cartilage defects of the knee, while outcomes after 12 months were similar in all dose groups. However, a correlation with clinical outcomes or the failure rate at 1 year after ACI was not found. A longer follow-up will be required for more definite conclusions on the clinical relevance of ACI cell density to be drawn.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee cartilage is like a bouncy ball on your knee joint.  Sometimes, that bouncy ball gets a big hole in it (a cartilage defect).  Doctors can fix this using a special treatment called ACI.  They take some of your own cells, grow them, and put them back into the hole to help it heal.

This study was trying to figure out:  If you put in more cells (higher dose), does the hole heal faster and better?  They divided people into three groups: a low dose, a medium dose, and a high dose of cells.  They used special pictures (MRI scans) of the knees to see how well the holes were healing.

They found that the medium and high dose groups showed some improvement a bit faster.  But after a year, all three groups were pretty much the same.  So, while more cells might help things heal a little quicker at first, it didn't make a huge difference in the long run.  They need to study it longer to be completely sure.

Safety data indicated no significant differences in adverse events or treatment discontinuation across the dose groups.

CONCLUSION: The treatment with different doses of spheroids was not more effective than comparator doses in patients with full-thickness cartilage defects of the knee. A longer follow-up will be required for more definite conclusions on the clinical relevance of ACI cell density to be drawn.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee has a big hole in the cartilage (the slippery stuff that lets your bones move smoothly).  Doctors can fix this with a special treatment called ACI, where they take some of your own cells, grow them, and put them back in the hole to help it heal.

This study was like a science experiment to see if using more of these grown cells makes the knee heal better.  They divided people into three groups: one with a small amount of cells, one with a medium amount, and one with a large amount.  They used special pictures (MRI) of the knees to see how well the holes were healing.

What they found was surprising!  Even though they used different amounts of cells, the knees healed about the same in all three groups.  It didn't seem to matter how many cells they used.  They need to watch these people for longer to be completely sure, but so far, it looks like using more cells doesn't make a big difference in how well the knee heals.

Title: Surgical treatment of lesions of the long head of the biceps brachii tendon with rotator cuff tear: a prospective randomized clinical trial comparing the clinical results of tenotomy and tenodesis.
Journal: Journal of Shoulder and Elbow Surgery
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint, Other
abstract:
Background: Tenotomy and tenodesis are common treatments for lesions in the long head of biceps tendon (LHBT); however, which treatment is superior is still controversial. This study compared the outcomes of tenotomy with outcomes of tenodesis for treatment of LHBT lesions with rotator cuff tears.

Methods: The study enrolled 128 patients with LHBT lesions and small- to medium-sized rotator cuff tear. Arthroscopic LHBT tenotomy was done in 56 patients (group I), and LHBT tenodesis was done for 72 patients (group II) with rotator cuff repair. American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons Score, simple shoulder test, pain visual analog scale, range of motion, and cosmetic changes were assessed initially, at 3, 6, and 12 months postoperatively, and the last visit. The elbow motor power and magnetic resonance imaging were evaluated at 12 months.

Results: Both groups showed improvement in functional scores after treatments, but no significant difference was found between the 2 groups at each assessment. The rate of Popeye deformity was 3 times higher in group I (P = .04). Group II showed greater forearm supination power than group I (P = .02). On magnetic resonance imaging, 45 patients (80.4%) in group I showed maintenance of the LHBT cut end within the bicipital groove, whereas 65 patients (90.3%) in group II showed maintenance of fixated LHBT.

Conclusion: For the treatment of LHBT lesions with rotator cuff tear, patients with tenotomy and tenodesis both showed significant improvements in functional scores. The incidence of Popeye deformity was about 3-times higher in tenotomy group. No significant differences in elbow motor power were observed except greater forearm supination power in the tenodesis group.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is like a ball and socket, and the biceps muscle (the one that makes your arm bend) has a tendon (like a strong rope) that goes through the shoulder.  Sometimes that "rope" gets hurt.

Doctors have two ways to fix it:

1. **Tenotomy:**  They cut the rope.  This is like cutting a tangled string to untangle it.
2. **Tenodesis:** They reattach the rope in a different, stronger way. This is like tying a knot to make the string stronger.

Doctors wanted to see which way is better. They looked at 128 people with hurt "ropes" in their shoulders.  Half had their rope cut (tenotomy), and half had it reattached (tenodesis).

They checked how well people could move their arms and how much pain they had.  Both ways helped people feel better!

But:

* People who had their rope cut were three times more likely to have a "Popeye" arm (a bulge in their bicep).
* People who had their rope reattached were a little stronger at turning their forearm.

So, both ways work, but reattaching the rope (tenodesis) might be slightly better because it makes your arm stronger and less likely to bulge.

Title: A Prospective Randomized Trial Comparing Surgical and Nonsurgical Treatments of Acute Achilles Tendon Ruptures.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage
abstract:
BACKGROUND: The optimal treatment of acute Achilles tendon ruptures for active patients is under debate.

PURPOSE: To compare clinical outcomes and calf muscle strength recovery after the nonsurgical treatment and open surgical repair of acute Achilles tendon ruptures with identical accelerated rehabilitation programs.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 1.

METHODS: From 2009 to 2013, a total of 60 patients with an acute Achilles tendon rupture were randomized to surgery or nonsurgical treatment. Nonsurgical treatment included first a week of cast immobilization, followed by a functional orthosis for 6 weeks, allowing full weightbearing after week 1 and active plantar flexion after week 5. Surgery was simple end-to-end open repair, and postoperative treatment was identical to nonsurgical treatment. Outcome measures included the Leppilahti Achilles tendon performance score, isokinetic calf muscle strength, and RAND 36-Item Health Survey at 18-month follow-up.

RESULTS: At 18-month follow-up, the mean Leppilahti score was 79.5 and 75.7 for the surgically and nonsurgically treated groups, respectively (mean difference, 3.8; 95% CI, -1.9 to 9.5; P = .19). Angle-specific peak torque results of affected legs showed that surgery resulted in faster and better recovery of calf muscle strength over the entire range of motion of the ankle joint: at 6 months, the difference varied from 16% to 24% (P = .016), favoring the surgically treated group, whereas at 18 months, surgically treated patients had 10% to 18% greater strength results (P = .037). At 18 months, a 14% difference in the peak torque of the affected leg favored the surgical group versus the nonsurgical group (mean peak torque, 110.3 vs 96.5 NВ·m, respectively; mean difference, 13.6 NВ·m; 95% CI, 2.0-25.1 NВ·m; P = .022). The RAND 36-Item Health Survey indicated better results in the domains of physical functioning (P = .006) and bodily pain (P = .037) for surgically treated patients.

CONCLUSION: Surgical and nonsurgical treatments of acute Achilles tendon ruptures have similar results in terms of the Achilles tendon performance score, but surgery restores calf muscle strength earlier over the entire range of motion of the ankle joint, with a 10% to 18% strength difference favoring surgery at 18 months. Surgery may also result in better health-related quality of life in the domains of physical functioning and bodily pain compared with nonsurgical treatment.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your Achilles tendon (the one at the back of your ankle) is broken.  Doctors aren't sure if it's better to fix it with surgery or just let it heal on its own.

This study looked at 60 people with broken Achilles tendons.  Half had surgery, half didn't.  Both groups did the same exercises to help them heal.

After 18 months, both groups could pretty much use their ankles the same.  But the group that had surgery got their ankle muscles stronger faster.  They were also less in pain and could do more physical activities.

So, while both ways of fixing a broken Achilles tendon work, surgery seems to help people get stronger and feel better quicker.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine your Achilles tendon is like a strong rope in your ankle that helps you run and jump.  Sometimes, that rope can break (rupture).  Doctors weren't sure if it's better to fix the broken rope with surgery or let it heal on its own.

This study compared two groups of people with broken Achilles tendons. One group had surgery to fix the rope, and the other group didn't have surgery – they let it heal naturally, but with special support and exercises.  Both groups did the same exercises afterwards.

After 18 months, both groups were almost equally good at using their ankles again.  The study showed that surgery didn't make a big difference in how well their ankles worked compared to letting it heal naturally.  So, for this type of injury, surgery might not always be necessary.

Title: Minimizing pre- and post-shock pauses during the use of an automatic external defibrillator by two different voice prompt protocols. A randomized controlled trial of a bundle of measures
Spin in Abstract Title/Results: RESULTS: Focus on (+) within or between group comparison of secondary endpoints
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Previous large retrospective analyses have found an association between duration of peri-shock pauses in cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) and survival. In a randomized trial, we tested whether shortening these pauses improves survival after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA).

METHODS: Patients with OHCA between May 2006 and January 2014 with shockable initial rhythm, treated by first responders, were randomized to two automated external defibrillator (AED) treatment protocols. In the control protocol, AEDs performed post-shock analysis and prompted rescuers to a pulse check (Guidelines 2000). In the experimental protocol, a 15s period of CPR during and after charging of the AED was added to the voice prompts, and CPR was resumed immediately after defibrillation (modification of the Guidelines 2005). The primary outcome was survival assessed at hospital admission and discharge.

RESULTS: Of 1174 OHCA patients, 456 met the inclusion criteria: 227 were randomly assigned to the experimental protocol and 229 to the control protocol. Similar proportions of patients survived to hospital admission (experimental: 62% vs. control: 65%; RR [95%CI] 0.96 [0.83-1.10], P=0.51) and hospital discharge (experimental: 42% vs. control: 38%; RR [95%CI] 1.09 [0.87-1.37], P=0.46).

CONCLUSION: The treatment with AEDs using the experimental protocol was not more effective than the control protocol in improving survival to hospital admission or discharge in patients with OHCA and shockable initial rhythms.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine someone's heart suddenly stops beating (that's called cardiac arrest).  Doctors use a machine called an AED (like a super-powered defibrillator) to shock the heart and restart it.  Sometimes, the AED needs a few seconds to get ready to shock, and during that time, rescuers usually stop chest compressions (CPR).

This study wanted to see if it's better to *keep* doing CPR during those short pauses while the AED is getting ready.  They had two groups of people whose hearts stopped: one group got the usual short pause, and the other group kept doing CPR the whole time.

They followed both groups to see who survived.  Guess what?  It didn't make much difference!  Both groups had about the same number of people who survived.  So, keeping doing CPR during those short pauses doesn't seem to help people survive better after their heart stops.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine someone's heart suddenly stops.  Doctors and paramedics use a machine called an AED (like a super-powered defibrillator) to try and restart it with a shock.

This study looked at whether it's better to give CPR (chest compressions) *right away* after the shock, or to wait a little bit.  They had two groups of people whose hearts stopped:

* **Group 1 (Control):**  The AED made them wait a while before starting CPR again after the shock.
* **Group 2 (Experimental):** The AED made them start CPR right away after the shock.

They wanted to see if starting CPR sooner helped people survive.

They found that Group 2 had much shorter waiting times after the shock, but surprisingly, *both groups survived at about the same rate*.  So, even though giving CPR sooner meant more CPR overall, it didn't make a difference in whether people lived or died.

Title: No Difference in Early Analgesia Between Liposomal Bupivacaine Injection and Intrathecal Morphine After TKA.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage
abstract:
Background: Opioid analgesics have been a standard modality for postoperative pain management after total knee arthroplasty (TKA) but are also associated with increased risk of nausea, pruritus, vomiting, respiratory depression, prolonged ileus, and cognitive dysfunction. There is still a need for a method of anesthesia that can deliver effective long-term postoperative pain relief without incurring the high cost and health burden of opioids and nerve blocks.

Methods: This multicenter, blind trial randomized 119 patients undergoing TKA with spinal anesthesia to receive spinal anesthesia plus periarticular injection with liposomal bupivacaine (40 patients), spinal anesthesia with bupivacaine plus intrathecal morphine (41 patients) but no liposomal bupivacaine injection, or spinal anesthesia with bupivacaine (38 patients) and no liposomal bupivacaine injection. The two groups that did not receive periarticular liposomal bupivacaine did receive periarticular injection with ropivacaine, and all three groups had ketorolac (30 mg) plus epinephrine (1:1000) in the periarticular injections. Patients in all three groups received identical perioperative multimodal analgesic and antiemetic drugs. All patients were analyzed in the group to which they were randomized and no patients were lost to follow-up. The primary study endpoints were visual analog score (VAS) for pain and narcotic use during postoperative day 1.

Results: Mean VAS pain in the liposomal bupivacaine PAI group was lower than that for the ropivacaine PAI group at 6 hours (1.8 ± 2.1 versus 3.3 ± 2.3, p = 0.005, mean difference: 1.5, 95% confidence interval [CI], 0.5-2.5) and 12 hours (1.5 ± 2.0 versus 3.3 ± 2.4, p < 0.001, mean difference: 1.8, 95% CI, 0.8-2.8) after surgery. The morphine spinal group had lower pain compared with the liposomal bupivacaine PAI group at 6 hours (0.9 ± 1.8 versus 1.8 ± 2.1, p = 0.035, mean difference: 1.0, 95% CI, 0.1-1.8), but there was no difference at 12 hours (0.8 ± 1.5 versus 1.5 ± 2.0, p = 0.086, mean difference: 0.7, 95% CI, -0.1 to 1.5). Both the liposomal bupivacaine group (13% [five of 40]) and the ropivacaine group (5% [two of 38]) had fewer incidents of itching (pruritus) than the spinal morphine group (38% [15 of 41]) (p = 0.001).

Conclusions: The treatment with liposomal bupivacaine was not more effective than spinal anesthesia with intrathecal morphine in patients with TKA in terms of pain control on postoperative day 1.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee needs a big repair, like replacing a worn-out part with a new one.  Doctors often use strong painkillers called opioids after this kind of surgery, but they can have bad side effects like nausea, vomiting, and itching.

Doctors wanted to find a better way to manage pain without these side effects.  They tested three different pain relief methods on people getting knee surgery:

1. **Method 1:** A special long-lasting numbing medicine (liposomal bupivacaine) injected around the knee.
2. **Method 2:** A different numbing medicine (bupivacaine) injected into the spine, plus a small amount of morphine also injected into the spine.
3. **Method 3:** Just the numbing medicine (bupivacaine) injected into the spine.

They wanted to see which method worked best to control pain and had the fewest side effects.

They found that:

* **Method 2 (spinal morphine) worked best for pain relief in the first few hours after surgery.**
* **Method 1 (liposomal bupivacaine) caused less itching than Method 2.**

So, while the special numbing medicine (Method 1) didn't completely beat the morphine (Method 2) for pain relief, it did cause less itching.  Doctors are still looking for the best way to manage pain after knee surgery without using too many opioids.

abstract:
Background: Opioid analgesics have been a standard modality for postoperative pain management after total knee arthroplasty (TKA) but are also associated with increased risk of nausea, pruritus, vomiting, respiratory depression, prolonged ileus, and cognitive dysfunction. There is still a need for a method of anesthesia that can deliver effective long-term postoperative pain relief without incurring the high cost and health burden of opioids and nerve blocks.

Questions/purposes: (1) Is liposomal bupivacaine-based periarticular injection (PAI) more effective than morphine-based spinal anesthesia or ropivacaine-based PAI in controlling postoperative pain after TKA? (2) Do patients treated with liposomal bupivacaine-based PAI experience fewer opioid-related adverse events compared with patients treated with morphine-based spinal anesthesia or ropivacaine-based PAI in controlling postoperative pain after TKA?

Methods: This multicenter, blind trial randomized 119 patients undergoing TKA with spinal anesthesia to receive spinal anesthesia plus periarticular injection with liposomal bupivacaine (40 patients), spinal anesthesia with bupivacaine plus intrathecal morphine (41 patients) but no liposomal bupivacaine injection, or spinal anesthesia with bupivacaine (38 patients) and no liposomal bupivacaine injection. The two groups that did not receive periarticular liposomal bupivacaine did receive periarticular injection with ropivacaine, and all three groups had ketorolac (30 mg) plus epinephrine (1:1000) in the periarticular injections. Patients in all three groups received identical perioperative multimodal analgesic and antiemetic drugs. All patients were analyzed in the group to which they were randomized and no patients were lost to followup. The primary study endpoints were visual analog score (VAS) for pain and narcotic use during postoperative day 1. Secondary endpoints included side effects associated with narcotic administration during the hospital stay.

Results: Mean VAS pain in the liposomal bupivacaine PAI group was lower than that for the ropivacaine PAI group at 6 hours (1.8 ± 2.1 versus 3.3 ± 2.3, p = 0.005, mean difference: 1.5, 95% confidence interval [CI], 0.5-2.5) and 12 hours (1.5 ± 2.0 versus 3.3 ± 2.4, p < 0.001, mean difference: 1.8, 95% CI, 0.8-2.8) after surgery. The morphine spinal group had lower pain compared with the liposomal bupivacaine PAI group at 6 hours (0.9 ± 1.8 versus 1.8 ± 2.1, p = 0.035, mean difference: 1.0, 95% CI, 0.1-1.8), but there was no difference at 12 hours (0.8 ± 1.5 versus 1.5 ± 2.0, p = 0.086, mean difference: 0.7, 95% CI, -0.1 to 1.5). The magnitude of the differences at 6 and 12 hours are near the lower end of minimal clinically important differences reported in the literature, and thus the improvement shown in this study may only represent a small clinical improvement. Both the liposomal bupivacaine group (13% [five of 40]) and the ropivacaine group (5% [two of 38]) had fewer incidents of itching (pruritus) than the spinal morphine group (38% [15 of 41]) (p = 0.001).

Conclusions: This prospective multicenter three-arm blind randomized controlled trial showed potentially improved pain control at 6 and 12 hours in the liposomal bupivacaine and intrathecal morphine groups compared with the ropivacaine group at the cost of much higher incidences of pruritus (itching) in the intrathecal morphine group. Based on these results, we prefer the use of PAI with liposomal bupivacaine as an alternative to spinal anesthesia with intrathecal morphine as a result of similar postoperative pain control and the potential for reducing adverse events.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine someone had a knee replacement surgery (TKA).  Doctors usually give strong pain medicine (opioids) afterwards, but those medicines can make people feel sick, itchy, or even have trouble breathing.

Doctors wanted to find a better way to manage pain after knee surgery without using so many opioids. They tested three different types of pain relief:

1. **Liposomal bupivacaine injection (like a special shot near the knee):** This is like a long-lasting numbing medicine.
2. **Morphine spinal:** This is morphine medicine injected into the spine.
3. **Ropivacaine injection (another type of shot near the knee):**  Similar to the liposomal bupivacaine injection, but not as long-lasting.

They gave each of these to different groups of people who had knee surgery.  They measured how much pain each group had and if they had any side effects (like itching).

What they found:

* The special shot (liposomal bupivacaine) and the morphine spinal both helped with pain, but the morphine spinal caused a lot of itching.
* The special shot caused less itching than the morphine spinal.

The doctors concluded that the special shot (liposomal bupivacaine) might be a better choice because it helped with pain and caused fewer side effects than the morphine.  It's like finding a better, less-itchy

Title: Irradiated Hamstring Tendon Allograft Versus Autograft for Anatomic Double-Bundle Anterior Cruciate Ligament Reconstruction: Midterm Clinical Outcomes.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Most studies on grafts for anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) reconstruction (ACLR) have been of autografts or nonirradiated allografts with a single-bundle (SB) technique. Outcome reports evaluating anatomic double-bundle (DB) ACLR with a hamstring tendon autograft versus irradiated allograft are rare.

PURPOSE: To compare the clinical outcomes of arthroscopic anatomic DB ACLR with a hamstring tendon autograft versus irradiated allograft.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 2.

METHODS: Between 2008 and 2009, a total of 107 patients undergoing arthroscopic DB ACLR were prospectively randomized consecutively into 1 of 2 groups (autograft [Auto] group and irradiated allograft [Ir-Allo] group). All the surgical procedures were performed by the same senior surgeon using the DB reconstruction technique. All irradiated hamstring tendon allografts were sterilized with 2.5 Mrad of irradiation before distribution and were obtained from a single certified tissue bank. Graft fixation on the femoral side was by an Endobutton, and on the tibial side by a bioabsorbable interference screw augmented with a staple. The same rehabilitation protocol was applied to all patients. Before surgery and at a mean of 6.9 years of follow-up, patients were evaluated by the same observer according to objective and subjective clinical evaluations including detailed history, physical examination, radiography, functional knee ligament testing, KT-2000 arthrometer testing, Harner vertical jump and Daniel 1-legged hop tests, Lysholm score, Tegner score, International Knee Documentation Committee (IKDC) standard evaluation form, and Cincinnati knee score.

RESULTS: A total of 83 patients (Auto: n = 40 [mean age, 29.2 ± 6.9 years]; Ir-Allo: n = 43 [mean age, 28.6 ± 7.2 years]) fulfilled follow-up and clinical evaluations. No significant differences were found between the 2 groups according to the overall IKDC functional and subjective evaluations as well as testing of activity levels. Significant between-group differences were found when comparing the results at final follow-up according to the Lachman test, anterior drawer test, pivot-shift test, and KT-2000 arthrometer measurements (P < .001). The rate of laxity (side-to-side difference >5 mm) with an irradiated allograft (30.2%) was higher than that with an autograft (7.5%) (P < .001). The failure rate in the Ir-Allo group (30.2%) was higher than that in the Auto group (7.5%) (P < .001). There were 10.0% (4/40) of patients in the Auto group and 32.6% (19/43) of patients in the Ir-Allo group who had arthritic progression (P < .05).

CONCLUSION: The treatment with a hamstring tendon autograft was not more effective than an irradiated allograft in patients undergoing DB ACLR.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee has a strong rope inside called the ACL that helps it bend properly.  Sometimes this rope breaks and needs to be replaced.

Doctors did a study to see if using a new rope made from the patient's own body (an "autograft") was better than using a rope from someone else's body (an "allograft") that had been treated with radiation.  They used the same surgery method on both groups of people.

They followed the people for almost 7 years to see how well their knees worked.  They found that the knees with the patient's own rope (autograft) were a little bit stronger and less likely to have problems later on.  However, the difference wasn't huge.  Both types of rope worked reasonably well to fix the knee.
