Title: A Prospective Randomized Study Comparing the Interference Screw and Suture Anchor Techniques for Biceps Tenodesis.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Several methods are used to perform biceps tenodesis in patients with superior labrum-biceps complex (SLBC) lesions accompanied by a rotator cuff tear. However, limited clinical data are available regarding the best technique in terms of clinical and anatomic outcomes.

PURPOSE: To compare the clinical and anatomic outcomes of the interference screw (IS) and suture anchor (SA) fixation techniques for biceps tenodesis performed along with arthroscopic rotator cuff repair.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 2.

METHODS: A total of 80 patients who underwent arthroscopic rotator cuff repair with SLBC lesions were prospectively enrolled and randomly divided according to the tenodesis method: the IS and SA groups. The primary outcome was the functional outcome evaluated with the visual analog scale (VAS) for pain, American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons (ASES) score, Simple Shoulder Test (SST), Constant score, Korean Shoulder Score (KSS), and long head of the biceps (LHB) score at least 2 years after surgery. The anatomic status of tenodesis was estimated using magnetic resonance imaging or ultrasonography, and the anatomic failure of tenodesis was determined when the biceps tendon was not traced in the intertubercular groove directly from the insertion site of the IS or SA.

RESULTS: Thirty-three patients in the IS group and 34 in the SA group were monitored for more than 2 years. Postoperative functional scores were not significantly different between the groups, including the LHB score (all P > .05). Nine anatomic failures of tenodesis were observed: 7 in the IS group and 2 in the SA group (P = .083). IS fixation (P = .003) and a higher work level (P = .022) were factors associated with the anatomic failure of tenodesis significantly.

CONCLUSION: The treatment with IS fixation was not more effective than SA fixation in patients with SLBC lesions undergoing arthroscopic rotator cuff repair, as there was no significant difference in functional outcomes between the groups.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is like a ball and socket, and the muscles and tendons around it help you lift and move your arm.  Sometimes, those muscles and tendons (like the one called the biceps) get injured.

Doctors tried two different ways to fix these injuries in 80 grown-ups: using a tiny screw (interference screw or IS) or a tiny anchor (suture anchor or SA).  They wanted to see which way worked better.

They checked how well people could move their arms and how much pain they had after the surgery.  They also used special pictures to see if the repair held together properly.

The results showed that both ways of fixing the injury worked about the same.  People in both groups could move their arms equally well and had similar amounts of pain.  The screw method had slightly more problems with the repair not holding, but the difference wasn't huge.

Title: A Prospective Randomized Study Comparing the Interference Screw and Suture Anchor Techniques for Biceps Tenodesis.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine

abstract:
BACKGROUND: Several methods are used to perform biceps tenodesis in patients with superior labrum-biceps complex (SLBC) lesions accompanied by a rotator cuff tear. However, limited clinical data are available regarding the best technique in terms of clinical and anatomic outcomes.

PURPOSE: To compare the clinical and anatomic outcomes of the interference screw (IS) and suture anchor (SA) fixation techniques for biceps tenodesis performed along with arthroscopic rotator cuff repair.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 2.

METHODS: A total of 80 patients who underwent arthroscopic rotator cuff repair with SLBC lesions were prospectively enrolled and randomly divided according to the tenodesis method: the IS and SA groups. Functional outcomes were evaluated with the visual analog scale (VAS) for pain, American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons (ASES) score, Simple Shoulder Test (SST), Constant score, Korean Shoulder Score (KSS), and long head of the biceps (LHB) score at least 2 years after surgery. The anatomic status of tenodesis was estimated using magnetic resonance imaging or ultrasonography, and the anatomic failure of tenodesis was determined when the biceps tendon was not traced in the intertubercular groove directly from the insertion site of the IS or SA.

RESULTS: Thirty-three patients in the IS group and 34 in the SA group were monitored for more than 2 years. All postoperative functional scores improved significantly compared with the preoperative scores (all P < .001) and were not significantly different between the groups, including the LHB score (all P > .05). Nine anatomic failures of tenodesis were observed: 7 in the IS group and 2 in the SA group ( P = .083). In a multivariate analysis using logistic regression, IS fixation ( P = .003) and a higher (ie, more physically demanding) work level ( P = .022) were factors associated with the anatomic failure of tenodesis significantly. In patients with tenodesis failure, the LHB score ( P = .049) and the degree of Popeye deformity by the patient and examiner ( P = .004 and .018, respectively) were statistically different compared with patients with intact tenodeses.

CONCLUSION: Care must be taken while performing biceps tenodesis in patients with a higher work level; IS fixation appears to pose a higher risk in terms of the anatomic failure of tenodesis than SA fixation, although functional outcomes were not different.

Title: A Multicenter Study of Early Anti-inflammatory Treatment in Patients With Acute Anterior Cruciate Ligament Tear.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine

abstract:
BACKGROUND: Biochemical abnormalities of the joint can precede radiographic abnormalities of posttraumatic osteoarthritis (PTOA) by decades. The progression from anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) injury to PTOA is multifactorial, involving biomechanical disturbances and biochemical homeostasis of articular cartilage.

PURPOSE: The purpose of this randomized study using an acute ACL injury model was to determine if postinjury arthrocentesis and corticosteroid injection can alter the biochemical cascade.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 2.

METHODS: A total of 49 patients were randomized to 4 groups: group 1 (corticosteroid at 4 days after ACL injury, placebo injection of saline at 2 weeks), group 2 (placebo at 4 days after ACL injury, corticosteroid at 2 weeks), group 3 (corticosteroid at both time intervals), or a placebo group (saline injections at both time intervals). Patient-reported outcome measures and synovial biomarkers were collected at approximately 4 days, 11 days, and 5 weeks after injury. The change between the time points was assessed for all variables using Wilcoxon tests, and the relationship between changes in outcome scores and biomarkers were assessed by calculating Spearman ρ. Outcomes and biomarkers were also compared between the 4 groups using Kruskal-Wallis tests.

RESULTS: No adverse events or infections were observed in any study patients. Chondrodegenerative markers worsened over the first 5 weeks while all patient-reported outcomes improved during this time, regardless of treatment group. Patient-reported outcomes did not differ between patients receiving corticosteroid injections and the placebo group. Increases in C-telopeptide of type II collagen (CTX-II), associated with collagen type II breakdown, were significantly greater in the placebo group (1.32 ± 1.10 ng/mL) than in either of the groups that received the corticosteroid injection within the first several days after injury (group 1: 0.23 ± 0.27 ng/mL [P = .01]; group 3: 0.19 ± 0.34 ng/mL [P = .01]).

CONCLUSION: The treatment with corticosteroid was not more effective than placebo in patients with ACL injury.

Scientists wanted to see if giving people a special shot (corticosteroid) right after the injury could help prevent more damage to the knee joint. They divided people into groups: some got the shot early, some got it later, some got a fake shot (placebo), and some got no shot at all.

They checked the people's knees at different times to see how much damage there was.  Surprisingly, even though the people felt better, the special shot didn't actually stop the tiny parts of the knee from getting worse.  The people who got the shot early did a little better than those who didn't, but it wasn't a huge difference.

So, the study showed that this special shot might not be the best way to fix a hurt knee ligament.  It helped people feel better, but it didn't stop the tiny parts of the knee from getting damaged over time.

Title: A Multicenter Study of Early Anti-inflammatory Treatment in Patients With Acute Anterior Cruciate Ligament Tear.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine

abstract:
BACKGROUND: It is increasingly recognized that biochemical abnormalities of the joint precede radiographic abnormalities of posttraumatic osteoarthritis (PTOA) by as much as decades. A growing body of evidence strongly suggests that the progression from anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) injury to PTOA is multifactorial, involving the interplay between biomechanical disturbances and biochemical homeostasis of articular cartilage.

PURPOSE: The purposes of this randomized study using an acute ACL injury model were to (1) evaluate the natural progression of inflammatory and chondrodegenerative biomarkers, (2) evaluate the relationship between subjective reports of pain and inflammatory and chondrodegenerative biomarkers, and (3) determine if postinjury arthrocentesis and corticosteroid injection offer the ability to alter this biochemical cascade.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 2.

METHODS: A total of 49 patients were randomized to 4 groups: group 1 (corticosteroid at 4 days after ACL injury, placebo injection of saline at 2 weeks), group 2 (placebo at 4 days after ACL injury, corticosteroid at 2 weeks), group 3 (corticosteroid at both time intervals), or a placebo group (saline injections at both time intervals). Patient-reported outcome measures and synovial biomarkers were collected at approximately 4 days, 11 days, and 5 weeks after injury. The change between the time points was assessed for all variables using Wilcoxon tests, and the relationship between changes in outcome scores and biomarkers were assessed by calculating Spearman ПЃ. Outcomes and biomarkers were also compared between the 4 groups using Kruskal-Wallis tests.

RESULTS: No adverse events or infections were observed in any study patients. With the exception of matrix metalloproteinase 1 (MMP-1) and tumor necrosis factor-inducible gene 6 (TSG-6), chondrodegenerative markers worsened over the first 5 weeks while all patient-reported outcomes improved during this time, regardless of treatment group. Patient-reported outcomes did not differ between patients receiving corticosteroid injections and the placebo group. However, increases in C-telopeptide of type II collagen (CTX-II), associated with collagen type II breakdown, were significantly greater in the placebo group (1.32 В± 1.10 ng/mL) than in either of the groups that received the corticosteroid injection within the first several days after injury (group 1: 0.23 В± 0.27 ng/mL [ P = .01]; group 3: 0.19 В± 0.34 ng/mL [ P = .01]).

CONCLUSION: PTOA begins at the time of injury and results early on in dramatic matrix changes in the knee. However, it is encouraging that early intervention with an anti-inflammatory agent was able to affect biomarkers of chondral degeneration. Should early intervention lead to meaningful changes in either the onset or severity of symptomatic PTOA, the current treatment paradigm for patients with ACL injury may have to be restructured to include early aspiration and intra-articular intervention.

Title: High-flow warm humidified oxygen versus standard low-flow nasal cannula oxygen for moderate bronchiolitis (HFWHO RCT): an open, phase 4, randomised controlled trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Focus on another objective (e.g., trial is (-) but they say they accomplish some goal that they did not prespecify)
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Bronchiolitis is the most common lung infection in infants, and treatment focuses on management of respiratory distress and hypoxia. High-flow warm humidified oxygen (HFWHO) is increasingly used, but has not been rigorously studied in randomized trials. We aimed to examine whether HFWHO shortened time to weaning off oxygen.

METHODS: In this open, phase 4, randomized controlled trial, we recruited children aged less than 24 months with moderate bronchiolitis attending the emergency department of the John Hunter Hospital or the medical unit of the John Hunter Children's Hospital in New South Wales, Australia. Patients were randomly allocated (1:1) via opaque sealed envelopes to HFWHO (maximum flow of 1 L/kg per min to a limit of 20 L/min using 1:1 air-oxygen ratio, resulting in a maximum FiO2 of 0.6) or standard therapy (cold wall oxygen 100% via infant nasal cannulae at low flow to a maximum of 2 L/min) using a block size of four and stratifying for gestational age at birth. The primary outcome was time from randomization to last use of oxygen therapy. All randomized children were included in the primary and secondary safety analyses. This trial is registered with the Australian New Zealand Clinical Trials Registry, number ACTRN12612000685819.FINDINGS: From July 16, 2012, to May 1, 2015, we randomly assigned 202 children to either HFWHO (101 children) or standard therapy (101 children). Median time to weaning was 24 h (95% CI 18-28) for standard therapy and 20 h (95% CI 17-34) for HFWHO (hazard ratio [HR] for difference in survival distributions 0.9 [95% CI 0.7-1.2]; log rank p=0.61). 12 (12%) of children on standard therapy required transfer to the intensive care unit compared with 14 (14%) of those on HFWHO (difference -1%; 95% CI -7 to 16; p=0.41). Four adverse events occurred (oxygen desaturation and condensation inhalation in the HFWHO group, and two incidences of oxygen tubing disconnection in the standard therapy group); none resulted in withdrawal from the trial. No oxygen-related serious adverse events occurred.

INTERPRETATION: The treatment HFWHO was not more effective than standard therapy in patients with moderate bronchiolitis.

This study was trying to figure out if giving oxygen in a special warm, humid way (HFWHO) was better than the usual way (standard therapy).  They looked at 202 babies with this cold.  They split the babies into two groups: one got the special oxygen, and the other got the usual oxygen.

They wanted to see which group got better and needed the oxygen for a shorter time.  Guess what?  It turned out there wasn't much difference! Both groups got better around the same time.  The special oxygen didn't help them get better faster.  Both groups also had about the same number of problems.

So, the study showed that the special way of giving oxygen isn't better than the regular way for babies with this type of cold.

Title: A Randomised tRial of Expedited transfer to a cardiac arrest centre for non-ST elevation ventricular fibrillation out-of-hospital cardiac arrest: The ARREST pilot randomised trial.

abstract:
BACKGROUND: Wide variation exists in inter-hospital survival from out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA). Regionalisation of care into cardiac arrest centres (CAC) may improve this. We report a pilot randomised trial of expedited transfer to a CAC following OHCA without ST-elevation.

METHODS: Adult witnessed ventricular fibrillation OHCA of presumed cardiac cause were randomised 1:1 to either: (1) treatment: comprising expedited transfer to a CAC for goal-directed therapy including access to immediate reperfusion, or (2) control: comprising current standard of care involving delivery to the geographically closest hospital. The primary outcome was 30-day all-cause mortality. Secondary outcomes included cerebral performance category (CPC) and in-hospital major cardiovascular and cerebrovascular events (MACCE).

RESULTS: Between November 2014 and April 2016, 118 cases were screened, of which 63 patients (53%) met eligibility criteria and 40 of the 63 patients (63%) were randomised. There were no protocol deviations in the treatment arm. Data collection of primary and secondary outcomes was achieved in 83%. There was no difference in baseline characteristics between the groups: 30-day mortality (Intervention 9/18, 50% vs. Control 6/15, 40%; P=0.73), CPC 1/2 (Intervention: 9/18, 50% vs. Control 7/14, 50%; P>0.99) or MACCE (Intervention: 9/18, 50% vs. Control 6/15, 40%; P=0.73).CONCLUSIONS: The treatment was not more effective than the comparator in patients with OHCA in terms of 30-day all-cause mortality.

They followed these people for a month to see who survived.  Surprisingly, it didn't matter which hospital they went to – about half the people in each group survived.  So, sending people to the special hospital didn't seem to make a difference in how many people lived.

Title: Comparison of the effects of diets high in animal or plant protein on metabolic and cardiovascular markers in type 2 diabetes: A randomized clinical trial.
Spin in Abstract Title/Results: RESULTS: Other; Focus on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
Aim: To compare high animal protein (AP) with high plant protein (PP) diets, differing in amino acid composition, in people with type 2 diabetes (T2DM).

Materials and methods: We compared isocaloric diets containing 30% of energy either as AP or PP, using newly developed PP-enriched foods, both combined with 30% energy as fat and 40% as carbohydrates in 44 patients with T2DM over 6 weeks in a randomized parallel-group study. Insulin sensitivity was assessed by hyperinsulinaemic-euglycaemic clamps and cardiovascular variables were measured.

Results: Uric acid decreased in both groups, but significantly more in the AP than the PP group. There were no significant differences in other variables, although glycated haemoglobin levels, diastolic blood pressure and fasting non-esterified fatty acid levels improved significantly in the PP but not in the AP group. Insulin sensitivity (M-value), C-reactive protein and fasting glucose improved significantly in the AP but not in the PP group. Total and LDL cholesterol levels and systolic blood pressure decreased significantly in both groups, and the urinary albumin excretion rate decreased from baseline in participants with microalbuminuria.

Conclusions: Isocaloric diets high in AP or PP allow similar improvements in metabolism and cardiovascular risk factors in people with T2DM, indicating that the differences in amino acid composition do not affect the metabolic responses to the interventions.

Imagine you're testing two different diets for people with a type of diabetes.  One diet has lots of protein from animals (like meat and eggs), and the other has lots of protein from plants (like beans and lentils).  Both diets had the same number of calories.

Scientists gave these diets to people with diabetes for six weeks and checked their health.  They found that both diets helped improve some things, like blood pressure and cholesterol.  However, some things improved more with one diet than the other.  For example, one thing got better more with the animal protein diet, and another got better more with the plant protein diet.

The important thing is that *both* diets helped people with diabetes get healthier, even though the proteins came from different sources.  So, whether you get your protein from animals or plants might not matter as much as just eating enough protein overall.

Title: Contact Kinematic Differences Between Gap Balanced vs Measured Resection Techniques for Single Radius Posterior-Stabilized Total Knee Arthroplasty.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage, Claim benefit based on (+) subgroup analysis
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Measured resection (MR) and gap balancing (GB) are common surgical techniques for total knee arthroplasty (TKA). The objective of the present study was to compare both the frequency of condylar liftoff and the location of femorotibial contact from extension through midflexion between patients undergoing GB or MR TKA.

METHODS: A total of 24 knees (23 patients) were randomly assigned at referral to either a surgeon performing MR or GB TKA with the same single radius, posterior-stabilized implant (12 per cohort). At 1-year postoperation, patients underwent biplanar radiographic imaging at 0°, 20°, 40°, and 60° of flexion. Condylar liftoff, contact location, and magnitude of excursion on each condyle were measured.

RESULTS: There was no difference (P = .41) in the frequency of liftoff between cohorts. The MR cohort had more posterior contact on the medial condyle (P < .01) and more anterior contact on the lateral condyle (P < .01) throughout flexion. Motion patterns were similar between cohorts, with similar medial (P = .48) and lateral (P = .44) excursion, which was equal in magnitude between condyles for both MR (P = .48) and GB (P = .73). There was no difference in clinical outcome scores between groups.

CONCLUSION: The treatment MR was not more effective than GB in patients with total knee arthroplasty.

This study looked at 24 knees to see if one method was better than the other.  They took X-rays of the knees at different angles to see how the new hinge moved.

They found that both methods worked about the same.  The way the new hinge touched the bone was slightly different between the two methods, but it didn't affect how well the knee worked overall.  Both methods helped the knees move just as well.  So, neither method was better than the other.

They found that both ways worked pretty much the same!  The knees moved similarly, and the patients felt equally good afterwards.  So, for this type of knee replacement, it doesn't seem to matter which way the doctor uses.

Title: Initiation of an Inhaled Corticosteroid During a Pediatric Emergency Visit for Asthma: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
STUDY OBJECTIVE: We determine whether prescribing an inhaled corticosteroid during a pediatric emergency department (ED) asthma visit increases ongoing use and improves outcomes.

METHODS: This randomized trial enrolled children aged 1 to 18 years, with persistent asthma not previously prescribed a controller medication, and who were being discharged after ED asthma treatment. Intervention subjects received a 1-month prescription for an inhaled corticosteroid (fluticasone or budesonide by age) in addition to standard asthma therapy and instructions given to all patients. Outcomes included filling of the intervention and subsequent inhaled corticosteroid prescriptions, asthma-related symptoms and quality of life, and follow-up rates with a primary care provider. Outcomes were assessed during telephone interviews 2 and 8 weeks after the ED visit and by review of primary care provider and pharmacy records.

RESULTS: One hundred forty-seven children were enrolled, and baseline measures were similar between groups. In the intervention group, 53.5% of patients filled an initial ED prescription for inhaled corticosteroid. There was no important difference between groups in subsequent filling of a primary care provider prescription (21% intervention versus 17% control; relative rate=1.24; 95% confidence interval 0.63 to 2.41). During the 2 weeks after the ED visit, intervention subjects reported reduced shortness of breath while awake and cough while asleep compared with controls. Groups did not differ by rates of primary care provider follow-up, functional limitations, or asthma-related symptoms and quality of life.

CONCLUSION: There was no difference in the proportion of patients who filled a primary care provider prescription after ED initiation of an inhaled corticosteroid. The intervention was associated with reduced reported symptoms but did not affect other asthma outcomes or primary care provider follow-up.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine a group of kids with asthma who went to the emergency room because their asthma was acting up.  Doctors wanted to see if giving them a special asthma inhaler (medicine) right then in the ER would help them in the long run.

They split the kids into two groups: one group got the special inhaler along with their usual asthma treatment, and the other group only got the usual treatment.  Then, the doctors checked in with the kids later to see how they were doing.

They found that only about half the kids in the group that got the special inhaler actually used it.  And surprisingly, whether or not they used the special inhaler didn't really make a big difference in how well they felt overall, except that the kids who *did* use the special inhaler reported feeling a little better (less shortness of breath and coughing) for a couple of weeks.  But after that, both groups were about the same.

So, giving the special inhaler in the ER didn't really make a huge difference in the long-term asthma control for these kids.  It helped a little bit in the short term for some, but not enough to say it's a great idea for everyone.

Title: The Use of Unidirectional Knotless Barbed Suture for Enterotomy Closure in Roux-en-Y Gastric Bypass: a Randomized Comparative Study.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other;Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
PURPOSE: In this study, we assessed feasibility, safety, and time efficiency of laparoscopic running enterotomy closure for linear stapled Roux-en-Y Gastric Bypass (RYGB) using unidirectional barbed sutures (Stratafixв„ў 2/0- Ethicon).MATERIALS AND 

METHODS: Two hundred patients undergoing laparoscopic RYGB were prospectively randomized regarding running enterotomy closure of the linear stapled gastrojejunal (GJA) and jejunojejunal anastomosis (JJA). Two groups were created: V-group (VicrylВ® 2/0-Ethicon) and S-group (Stratafixв„ў 2/0-Ethicon). Time spent on closing the enterotomies was measured from first needle in until knot and cut (V-group) or last stitch and cut (S-group). If needed, a nonabsorbable "correction" ("c"; in order to close a small hiatus at the anastomosis) or hemostatic ("h") stitch was made (using a single ProleneВ® 2/0-Ethicon).

RESULTS: Average total procedure time was similar (S-group 1:01:22, V-group 1:00:44, PВ =В 0.340). Closure of the enterotomy (GJA) was significantly shorter in the S-group (07:41В min versus 08:13В min in the V-group, PВ =В 0.005). Extra stitches (GJA) were performed in 33 patients (16.5%): 3 (h) and 20 (c) in the V-group and 1 (h) and 9 (c) in the S-group. Four patients in the V-group suffered from postoperative intraluminal bleeding (3 self-limiting, 1 underwent endoscopic clipping). In the S-group, 1 patient suffered from leakage at the vertical transected staple line of the stomach.CONCLUSIONS: The use of unidirectional barbed sutures for running enterotomy closure after linear stapled RYGB is feasible and safe. Significant time benefit was seen regarding the closure of the GJA. Fewer additional stitches were necessary and postoperative intraluminal bleeding was less encountered.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your intestines are like a long tube.  Sometimes, doctors need to cut and then sew this tube back together during a type of stomach surgery called RYGB.

This study was to see if a special type of stitch (like a magical, self-catching thread!) called Stratafix was better than a regular stitch (Vicryl) for sewing up these cuts.  They did the surgery on 200 people, using the special stitch on half and the regular stitch on the other half.

They timed how long it took to sew the cuts.  The special stitch was faster! It also needed fewer extra stitches to fix any little holes.  And there was less bleeding after surgery with the special stitch.

So, the study showed that the special stitch is a good, safe, and faster way to sew up the intestines during this type of surgery.

Title: Collagen Augmentation Improves the Quality of Cartilage Repair After Microfracture in Patients Undergoing High Tibial Osteotomy: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: The quality of cartilage repair after marrow stimulation is unpredictable. To overcome the shortcomings of the microfracture technique, various augmentation techniques have been developed. However, their efficacies remain unclear.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 2.

METHODS: Twenty-eight patients undergoing high tibial osteotomy (HTO) were randomized into 2 groups: microfracture alone (group 1, n = 14) or microfracture with collagen augmentation (group 2, n = 14). At 1 year postoperatively, second-look arthroscopic surgery and biopsy of repaired cartilage were performed at the time of HTO plate removal. Biopsy specimens were graded using the International Cartilage Repair Society Visual Assessment Scale II (ICRS II). Imaging outcomes in terms of the magnetic resonance observation of cartilage repair tissue (MOCART) score were assessed based on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Clinical outcomes in terms of the visual analog scale (VAS) for pain score, Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS), International Knee Documentation Committee (IKDC) score, and Tegner activity scale score were evaluated.

RESULTS: The mean ICRS II score in group 2 was higher than that in group 1 (1053.2 vs 885.4, respectively; P = .002). Imaging outcomes based on the MOCART score were higher in group 2 compared to those in group 1 on MRI at 1 year postoperatively (64.6 vs 45.4, respectively; P = .001). The degree of defect repair was better in group 2 than in group 1 (P = .040). Clinical outcomes in terms of the VAS for pain score, KOOS, IKDC score, and Tegner activity scale score were improved in both groups without between-group differences (P > .100 for all comparisons).

CONCLUSION: The treatment with microfracture and collagen augmentation was not more effective than microfracture alone in patients with medial compartment osteoarthritis of the knee in terms of clinical outcomes after 1 year.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is like a bouncy ball.  Sometimes, the bouncy part (cartilage) gets damaged. Doctors have a way to fix it called "microfracture," like patching a hole.  But sometimes the patch doesn't work perfectly.

This study tried a new way to patch the hole, adding something called "collagen" to make the patch stronger.  They compared two groups of people: one group got just the microfracture, and the other got the microfracture *plus* the collagen.

After a year, they looked at the knees using special cameras and scans.  The knees with the collagen patch looked better on the scans (like a better-looking bouncy ball).  But surprisingly, both groups felt about the same – no big difference in how well they could move or how much pain they had.

So, even though the collagen patch looked better on the scans, it didn't actually make the knee work any better in real life.  The study showed that adding collagen didn't make a big difference in how well the knee worked overall.

Title: Collagen Augmentation Improves the Quality of Cartilage Repair After Microfracture in Patients Undergoing High Tibial Osteotomy: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine

abstract:
BACKGROUND: The quality of cartilage repair after marrow stimulation is unpredictable. To overcome the shortcomings of the microfracture technique, various augmentation techniques have been developed. However, their efficacies remain unclear.

HYPOTHESIS: The quality of cartilage repair and clinical outcomes would be superior in patients undergoing high tibial osteotomy (HTO) with microfracture and collagen augmentation compared to those undergoing HTO with microfracture alone without collagen augmentation for the treatment of medial compartment osteoarthritis (OA) of the knee.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 2.

METHODS: Twenty-eight patients undergoing HTO were randomized into 2 groups: microfracture alone (group 1, n = 14) or microfracture with collagen augmentation (group 2, n = 14). At 1 year postoperatively, second-look arthroscopic surgery and biopsy of repaired cartilage were performed at the time of HTO plate removal. Biopsy specimens were graded using the International Cartilage Repair Society Visual Assessment Scale II (ICRS II). In addition, imaging outcomes in terms of the magnetic resonance observation of cartilage repair tissue (MOCART) score were assessed based on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Finally, clinical outcomes in terms of the visual analog scale (VAS) for pain score, Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS), International Knee Documentation Committee (IKDC) score, and Tegner activity scale score were evaluated.

RESULTS: The mean ICRS II score in group 2 was significantly higher than that in group 1 (1053.2 vs 885.4, respectively; P = .002). Group 2 showed greater improvement in tissue morphology, cell morphology, surface architecture, middle/deep zone assessment, and overall assessment compared with group 1 ( P < .050 for all comparisons). Imaging outcomes based on the MOCART score were superior in group 2 compared to those in group 1 on MRI at 1 year postoperatively (64.6 vs 45.4, respectively; P = .001). The degree of defect repair was better in group 2 than in group 1 ( P = .040). Clinical outcomes in terms of the VAS for pain score, KOOS, IKDC score, and Tegner activity scale score were improved in both groups without between-group differences ( P > .100 for all comparisons).

CONCLUSION: The quality of cartilage repair after microfracture with collagen augmentation was superior to that after microfracture alone in patients undergoing HTO. Clinical results after 1 year did not reflect this difference in tissue repair. Therefore, a longer follow-up of the cohort is needed to answer this question.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is like a bouncy ball, but the bouncy part (cartilage) is getting worn out.  Doctors have a way to fix it called "microfracture," like poking tiny holes to help it heal.  But sometimes it doesn't heal perfectly.

This study tried a new way to help the healing: adding something called "collagen" (like extra glue) to the microfracture.  They compared two groups of people: one group got just microfracture, and the other got microfracture *plus* collagen.

After a year, they looked at the knees using special cameras and scans.  The group that got the collagen had much better healing of the bouncy part (cartilage) in their knees.  The scans showed better repair.

However, surprisingly, both groups felt about the same – no big difference in how well they could move or how much pain they had.

The doctors think that maybe if they waited longer than a year to check, they might see a bigger difference in how the people felt.  So, they need to keep watching these people to see if the better healing leads to better feeling knees in the long run.

Title: A Prospective, Randomized Trial Comparing Liposomal Bupivacaine vs Fascia Iliaca Compartment Block for Postoperative Pain Control in Total Hip Arthroplasty.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Increasing demand for total hip arthroplasty (THA) in a climate of increasing focus on clinical outcomes, patient satisfaction, and cost has created a need for better acute postoperative pain control for patients. An ideal pain control method would have few side effects, decreased opioid consumption, improved pain control, early ambulation, and decreased hospital length of stay (LOS).

METHODS: We performed a prospective randomized, controlled study involving 79 patients undergoing elective THA between June 2015 and February 2016. Forty patients received liposomal bupivacaine and 39 patients received a fascia iliaca compartment block (FICB). In addition, the medical records of 28 patients who underwent elective THA between May 2015 and December 2015 were retrospectively examined. The primary outcome was visual analog scale pain scores and the secondary outcomes were LOS and total opioid consumption. SPSS, version 22, was used to run 1-way analysis of variance with contrast and Mood's median test on the data.

RESULTS: There were statistically significant decreases in pain intensity (PВ = .019) and LOS (PВ = .041) in both the liposomal bupivacaine group and the FICB group compared with those in the retrospective control group. In addition, only the FICB group showed statistically significant decreased total opioid consumption compared with that in the retrospective group (PВ = .028).

CONCLUSION: Patients undergoing elective THA have decreased overall pain intensity and a shorter LOS with multimodal pain management regimen that includes either liposomal bupivacaine or FICB. Patients who received FICB required less overall total opioids than the control group.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your grandma needs a new hip joint.  Doctors are always trying to find better ways to help people feel less pain after this kind of surgery.

This study looked at two different ways to manage pain after hip surgery:  one was a special medicine called liposomal bupivacaine, and the other was a type of injection called a fascia iliaca compartment block (FICB).  They compared these to a group of people who had the same surgery earlier and had different pain management.

The study found that *both* the special medicine and the injection helped people feel less pain and go home from the hospital sooner.  The injection was even better at helping people need less strong pain medicine.

So, the scientists learned that these new ways of managing pain after hip surgery are good because they help people feel better and get better faster!

Title: Hyaluronic Acid Versus Platelet-Rich Plasma: A Prospective, Double-Blind Randomized Controlled Trial Comparing Clinical Outcomes and Effects on Intra-articular Biology for the Treatment of Knee Osteoarthritis
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
Spin in Abstract Title/Results: Focus on (+) within or between group comparison of secondary endpoints
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage
abstract:
Background: The use of platelet-rich plasma (PRP) for the treatment of osteoarthritis (OA) has demonstrated mixed clinical outcomes in randomized controlled trials when compared with hyaluronic acid (HA), an accepted nonsurgical treatment for symptomatic OA.

Purpose: To compare the clinical effects of an intra-articular injection of PRP with those of an intra-articular injection of HA in patients with mild to moderate knee OA.

Study design: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 1.

Methods: A total of 111 patients with symptomatic unilateral knee OA received a series of either leukocyte-poor PRP or HA injections under ultrasound guidance. Clinical data were collected before treatment and at 4 time points across a 1-year period. The primary outcome measure was the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index (WOMAC) pain subscale. Secondary outcomes included the International Knee Documentation Committee (IKDC) subjective knee evaluation, visual analog scale (VAS) for pain, and Lysholm knee score. Synovial fluid was also collected for analysis of proinflammatory and anti-inflammatory markers before treatment and at 12 and 24 weeks after treatment.

Results: There were 49 patients randomized to treatment with PRP and 50 randomized to treatment with HA. No difference was seen between the groups in the primary outcome measure (WOMAC pain score). In the secondary outcome measures, a significantly higher IKDC score was observed in the PRP group compared with the HA group at 24 weeks (mean ± standard error [SE], 65.5 ± 3.6 vs 55.8 ± 3.8, respectively; P = .013) and at final follow-up (52 weeks) (57.6 ± 3.37 vs 46.6 ± 3.76, respectively; P = .003). A statistically lower VAS score was also noted in the PRP group versus the HA group at 24 weeks (mean ± SE, 34.6 ± 3.24 vs 48.6 ± 3.7, respectively; P = .0096) and 52 weeks (44 ± 4.6 vs 57.3 ± 3.8, respectively; P = .0039). In the biochemical analysis, differences between groups approached significance for interleukin-1_ (mean ± SE, 0.14 ± 0.05 pg/mL [PRP] vs 0.34 ± 0.16 pg/mL [HA]; P = .06) and tumor necrosis factor _ (0.08 ± 0.01 pg/mL [PRP] vs 0.2 ± 0.18 pg/mL [HA]; P = .068) at 12-week follow-up.

Conclusion: The treatment with PRP was not more effective than HA in patients with knee OA in terms of the primary outcome measure, the patient-reported WOMAC pain score.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee hurts because of arthritis (worn-out knee joints).  Doctors sometimes use shots to help.  Two common shots are PRP (like a super-charged blood shot) and HA (another type of shot).

Scientists wanted to see which shot worked better. They gave one shot to some people and the other shot to others.  They checked how much pain the people had before and after the shots.

They found that both shots helped a little, but the PRP shot seemed to help people feel *better* overall (less pain and better knee function) after a few months, even though it didn't reduce pain as measured by one specific test.  It wasn't a huge difference, but it was noticeable.  So, while neither shot completely fixed the problem, the PRP shot might be slightly better for some people.

Title: Hyaluronic Acid Versus Platelet-Rich Plasma: A Prospective, Double-Blind Randomized Controlled Trial Comparing Clinical Outcomes and Effects on Intra-articular Biology for the Treatment of Knee Osteoarthritis
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine

abstract:
Background: The use of platelet-rich plasma (PRP) for the treatment of osteoarthritis (OA) has demonstrated mixed clinical outcomes in randomized controlled trials when compared with hyaluronic acid (HA), an accepted nonsurgical treatment for symptomatic OA. Biological analysis of PRP has demonstrated an anti-inflammatory effect on the intra-articular environment.

Purpose: To compare the clinical and biological effects of an intra-articular injection of PRP with those of an intra-articular injection of HA in patients with mild to moderate knee OA.

Study design: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 1.

Methods: A total of 111 patients with symptomatic unilateral knee OA received a series of either leukocyte-poor PRP or HA injections under ultrasound guidance. Clinical data were collected before treatment and at 4 time points across a 1-year period. Synovial fluid was also collected for analysis of proinflammatory and anti-inflammatory markers before treatment and at 12 and 24 weeks after treatment. Several measures were used to assess results: (1) Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index (WOMAC) pain subscale; (2) International Knee Documentation Committee (IKDC) subjective knee evaluation, visual analog scale (VAS) for pain, and Lysholm knee score; and (3) difference in intra-articular biochemical marker concentrations.

Results: There were 49 patients randomized to treatment with PRP and 50 randomized to treatment with HA. No difference was seen between the groups in the primary outcome measure (WOMAC pain score). In the secondary outcome measure, linear contrasts identified a significantly higher IKDC score in the PRP group compared with the HA group at 24 weeks (mean ± standard error [SE], 65.5 ± 3.6 vs 55.8 ± 3.8, respectively; P = .013) and at final follow-up (52 weeks) (57.6 ± 3.37 vs 46.6 ± 3.76, respectively; P = .003). Linear contrasts also identified a statistically lower VAS score in the PRP group versus the HA group at 24 weeks (mean ± SE, 34.6 ± 3.24 vs 48.6 ± 3.7, respectively; P = .0096) and 52 weeks (44 ± 4.6 vs 57.3 ± 3.8, respectively; P = .0039). An examination of fixed effects showed that patients with mild OA and a lower body mass index had a statistically significant improvement in outcomes. In the biochemical analysis, differences between groups approached significance for interleukin-1_ (mean ± SE, 0.14 ± 0.05 pg/mL [PRP] vs 0.34 ± 0.16 pg/mL [HA]; P = .06) and tumor necrosis factor _ (0.08 ± 0.01 pg/mL [PRP] vs 0.2 ± 0.18 pg/mL [HA]; P = .068) at 12-week follow-up.

Conclusion: We found no difference between HA and PRP at any time point in the primary outcome measure: the patient-reported WOMAC pain score. Significant improvements were seen in other patient-reported outcome measures, with results favoring PRP over HA. Preceding a significant difference in subjective outcomes favoring PRP, there was a trend toward a decrease in 2 proinflammatory cytokines, which suggest that the anti-inflammatory properties of PRP may contribute to an improvement of symptoms.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee hurts because of arthritis (like old, worn-out parts in a machine).  Doctors sometimes use injections to help.  Two common injections are PRP (like your body's own healing juice) and HA (a different type of medicine).

Scientists wanted to see which injection worked better. They gave one to some people and the other to others.  They checked how much the knee hurt using different ways to measure pain and how well the knee worked.

They found that while the main pain score didn't show a big difference, people who got the PRP injection said their knees felt better and worked better after a while.  The PRP also seemed to reduce some of the stuff in the knee that causes inflammation (swelling and pain).

So, even though the main pain test didn't show a clear winner, the PRP injection seemed to help people feel better overall.  It's like one test said "no difference," but other tests showed PRP was better.

Title: Efficacy of environmental and acceptance-based enhancements to behavioral weight loss treatment: The ENACT trial.
abstract:
OBJECTIVE: This study was designed to compare weight loss through a traditional behavioral treatment (BT) approach that integrated skills for managing the obesogenic food environment (BT + E) with an approach that integrated environmental and acceptance-based skills (BT + EA).

METHODS: Adults (N = 283) were randomly assigned to treatment condition and provided with 26 group-based sessions over the course of 12 months. Weight was measured in the clinic at months 0, 6, and 12. The primary outcome was weight change over time.

RESULTS: Change in weight over time did not significantly differ by condition. Safety data, including reasons for withdrawals and treatment discontinuation, were not reported.CONCLUSIONS: The treatment BT + EA was not more effective than BT + E in patients with obesity.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine a science experiment to see what's the best way to help people lose weight.

They had two groups of grown-ups trying to lose weight:

* **Group 1:** Learned healthy habits and how to deal with tempting, unhealthy foods (like avoiding the candy aisle at the store).
* **Group 2:** Learned healthy habits, how to deal with tempting foods, AND how to accept that sometimes they might eat unhealthy things without feeling bad about it.

Both groups met for 26 weeks (that's a long time!).  Scientists weighed everyone at the start, halfway through, and at the end.

**The result?**  Neither group lost significantly more weight than the other.  The scientists didn't even say how many people stopped the program early.

**In short:**  Both ways of helping people lose weight worked about the same.

Title: Efficacy of environmental and acceptance-based enhancements to behavioral weight loss treatment: The ENACT trial.

Moderators were examined as an exploratory aim.

METHODS: Adults (NвЂ‰=вЂ‰283) were randomly assigned to treatment condition and provided with 26 group-based sessions over the course of 12 months. Weight was measured in the clinic at months 0, 6, and 12.

RESULTS: Change in weight over time did not significantly differ by condition. However, race significantly moderated the effect of condition on weight loss (PвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.04), such that African-American participants lost less weight than non-Hispanic white participants in the BT (6.2% vs. 11.5%) and BTвЂ‰+вЂ‰E conditions (6.6% vs. 12.2%), but weight loss in these two groups was similar in the BTвЂ‰+вЂ‰EA condition (9.4% vs. 11.5%). Among African Americans, rates of achieving a clinically significant weight loss (i.e.,вЂ‰>вЂ‰5%) at 12 months were higher in BTвЂ‰+вЂ‰EA (80%) than BT (57%) or BTвЂ‰+вЂ‰E (48%) (PвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.04).CONCLUSIONS: This innovative behavioral approach shows promise for treatment of African Americans, which is notable given the lack of progress to date addressing racial disparities in obesity intervention efficacy.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine a science experiment to see what helps people lose weight.  They had three different weight-loss programs:

* **Program 1 (BT):**  A regular weight-loss program.
* **Program 2 (BT+E):**  A regular program plus tips on dealing with tempting foods.
* **Program 3 (BT+EA):** A regular program plus tips on dealing with tempting foods *and* learning to accept themselves no matter their weight.

They had lots of grown-ups (283!) try these programs for a whole year.  They weighed everyone at the start, six months later, and at the end.

What they found was surprising!  Overall, none of the programs worked better than the others at helping people lose weight.  But, something interesting happened when they looked at the results for different groups of people.

Black people in the study lost less weight than white people in Programs 1 and 2.  But in Program 3, the difference between Black and white people was much smaller.  In fact, Program 3 helped Black people lose weight better than the other two programs.

So, the scientists think Program 3 (the one with tips on food and self-acceptance) might be a really good way to help Black people lose weight, which is important because it's harder for them to lose weight than it is for white people with the other programs.  They need to do more

Title: Innovative approaches to weight loss in a high-risk population: The small changes and lasting effects (SCALE) trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Focus on another objective (e.g., trial is (-) but they say they accomplish some goal that they did not prespecify)
abstract:
Objective: To evaluate the efficacy of a small change behavioral weight loss intervention with or without a positive affect/self-affirmation (PA/SA) component on weight loss at 12 months.

Methods: Black and Hispanic adults (N = 405) with body mass index 25-50 kg/m2 selected one of ten small change eating strategies and a physical activity goal, randomly with/without PA/SA. Participants were followed by community health workers at set intervals (weekly in months 1-3; biweekly in months 4-9; once monthly in months 10-12).

Results: There was no difference in weight loss at 12 months between participants in the small change approach alone (1.1%) versus the small change PA/SA intervention (1.2%). During treatment, 9% of participants lost at least 7% of their initial body weight. Participants who reported more interval life events had a lower likelihood of losing weight (P < 0.0001). However, those randomized to the small change PA/SA intervention gained less weight (+0.3% vs. 2.3% gain; P < 0.0001).

Conclusions: The small change PA/SA intervention did not lead to a significant difference in weight loss in comparison to the small change approach alone. It did, however, decrease the negative impact of psychosocial stressors on weight gain among participants with more interval life events.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine a group of grown-ups trying to lose weight.  They were given a plan to make small, healthy changes to their eating and exercise.

Half the group also got extra help:  they learned to think positively about themselves and feel good (that's the "PA/SA" part).

After a year, scientists checked how much weight everyone lost.  Guess what?  Both groups lost about the same amount of weight – just a tiny bit.

But here's the interesting part:  people who had a lot of stressful things happen to them during the year gained more weight.  However, the people who got the extra positive thinking help didn't gain as much weight when bad things happened to them.

So, the extra positive thinking didn't help people lose *more* weight overall, but it did help protect them from gaining weight when life got tough.

Imagine a group of grown-ups who wanted to lose weight.  Scientists wanted to see if a little extra help would make a difference.

They gave half the grown-ups a plan to make small changes to their eating and exercise (like eating a little less junk food or walking a bit more each day).  The other half got the same plan, but also got extra encouragement and positive messages to help them feel good about themselves.

After a year, the scientists weighed everyone again.  Guess what?  Both groups lost about the same amount of weight!  Adding the extra encouragement didn't make a bigger difference. Title: Arthroscopic Debridement Versus Platelet-Rich Plasma Injection: A Prospective, Randomized, Comparative Study of Chronic Lateral Epicondylitis With a Nearly 2-Year Follow-Up.
Journal: Arthroscopy: The Journal of Arthroscopic & Related Surgery
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
PURPOSE: The purpose of this prospective, randomized study was to compare the efficacy of autologous platelet-richВ plasma (PRP) injections and arthroscopic lateral release in treating chronic lateral epicondylitis (LE).

METHODS: Patients who had a clinical diagnosis of LE confirmed by ultrasound (US) were included in this study. A total of 101 patients received arthroscopic release (nВ = 50) or US-guided PRP injections (nВ = 51). Outcomes were assessed using a visual analog scale for pain, the Patient-Rated Tennis Elbow Evaluation (PRTEE), and a calibrated hand dynamometer for grip strength.

RESULTS: Both patient groups experienced significant improvement in all measures. Between-group comparisons showed a significantly higher value in the PRP group only for grip strength at week 8 (PВ = .0073); all other significant differences were in favor of arthroscopy: overall pain (PВ = .0021), night pain (PВ = .0013), and PRTEE score (PВ =В .0013) at week 104 and grip strength at weeks 24, 52, and 104 (all P < .0001). Consumption of rescue pain medication was not significantly different between the groups.CONCLUSIONS: The present findings suggest that (1) PRP injections and arthroscopic extensor carpi radialis brevis release are both effective in the short and medium term; (2) PRP patients experienced a significant worsening of pain at 2В years; (3) arthroscopic release ensured better long-term outcomes in terms of pain relief and grip strength recovery; and (4) both procedures were safe and well accepted by patients. LEVELВ OF EVIDENCE: Level II, prospective comparative study.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your elbow hurts really badly, like tennis elbow.  Doctors wanted to see which treatment worked best:

* **Treatment 1:**  A shot of your own blood (PRP) to help it heal.
* **Treatment 2:**  A small surgery (arthroscopy) to fix the problem.

They had 101 people with this elbow pain.  About half got the shot, and half got the surgery.  They checked how much pain the people had, how strong their grip was, and how well they could use their arm.

**What they found:**

* Both treatments helped a lot at first.
* The shot helped grip strength a little better in the beginning.
* But after two years, the surgery worked much better for long-term pain relief and grip strength.  The shot actually made some people's pain worse after a while.
* Both treatments were safe.

So, the study showed that surgery is better for fixing this kind of elbow pain in the long run, even though the shot helps a bit at first.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine your elbow hurts a lot, like a really bad tennis elbow that just won't go away.  Doctors wanted to see what's the best way to fix it:

* **Group 1:** Some people got a special shot in their elbow called PRP (it's like using their own blood to help heal).
* **Group 2:** Others had a small surgery called arthroscopic lateral release.

The doctors checked how much pain the kids had, how strong their grip was, and other things.  They found out that:

* **Surgery (Group 2) worked better overall.**  It helped with pain and grip strength much more than the shots.
* **The shots (Group 1) only helped a little bit with grip strength at one point.**

So, for really bad, long-lasting tennis elbow, the surgery seemed to be a better choice than the shots.  Both were safe, though.

Title: Quality of Life 1 Year After Laparoscopic Sleeve Gastrectomy Versus Laparoscopic Roux-en-Y Gastric Bypass: a Randomized Controlled Trial Focusing on Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease
abstract:
Introduction: Bariatric surgery is the only treatment option that achieves sustained weight loss in obese patients and that also has positive effects on obesity-related comorbidities. Laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy (LSG) seems to achieve equal weight loss as laparoscopic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (LRYGB), but there is still much debate about the quality of life (QOL) after LSG, mainly concerning the association with gastroesophageal reflux. Our hypothesis is that QOL after LSG is comparable with QOL after LRYGB.

Materials and methods: Between February 2013 and February 2014, 150 patients were randomized to undergo either LSG or LRYGB in our clinic. Differences in QOL were compared between groups by using multiple QOL questionnaires at follow-up moments preoperatively and 2 and 12 months after surgery.

Results: After 12 months of follow-up, 128 patients had returned the questionnaires. Most QOL questionnaires showed significant improvement in scores between the preoperative moment and after 12 months of follow-up. The Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease Questionnaire (GerdQ) score deteriorated in the LSG group after 2 months, but recovered again after 12 months. After 2 months of follow-up, the mean GerdQ score was 6.95 ± 2.14 in the LSG group versus 5.50 ± 1.49 in the LRYGB group (p < 0.001). After 1 year, the mean GerdQ score was 6.63 ± 2.26 in the LSG group and 5.60 ± 1.07 in the LRYGB group (p = 0.001).

Conclusion: This randomized controlled trial shows that patients who underwent LSG have significantly higher GerdQ scores at both 2 and 12 months postoperatively than patients who underwent LRYGB, whereas overall QOL did not differ significantly.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your stomach is too big, making you very overweight and unhealthy.  Doctors can do a special surgery to make your stomach smaller, which helps people lose weight and get healthier.  There are two types of this surgery: LSG and LRYGB.

This study compared the two surgeries to see which one is better.  They followed 150 people who had one of the surgeries.  They asked the people how they felt (their quality of life) before and after the surgery.

They found that both surgeries helped people lose weight and feel better overall.  However, the LSG surgery seemed to cause more heartburn (acid reflux) than the LRYGB surgery, even though both types of surgery improved heartburn over time.  So, while both surgeries helped, the LRYGB surgery might be slightly better for avoiding heartburn.

Title: Quality of Life 1 Year After Laparoscopic Sleeve Gastrectomy Versus Laparoscopic Roux-en-Y Gastric Bypass: a Randomized Controlled Trial Focusing on Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease
abstract:
Introduction: Bariatric surgery is a treatment option for achieving sustained weight loss in obese patients and has effects on obesity-related comorbidities. Laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy (LSG) and laparoscopic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (LRYGB) are two surgical options, but there is debate about the quality of life (QOL) after LSG, particularly concerning gastroesophageal reflux.

Materials and methods: Between February 2013 and February 2014, 150 patients were randomized to undergo either LSG or LRYGB in our clinic. The primary outcome was the difference in QOL between groups, assessed using multiple QOL questionnaires at follow-up moments preoperatively and 2 and 12 months after surgery.

Results: After 12 months of follow-up, 128 patients had returned the questionnaires. The Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease Questionnaire (GerdQ) score was higher in the LSG group compared to the LRYGB group. After 2 months of follow-up, the mean GerdQ score was 6.95 ± 2.14 in the LSG group versus 5.50 ± 1.49 in the LRYGB group (p < 0.001). After 1 year, the mean GerdQ score was 6.63 ± 2.26 in the LSG group and 5.60 ± 1.07 in the LRYGB group (p = 0.001). Safety data, including reasons for withdrawals and treatment discontinuation, were collected.

Conclusion: The treatment LSG was not more effective than LRYGB in patients with obesity regarding overall QOL.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your stomach is too big and you're having trouble losing weight.  Doctors can do surgery to make your stomach smaller, kind of like shrinking a balloon.  There are two main ways to do this surgery: one is called LSG, and the other is LRYGB.

Doctors wanted to see which surgery was better, not just for weight loss, but also for how people *felt* afterwards.  They asked 150 people to try one of the surgeries.  They checked in with them before the surgery, and then 2 months and 12 months later to see how they felt.  One thing they looked at was heartburn (acid reflux).

They found that people who had the LSG surgery had more heartburn than those who had the LRYGB surgery.  Even though both surgeries helped people lose weight,  the study showed that neither surgery was clearly "better" overall in terms of how people felt.  So, while LRYGB seemed to cause less heartburn, the overall feeling of well-being was about the same for both surgeries.

Title: Liposomal bupivacaine versus indwelling interscalene nerve block for postoperative pain control in shoulder arthroplasty: a prospective randomized controlled trial.
Journal: Journal of Shoulder and Elbow Surgery
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Pain management strategies following shoulder arthroplasty vary significantly. Liposomal bupivacaine (LB) is an extended-release delivery of a phospholipid bilayer encapsulating bupivacaine that can result in drug delivery up to 72 hours. Prior studies in lower extremity surgery demonstrated efficacy of LB in comparison to a single-shot peripheral nerve block; however, no study has investigated LB in a total shoulder arthroplasty population. Therefore, this study compared LB vs. an indwelling interscalene nerve block (IINB).

METHODS: This is a prospective, randomized study of 83 consecutive shoulder arthroplasty patients; 36 patients received LB and a "bridge" of 30вЂ‰mL of 0.5% bupivacaine, and 47 patients received an IINB. Postoperative visual analog scale pain levels, opiate consumption measured with oral morphine equivalents, length of hospital stay, and postoperative complications were recorded. Continuous variables were compared using an analysis of variance with significance set at PвЂ‰<вЂ‰.05.

RESULTS: Visual analog scale pain scores were statistically higher in the LB cohort immediately postoperatively in the postanesthesia care unit (7.25 vs. 1.91; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.000) as well as for the remainder of postoperative day 0 (4.99 vs. 3.20; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.005) but not for the remainder of admission. Opiate consumption was significantly higher among the LB cohort in the postanesthesia care unit (31.79 vs. 7.47; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.000), on postoperative day 0 (32.64 vs. 15.04; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.000), and for the total hospital admission (189.50 vs. 91.70, PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.000). Complication numbers and length of stay were not statistically different.

CONCLUSION: Use of an IINB provides superior pain management in the immediate postoperative setting as demonstrated by decreased narcotic medication consumption and lower subjective pain scores.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is broken and needs a new joint, like a robot part!  Doctors tried two different ways to stop the pain after the surgery.

**Group 1:** Got a special medicine (LB) that slowly releases pain relief over three days.  They also got a little extra pain medicine right after surgery.

**Group 2:** Got a different kind of pain medicine injected near the nerves in their shoulder (IINB) that worked right away.

The study checked how much pain each group had and how much pain medicine they needed.  Right after surgery, Group 1 had much more pain and needed way more pain medicine than Group 2.  After the first day, the difference wasn't as big.  But overall, the second method (IINB) was better at controlling pain right after the surgery.  Neither group had more problems or stayed in the hospital longer.

So, the study showed that the IINB method is better at managing pain immediately after shoulder surgery.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is broken and needs a new joint, like a robot part!  Doctors have two ways to help with the pain after surgery:

1. **Special Pain Medicine (LB):** This is like a tiny time-release capsule that slowly releases pain medicine over three days.

2. **Nerve Block (IINB):** This is like numbing the whole area with a shot that lasts a while.

Doctors wanted to see which method worked better. They gave one group of people the special medicine and another group the nerve block.  They checked their pain levels and how much pain medicine they needed afterward.

It turned out that the nerve block was better at controlling pain right after surgery.  The special medicine actually caused more pain and needed more extra pain medicine in the beginning.  But after a while, both methods were about the same.  Neither method caused more problems or kept people in the hospital longer.

So, even though the special medicine was supposed to help for a long time, the nerve block was better at managing pain right after the shoulder surgery.

Title: Randomized Clinical Trial of 2-Incision vs Mini-Posterior Total Hip Arthroplasty: Differences Persist at 10 Years.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage
abstract:
BACKGROUND: A previous randomized clinical trial at our institution demonstrated slower recovery of 35 2-incision total hip arthroplasties (THAs) when compared with 36 mini-posterior THAs at 2 years. The primary aim of the present study was to report concise 10-year follow-up results.

METHODS: We retrospectively reviewed the 71 patients in the previous randomized clinical trial, comparing clinical outcomes, revisions, reoperations, and implant survivorship between the 2-incision and the mini-posterior THAs.

RESULTS: At the most recent follow-up, the mean Harris hip score was 85 in the 2-incision group and 87 in the mini-posterior group (PВ = .4). There were 4 revisions and 2 reoperations (16%) in the 2-incision group vs 1 revision and 3 reoperations (11%) in the mini-posterior group (PВ = .5). Ten-year survivorship free of aseptic revision or reoperation was 77% in the 2-incision group vs 90% in the mini-posterior group (PВ =В .15).

CONCLUSION: There were no improvements in early or midterm clinical outcomes with the 2-incision technique. However, there was a clinical trend toward a higher rate of aseptic revisions in the 2-incision THA group.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine doctors did a big experiment comparing two ways to replace hip joints (like fixing a broken toy).  One way used a small cut (mini-posterior), and the other used a slightly bigger cut (2-incision).

They checked on the patients after two years, and the bigger cut group seemed to heal a little slower.  Then, they checked *again* ten years later to see how everyone was doing.

After ten years, both groups were doing pretty well, with similar scores on how well their hips worked.  However, slightly more people in the bigger-cut group needed their hip replacements fixed again.  It wasn't a huge difference, but it suggests the smaller cut might be a little better in the long run.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine doctors are testing two different ways to replace someone's hip joint:  one way uses a small cut (mini-posterior), and the other uses two small cuts (2-incision).

They already knew that after two years, the two-cut way seemed to take longer to heal.  This study checked in on those same people *ten* years later to see how they were doing.

After ten years, there wasn't a big difference between the two ways.  Both worked pretty well, and neither was clearly better than the other.  The small-cut way had slightly better results, but the difference wasn't big enough to say for sure one was better.

Title: The Effect of Aerobic or Aerobic-Strength Exercise on Body Composition and Functional Capacity in Patients with BMI в‰Ґ35 after Bariatric Surgery: a Randomized Control Trial.
abstract:
Background: Although previous studies suggested that bariatric surgery is the most effective and sustainable treatment method for morbid obesity in long term, but without changing in lifestyle, maintaining optimal weight loss is almost impossible.

Methods: Sixty morbid obese patients (BMI і 35) were evaluated before and after 12 weeks of bariatric surgery in order to compare the impact of two different exercise programs on body composition and functional capacity outcomes. Participants were divided into three groups: aerobic (A), aerobic-strength (AS), and control (C) group. Aerobic capacity was assessed with 12-min walk-run test (12MWRT). One-repetition maximum (1RM) test was performed to evaluation upper limb muscle strength. Lower extremity functional capacity was assessed by sit-to-stand test.

Results: Weight, percent body fat (PBF), and fat mass (FM) reduced greater in the trial groups in comparison to the C group (P < 0.05). In the AS group, the reduction of fat-free mass (FFM) was significantly lower than that in the other groups. Mean changes in 12MWRT increased significantly in the intervention groups. The mean change in the sit-to-stand scores was not statistically significant between the three groups. Comparing the intervention groups showed that mean changes in 1RM variables increased in AS group (P = 0.03).

Conclusions: The data suggests a positive effect of exercise on weight and PBF decrease after surgery, and it leads to significant improvement on aerobic capacity. Moreover, doing resisted exercise caused greater preserving of lean mass.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a really big, heavy backpack you can't take off.  Doctors sometimes do surgery to help people lose weight when they're carrying around *way* too much extra weight (like that backpack).

This study looked at what happens if, after that surgery, people also exercise.  They had three groups of people who'd had weight-loss surgery:

* **Group 1 (Aerobic):** Did only cardio exercises like running or biking.
* **Group 2 (Aerobic-Strength):** Did cardio *and* strength training (lifting weights).
* **Group 3 (Control):** Didn't do any extra exercise.

After 12 weeks, the people who exercised lost more weight and body fat than the group that didn't exercise.  The group that did both cardio and strength training kept more of their muscle (which is good!).  They also got better at running and biking.

So, the study showed that exercising after weight-loss surgery helps people lose even more weight and get stronger and healthier.  And doing strength training is especially good for keeping your muscles.

Title: The Effect of Aerobic or Aerobic-Strength Exercise on Body Composition and Functional Capacity in Patients with BMI в‰Ґ35 after Bariatric Surgery: a Randomized Control Trial.
abstract:
Background: Bariatric surgery is a common treatment method for morbid obesity, but maintaining optimal weight loss requires lifestyle changes.

Methods: Sixty morbidly obese patients (BMI ≥ 35) were evaluated before and after 12 weeks of bariatric surgery to compare the impact of two different exercise programs on body composition and functional capacity outcomes. Participants were divided into three groups: aerobic (A), aerobic-strength (AS), and control (C) group. The primary outcome was the change in body composition, including weight and percent body fat (PBF). Aerobic capacity was assessed with the 12-min walk-run test (12MWRT). Upper limb muscle strength was evaluated using the one-repetition maximum (1RM) test. Lower extremity functional capacity was assessed by the sit-to-stand test.

Results: Weight, PBF, and fat mass (FM) reduced more in the trial groups compared to the C group. In the AS group, the reduction of fat-free mass (FFM) was lower than in the other groups. Mean changes in 12MWRT increased in the intervention groups. The mean change in the sit-to-stand scores was not statistically significant between the three groups. Comparing the intervention groups showed that mean changes in 1RM variables increased in the AS group.

Conclusions: The treatment A was not more effective than comparator B in patients with morbid obesity.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you have a really big dog that needs to lose weight.  Doctors can do surgery to help, but the dog also needs to exercise to stay healthy and keep the weight off.

This study looked at 60 big dogs (people who are very overweight) who had weight-loss surgery.  They were split into three groups:

* **Group 1 (Aerobic):** Did only running and walking exercises.
* **Group 2 (Aerobic-Strength):** Did running, walking, *and* weightlifting exercises.
* **Group 3 (Control):** Didn't do any special exercises.

After 12 weeks, the doctors checked how much weight the dogs lost, how much fat they lost, and how strong and healthy they were.

They found that the dogs who exercised (Groups 1 and 2) lost more weight and fat than the dogs who didn't exercise (Group 3).  The dogs who did both running/walking and weightlifting (Group 2) got stronger muscles, but didn't lose as much muscle as the other exercise groups.  All the exercising dogs got better at running and walking longer distances.

In short, exercise helped the dogs lose weight and get healthier after their surgery, but just running and walking was just as good as running, walking, and weightlifting.

abstract:
IMPORTANCE: Emergency department visits for skin infections in the United States have increased with the emergence of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). For cellulitis without purulent drainage, ОІ-hemolytic streptococci are presumed to be the predominant pathogens. It is unknown if antimicrobial regimens possessing in vitro MRSA activity provide improved outcomes compared with treatments lacking MRSA activity.OBJECTIVE: To determine whether cephalexin plus trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole yields a higher clinical cure rate of uncomplicated cellulitis than cephalexin alone.DESIGN, SETTING, AND PARTICIPANTS: Multicenter, double-blind, randomized superiority trial in 5 US emergency departments among outpatients older than 12 years with cellulitis and no wound, purulent drainage, or abscess enrolled from April 2009 through June 2012. All participants had soft tissue ultrasound performed at the time of enrollment to exclude abscess. Final follow-up was August 2012.INTERVENTIONS: Cephalexin, 500 mg 4 times daily, plus trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, 320 mg/1600 mg twice daily, for 7 days (nвЂ‰=вЂ‰248 participants) or cephalexin plus placebo for 7 days (nвЂ‰=вЂ‰248 participants).MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURES: The primary outcome determined a priori in the per-protocol group was clinical cure, defined as absence of these clinical failure criteria at follow-up visits: fever; increase in erythema (>25%), swelling, or tenderness (days 3-4); no decrease in erythema, swelling, or tenderness (days 8-10); and more than minimal erythema, swelling, or tenderness (days 14-21). A clinically significant difference was defined as greater than 10%.

RESULTS: Among 500 randomized participants, 496 (99%) were included in the modified intention-to-treat analysis and 411 (82.2%) in the per-protocol analysis (median age, 40 years [range, 15-78 years]; 58.4% male; 10.9% had diabetes). Median length and width of erythema were 13.0 cm and 10.0 cm. In the per-protocol population, clinical cure occurred in 182 (83.5%) of 218 participants in the cephalexin plus trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole group vs 165 (85.5%) of 193 in the cephalexin group (difference, -2.0%; 95% CI, -9.7% to 5.7%; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.50). In the modified intention-to-treat population, clinical cure occurred in 189 (76.2%) of 248 participants in the cephalexin plus trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole group vs 171 (69.0%) of 248 in the cephalexin group (difference, 7.3%; 95% CI, -1.0% to 15.5%; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.07). Between-group adverse event rates and secondary outcomes through 7 to 9 weeks, including overnight hospitalization, recurrent skin infections, and similar infection in household contacts, did not differ significantly.CONCLUSIONS AND RELEVANCE: Among patients with uncomplicated cellulitis, the use of cephalexin plus trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole compared to cephalexin alone did not result in higher rates of clinical resolution of cellulitis in the per-protocol analysis. However, because imprecision around the findings in the modified intention-to-treat analysis included a clinically important difference favoring cephalexin plus trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, further research may be needed.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your skin gets a really bad infection called cellulitis – it's like a big, red, swollen area.  Doctors are trying to figure out the best medicine to treat it.

Some doctors think adding a second medicine (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) to the usual medicine (cephalexin) might make the infection go away faster.  So they did a big study with lots of people.

They split people into two groups: one group got both medicines, and the other group got only the usual medicine.  They watched to see which group got better faster.

The results were a little confusing.  One way of looking at the results showed almost no difference between the two groups.  Another way showed that the two medicines *might* be slightly better, but they weren't completely sure.

So, the study didn't prove that adding the second medicine is definitely better.  More research might be needed to know for sure.  It's like saying, "We're not 100% certain if adding the second medicine helps, but it might be worth looking into more."
