plain_language_summary:
Imagine your skin gets a really bad infection called cellulitis – it's like a really sore, red, swollen area.  Doctors are trying to figure out the best medicine to treat it.

Some doctors think adding a second medicine (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) to a common medicine (cephalexin) might make it heal faster.  So they did a big experiment with lots of people.

They split people into two groups: one group got cephalexin only, and the other group got cephalexin *plus* the extra medicine.  They watched to see who got better faster.

Guess what?  The extra medicine didn't really help!  Both groups got better at about the same rate.  So, for this type of skin infection, just using cephalexin seems to be just as good as using both medicines.

Title: Randomized Prospective Trial Comparing the Use of Intravenous versus Oral Acetaminophen in Total Joint Arthroplasty.
abstract:
Background: There is literature supporting that intravenous (IV) acetaminophen reduces postoperative pain and narcotic use in the total joint population. However, there are no studies comparing the effectiveness of IV vs oral (PO) acetaminophen as part of a standard multimodal perioperative pain regimen.

Methods: One hundred twenty patients undergoing hip and knee arthroplasty surgeries performed by one joint arthroplasty surgeon were prospectively randomized into 2 groups. Group 1 (63 patients) received IV and group 2 (57 patients) received PO acetaminophen in addition to a standard multimodal perioperative pain regimen. Each group received 1 gram of acetaminophen preoperatively and then every 6 hours for 24 hours. Total narcotic use and visual analog scale (VAS) scores were collected every 4 hours postoperatively.

Results: The 24-hour average hydromorphone equivalents given were not different between groups (3.71 vs 3.48) at 24 hours (P = .76), or at any of the individual 4-hour intervals. The 24-hour average visual analog scale scores in group 1 (IV) was 3.00 and in group 2 (PO) was 3.40 (P = .06). None of the 4-hour intervals were significantly different except the first interval (0-4 hour postoperatively), which favored the IV group (P = .03).

Conclusion: The treatment with IV acetaminophen was not more effective than oral acetaminophen in patients undergoing hip and knee arthroplasty surgeries.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you hurt your knee really badly and need surgery.  Doctors often give medicine to help with the pain afterwards.  This study looked at two ways to give the pain medicine acetaminophen (you might know it as Tylenol):  one through an IV (a needle in your arm) and the other by mouth (PO).

They gave 120 people getting knee or hip replacements either IV or oral acetaminophen, along with other pain medicines.  They wanted to see which way worked better at reducing pain and how much other stronger pain medicine they needed.

The results showed that there wasn't much difference between the two ways of giving acetaminophen.  Both groups needed about the same amount of extra strong pain medicine.  The IV group had slightly less pain right after surgery, but not enough to say it was definitely better overall.

So, the study concluded that giving acetaminophen through an IV isn't necessarily better than giving it by mouth for this type of surgery.  Both ways worked pretty much the same.

Title: Randomized Prospective Trial Comparing the Use of Intravenous versus Oral Acetaminophen in Total Joint Arthroplasty.
abstract:
Background: Multimodal pain management has had a significant effect on improving total joint arthroplasty recovery and patient satisfaction. There is literature supporting that intravenous (IV) acetaminophen reduces postoperative pain and narcotic use in the total joint population. However, there are no studies comparing the effectiveness of IV vs oral (PO) acetaminophen as part of a standard multimodal perioperative pain regimen.

Methods: One hundred twenty patients undergoing hip and knee arthroplasty surgeries performed by one joint arthroplasty surgeon were prospectively randomized into 2 groups. Group 1 (63 patients) received IV and group 2 (57 patients) received PO acetaminophen in addition to a standard multimodal perioperative pain regimen. Each group received 1 gram of acetaminophen preoperatively and then every 6 hours for 24 hours. Total narcotic use and visual analog scale (VAS) scores were collected every 4 hours postoperatively.

Results: The 24-hour average hydromorphone equivalents given were not different between groups (3.71 vs 3.48) at 24 hours (P = .76), or at any of the individual 4-hour intervals. The 24-hour average visual analog scale scores in group 1 (IV) was 3.00 and in group 2 (PO) was 3.40 (P = .06). None of the 4-hour intervals were significantly different except the first interval (0-4 hour postoperatively), which favored the IV group (P = .03).

Conclusion: The use of IV acetaminophen may have a role when given intraoperatively to reduce the immediate pain after surgery. Following that, it does not provide a significant benefit in reducing pain or narcotic use when compared with the much less expensive PO form.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee or hip is really, really sore because of a big surgery.  Doctors use different ways to help the pain, like medicine.

This study looked at two ways to give acetaminophen (like Tylenol), a common pain reliever:  one through an IV (a needle in your arm) and the other by mouth (PO).  They wanted to see which way worked better to reduce pain and the need for stronger pain medicine after hip and knee replacement surgeries.

They gave 120 people either IV or oral acetaminophen along with other pain medicines.  They measured their pain levels and how much strong pain medicine they needed.

The results showed that giving acetaminophen through an IV only helped a little bit right after the surgery.  After that, there wasn't much difference between giving it through an IV or by mouth.  Both ways helped about the same, and the oral (by mouth) way is much cheaper.

So, the study suggests that giving acetaminophen by mouth is just as good as giving it through an IV, except maybe for the very first few hours after surgery.

Title: A Community Health Worker-Delivered Intervention in African American Women with Type 2 Diabetes: A 12-Month Randomized Trial.
Spin in Abstract Title/Results: Focus on another objective (e.g., trial is (-) but they say they accomplish some goal that they did not prespecify)
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: RESULTS: Focus on (+) subgroup analysis
abstract:
OBJECTIVE: To evaluate the effectiveness of a community health worker (CHW)-delivered lifestyle intervention for African American women with type 2 diabetes.

METHODS: Participants were randomized to either 16 phone-based lifestyle intervention sessions aimed at making small changes in their diet and activity or 16 educational mailings sent across 12 months. The primary outcome was glycosylated hemoglobin (HbA1c). Secondary outcomes included blood pressure (BP) and weight (kg) changes.

RESULTS: Two hundred middle-aged (mean = 53 ± 10.24 years), rural, African American women with moderate obesity (mean BMI = 37.7 ± 8.02) and type 2 diabetes (mean HbA1c = 9.1 ± 1.83) were enrolled. At 12 months, the intervention group exhibited no significant differences in HbA1c (-0.29 ± 1.84 vs. + 0.005 ± 1.61; P = 0.789) or BP (-1.01 ± 20.46/+0.66 ± 13.24 vs. + 0.22 ± 25.33/-2.87 ± 1.52; P = 0.100) compared with controls. The intervention group showed greater weight loss (-1.35 ± 6.22 vs. -0.39 ± 4.57 kg, respectively; P = 0.046) compared with controls. Safety data and reasons for withdrawals were not reported.CONCLUSIONS: The treatment was not more effective than the comparator in patients with type 2 diabetes on the primary outcome of HbA1c.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine a group of grown-up women with a health problem called type 2 diabetes.  Doctors wanted to see if a special program could help them get healthier.

Half the women got 16 phone calls with advice on eating better and exercising more (like a personal health coach!). The other half got 16 letters with the same information in the mail.

After a year, doctors checked their health.  The phone calls didn't really help much with their main health problem (measured by something called HbA1c).  Blood pressure didn't improve much either.  However, the women who got the phone calls lost a little more weight than the women who got the letters.

So, the special phone program didn't work as well as hoped to improve their main health problem, but it did help a little with weight loss.

OBJECTIVE: To evaluate the effectiveness of a community health worker (CHW)-delivered lifestyle intervention for African American women with type 2 diabetes.

METHODS: Participants were randomized to either 16 phone-based lifestyle intervention sessions aimed at making small changes in their diet and activity or 16 educational mailings sent across 12 months. Main outcomes included glycosylated hemoglobin (HbA1c), blood pressure (BP), and weight (kg) changes.

RESULTS: Two hundred middle-aged (meanвЂ‰=вЂ‰53вЂ‰В±вЂ‰10.24 years), rural, African American women with moderate obesity (mean BMIвЂ‰=вЂ‰37.7вЂ‰В±вЂ‰8.02) and type 2 diabetes (mean HbA1c =вЂ‰9.1вЂ‰В±вЂ‰1.83) were enrolled. At 12 months, the intervention group exhibited no significant differences in HbA1c (-0.29вЂ‰В±вЂ‰1.84 vs.вЂ‰+вЂ‰0.005вЂ‰В±вЂ‰1.61; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.789) or BP (-1.01вЂ‰В±вЂ‰20.46/+0.66вЂ‰В±вЂ‰13.24 vs.вЂ‰+вЂ‰0.22вЂ‰В±вЂ‰25.33/-2.87вЂ‰В±вЂ‰1.52; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.100) but did exhibit greater weight loss (-1.35вЂ‰В±вЂ‰6.22вЂ‰ vs. -0.39вЂ‰В±вЂ‰4.57 kg, respectively; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.046) compared with controls. Exploratory post hoc analyses revealed that participants not using insulin had significantly greater reductions in HbA1c (-0.70вЂ‰В±вЂ‰1.86 vs.вЂ‰+вЂ‰0.07вЂ‰В±вЂ‰2.01; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.000), diastolic BP (-5.17вЂ‰В±вЂ‰14.16 vs. -3.40вЂ‰В±вЂ‰14.72 mmHg; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.035), and weight (-2.36вЂ‰В±вЂ‰6.59 vs. -1.64вЂ‰В±вЂ‰4.36 kg; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.003) compared to controls not on insulin.CONCLUSIONS: A phone-based CHW intervention resulted in no significant improvements in HbA1c or BP but did demonstrate modest improvements in weight. Women not using insulin showed significant improvements in all primary outcomes.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine a science experiment to help women with diabetes get healthier.  

The experiment had two groups of women:

* **Group 1:** Got 16 phone calls with advice on eating better and exercising more.  Think of it like having a health coach on the phone.
* **Group 2:** Got 16 letters with health information in the mail.

Scientists wanted to see if the phone calls helped more than the letters. They checked things like:

* **Blood sugar:**  How well their bodies used sugar.
* **Blood pressure:** How hard their hearts had to work.
* **Weight:** How much they weighed.

**What happened?**

Overall, the phone calls didn't make a big difference in blood sugar or blood pressure.  But the women who got the phone calls *did* lose a little more weight than the women who got the letters.

Interestingly, if you only looked at the women who *weren't* taking insulin for their diabetes, the phone calls *did* help improve their blood sugar, blood pressure, and weight.

**In short:**  The phone calls helped a little with weight loss, and helped a lot more for the women not using insulin.  It shows that talking to someone on the phone about healthy habits can be helpful, especially if you aren't taking medicine for your diabetes.

Title: Intravenous Acetaminophen in Multimodal Pain Management for Patients Undergoing Total Knee Arthroplasty: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
BACKGROUND: The efficacy of intravenous (IV) acetaminophen compared with its oral formulation for postoperative analgesia is unknown. 

METHODS: The study was a single-center, randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled clinical trial on the efficacy of IV vs oral acetaminophen in patients undergoing unilateral total knee arthroplasty (TKA). One hundred seventy-four subjects were randomized to one of the 3 groups: IV acetaminophen group (IV group, n = 57) received 1 g IV acetaminophen and oral placebo before postanesthesia care unit (PACU) admission; oral acetaminophen group (PO group, n = 58) received 1 g oral acetaminophen and volume-matched IV normal saline; placebo group (Placebo group, n = 59) received oral placebo and volume-matched IV normal saline. Pain scores were obtained every 15 minutes during PACU stay. The primary outcome was the average pain score during PACU stay. Secondary outcomes included total opiate consumption, time to PACU discharge, time to rescue analgesia, and time to breakthrough pain.

RESULTS: The average PACU pain score was similar in the IV group (0.56 ± 0.99 [mean ± standard deviation]) compared with the PO group (0.67 ± 1.20; P = .84) and Placebo group (0.58 ± 0.99; P = .71). Total opiate consumption at 6 hours (0.47 mg hydromorphone equivalents ± 0.56 vs 0.54 ± 0.53 vs 0.54 ± 0.61; P = .69) and at 24 hours (1.25 ± 1.30 vs 1.49 ± 1.34 vs 1.36 ± 1.31; P = .46) were also similar between the IV, PO, and Placebo groups. No significant differences were found between all groups for any other outcome.

CONCLUSION: The treatment with IV or oral acetaminophen was not more effective than placebo in patients undergoing TKA in the setting of a spinal anesthetic.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you had knee surgery.  Doctors wanted to see if giving pain medicine (acetaminophen, which is like Tylenol) through an IV (a needle in your arm) worked better than taking it as a pill, or even better than taking nothing at all (a placebo, which is like a sugar pill).

They did a study with people getting knee replacements.  They split them into three groups:

* **Group 1:** Got pain medicine through an IV.
* **Group 2:** Got pain medicine as a pill.
* **Group 3:** Got a sugar pill (placebo) – no real medicine.

They checked how much pain each person had after surgery.  Guess what?  It didn't matter if they got the medicine through an IV or as a pill, or even if they got no medicine at all!  Everyone had about the same amount of pain.

So, the study showed that giving acetaminophen through an IV or as a pill didn't help with pain any more than taking nothing at all after this type of knee surgery.

Title: Intravenous Acetaminophen in Multimodal Pain Management for Patients Undergoing Total Knee Arthroplasty: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
BACKGROUND: The efficacy of intravenous (IV) acetaminophen compared with its oral formulation for postoperative analgesia is unknown. We hypothesized that the addition of acetaminophen to a multimodal analgesia regimen would provide improved pain management in patients after total knee arthroplasty (TKA) and that the effect of acetaminophen would be variable based on the route of delivery.

METHODS: The study was a single-center, randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled clinical trial on the efficacy of IV vs oral acetaminophen in patients undergoing unilateral TKA. One hundred seventy-four subjects were randomized to one of the 3 groups: IV acetaminophen group (IV group, nВ = 57) received 1 g IV acetaminophen and oral placebo before postanesthesia care unit (PACU) admission; oral acetaminophen group (PO group, nВ = 58) received 1 g oral acetaminophen and volume-matched IV normal saline; placebo group (Placebo group, nВ = 59) received oral placebo and volume-matched IV normal saline. Pain scores were obtained every 15В minutes during PACU stay. Average pain scores, maximum pain score, and pain scores before physical therapy were compared among the 3 groups. Secondary outcomes included total opiate consumption, time to PACU discharge, time to rescue analgesia, and time to breakthrough pain.

RESULTS: The average PACU pain score was similar in the IV group (0.56 В± 0.99 [mean В± standard deviation]) compared with the PO group (0.67 В± 1.20; PВ = .84) and Placebo group (0.58 В± 0.99; PВ = .71). Total opiate consumption at 6В hours (0.47 mg hydromorphone equivalents В± 0.56 vs 0.54 В± 0.53 vs 0.54 В± 0.61; PВ = .69) and at 24В hours (1.25 В± 1.30 vs 1.49 В± 1.34 vs 1.36 В± 1.31; PВ = .46) were also similar between the IV, PO, and Placebo groups. No significant differences were found between all groups for any other outcome.

CONCLUSION: Neither IV nor oral acetaminophen provides additional analgesia in the immediate postoperative period when administered as an adjunct to multimodal analgesia in patients undergoing TKA in the setting of a spinal anesthetic.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine you just had a really big surgery on your knee.  It would hurt a lot, right?  Doctors want to make sure you don't have too much pain.

This study was trying to figure out the best way to give medicine to help with that pain after knee surgery.  The medicine was acetaminophen (like Tylenol).  They wanted to see if giving it through an IV (a needle in your arm) was better than giving it as a pill.

They gave some people the acetaminophen through an IV, some people got it as a pill, and some people got a fake medicine (a placebo) to compare.  They checked how much pain everyone had and how much other pain medicine they needed.

The surprise was that it didn't matter if they got the acetaminophen through an IV or as a pill – it didn't help much with the pain after the surgery.  Both ways were about the same as getting no acetaminophen at all.  So, for this kind of surgery, giving extra acetaminophen didn't really make a difference in how much pain people felt.

Title: Randomized controlled trial of internal and external targeted temperature management methods in post- cardiac arrest patients.
Journal: The American Journal of Emergency Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) subgroup analysis
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Targeted temperature management post-cardiac arrest is currently implemented using various methods, broadly categorized as internal and external. This study aimed to evaluate survival-to-hospital discharge and neurological outcomes (Glasgow-Pittsburgh Score) of post-cardiac arrest patients undergoing internal cooling versus external cooling.METHODOLOGY: A randomized controlled trial of post-resuscitation cardiac arrest patients was conducted from October 2008 to September 2014. Patients were randomized to either internal or external cooling methods. Analysis using SPSS version 21.0 presented descriptive statistics and frequencies while univariate logistic regression was done using R 3.1.3.

RESULTS: 23 patients were randomized to internal cooling and 22 patients to external cooling. No significant difference was seen between internal and external cooling in terms of survival, neurological outcomes, and complications. Safety data indicated that in the internal cooling arm, there was a lower risk of developing overcooling and rebound hyperthermia. Cooling curves showed tighter temperature control for internal compared to external cooling.

CONCLUSION: The treatment of internal cooling was not more effective than external cooling in patients with post-cardiac arrest in terms of survival-to-hospital discharge and neurological outcomes.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine someone's heart stops suddenly.  Doctors can try to cool the body down afterwards (this helps the brain).  They do this in two ways:  inside the body (internal cooling) or outside the body (external cooling).

This study compared the two methods.  They looked at whether people survived and how well their brains worked afterward.  They studied 45 people total.

The study found that both methods were equally good at helping people survive and recover.  Internal cooling was a little better at keeping the body temperature just right, but it didn't make a difference in the final outcome.  So, both ways of cooling are pretty much the same for helping people after their heart stops.

Title: Randomized controlled trial of internal and external targeted temperature management methods in post- cardiac arrest patients.
Journal: The American Journal of Emergency Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) subgroup analysis
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Targeted temperature management post-cardiac arrest is currently implemented using various methods, broadly categorized as internal and external. This study aimed to evaluate survival-to-hospital discharge and neurological outcomes (Glasgow-Pittsburgh Score) of post-cardiac arrest patients undergoing internal cooling verses external cooling.METHODOLOGY: A randomized controlled trial of post-resuscitation cardiac arrest patients was conducted from October 2008-September 2014. Patients were randomized to either internal or external cooling methods. Historical controls were selected matched by age and gender. Analysis using SPSS version 21.0 presented descriptive statistics and frequencies while univariate logistic regression was done using R 3.1.3.

RESULTS: 23 patients were randomized to internal cooling and 22 patients to external cooling and 42 matched controls were selected. No significant difference was seen between internal and external cooling in terms of survival, neurological outcomes and complications. However in the internal cooling arm, there was lower risk of developing overcooling (p=0.01) and rebound hyperthermia (p=0.02). Compared to normothermia, internal cooling had higher survival (OR=3.36, 95% CI=(1.130, 10.412), and lower risk of developing cardiac arrhythmias (OR=0.18, 95% CI=(0.04, 0.63)). Subgroup analysis showed those with cardiac cause of arrest (OR=4.29, 95% CI=(1.26, 15.80)) and sustained ROSC (OR=5.50, 95% CI=(1.64, 20.39)) had better survival with internal cooling compared to normothermia. Cooling curves showed tighter temperature control for internal compared to external cooling.

CONCLUSION: Internal cooling showed tighter temperature control compared to external cooling. Internal cooling can potentially provide better survival-to-hospital discharge outcomes and reduce cardiac arrhythmia complications in carefully selected patients as compared to normothermia.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine someone's heart stops suddenly.  Doctors can try to cool the body down (like a refrigerator) to help the brain heal.  They do this in two ways:  inside the body (internal cooling) or outside the body (external cooling).

This study compared the two methods.  They took about 40 people whose hearts stopped and randomly chose which cooling method to use. They also looked at people who didn't get cooled down (normothermia).

What they found:  Both methods were pretty similar in terms of whether people survived and how well their brains worked afterwards.  However, internal cooling was better at keeping the body temperature just right, preventing it from getting too cold or too hot again afterwards.  Internal cooling also seemed to help people survive better and have fewer heart problems, especially for people whose heart stopped because of a heart problem itself, or who had a good response to CPR.

In short:  Cooling the body after a heart stops can help, and cooling from *inside* the body might be a slightly better way to do it.

Title: Efficacy of a Community- Versus Primary Care-Centered Program for Childhood Obesity: TX CORD RCT.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) subgroup analysis
abstract:
OBJECTIVE: This randomized controlled trial was conducted to compare the efficacy of a 12-month community-centered weight management program (MEND2-5 for ages 2-5 or MEND/CATCH6-12 for ages 6-12) against a primary care-centered program (Next Steps) in low-income children.

METHODS: Five hundred forty-nine Hispanic and black children (BMI ≥ 85th percentile) were randomly assigned to MEND2-5 (27 contact hours)/MEND/CATCH6-12 (121.5 contact hours) or Next Steps (8 contact hours). The primary outcome, BMI value at the 95th percentile (%BMIp95), was measured at baseline, 3 months (Intensive Phase), and 12 months (Transition Phase).

RESULTS: For age group 6-8, MEND/CATCH6-12 resulted in an effect size (95% CI) of -1.94 (-3.88, -0.01) percentage points for %BMIp95 during the Intensive Phase (P = 0.05). For age group 9-12, the effect size was -1.38 (-2.87, 0.16) percentage points for %BMIp95 (P = 0.07). MEND2-5 did not differentially affect %BMIp95. Attendance averaged 52% and 22% during the Intensive and Transition Phases. In the Transition Phase, %BMIp95 was maintained or rebounded in both programs. Safety data, including reasons for withdrawals and treatment discontinuation, were not specified.CONCLUSIONS: The treatment MEND/CATCH6-12 was not more effective than Next Steps in patients with low-income backgrounds for BMI reduction over 12 months.
plain_language_summary:

Title: Efficacy of a Community- Versus Primary Care-Centered Program for Childhood Obesity: TX CORD RCT.

OBJECTIVE: This randomized controlled trial was conducted to determine comparative efficacy of a 12-month community-centered weight management program (MEND2-5 for ages 2-5 or MEND/CATCH6-12 for ages 6-12) against a primary care-centered program (Next Steps) in low-income children.

METHODS: Five hundred forty-nine Hispanic and black children (BMIвЂ‰в‰ҐвЂ‰85th percentile), stratified by age groups (2-5, 6-8, and 9-12 years), were randomly assigned to MEND2-5 (27 contact hours)/MEND/CATCH6-12 (121.5 contact hours) or Next Steps (8 contact hours). Primary (BMI value at the 95th percentile [%BMIp95 ]) and secondary outcomes were measured at baseline, 3 months (Intensive Phase), and 12 months (Transition Phase).

RESULTS: For age group 6-8, MEND/CATCH6-12 resulted in greater improvement in %BMIp95 than Next Steps during the Intensive Phase. Effect size (95% CI) was -1.94 (-3.88, -0.01) percentage points (PвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.05). For age group 9-12, effect size was -1.38 (-2.87, 0.16) percentage points for %BMIp95 (PвЂ‰=вЂ‰0.07). MEND2-5 did not differentially affect %BMIp95 . Attendance averaged 52% and 22% during the Intensive and Transition Phases. Intervention compliance was inversely correlated to change in %BMIp95 during the Intensive Phase (PвЂ‰<вЂ‰0.05). Title: Targeted Temperature Management for 48 vs 24 Hours and Neurologic Outcome After Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Focus on another objective (e.g., trial is (-) but they say they accomplish some goal that they did not prespecify)
abstract:
IMPORTANCE: International resuscitation guidelines recommend targeted temperature management (TTM) at 33В°C to 36В°C in unconscious patients with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest for at least 24 hours, but the optimal duration of TTM is uncertain.OBJECTIVE: To determine whether TTM at 33В°C for 48 hours results in better neurologic outcomes compared with currently recommended, standard, 24-hour TTM.DESIGN, SETTING, AND PARTICIPANTS: This was an international, investigator-initiated, blinded-outcome-assessor, parallel, pragmatic, multicenter, randomized clinical superiority trial in 10 intensive care units (ICUs) at 10 university hospitals in 6 European countries. Three hundred fifty-five adult, unconscious patients with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest were enrolled from February 16, 2013, to June 1, 2016, with final follow-up on December 27, 2016.INTERVENTIONS: Patients were randomized to TTM (33вЂ‰В±вЂ‰1В°C) for 48 hours (nвЂ‰=вЂ‰176) or 24 hours (nвЂ‰=вЂ‰179), followed by gradual rewarming of 0.5В°C per hour until reaching 37В°C.MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURES: The primary outcome was 6-month neurologic outcome, with a Cerebral Performance Categories (CPC) score of 1 or 2 used to define favorable outcome. Secondary outcomes included 6-month mortality, including time to death, the occurrence of adverse events, and intensive care unit resource use.

RESULTS: In 355 patients who were randomized (mean age, 60 years; 295 [83%] men), 351 (99%) completed the trial. Of these patients, 69% (120/175) in the 48-hour group had a favorable outcome at 6 months compared with 64% (112/176) in the 24-hour group (difference, 4.9%; 95% CI, -5% to 14.8%; relative risk [RR], 1.08; 95% CI, 0.93-1.25; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.33). Six-month mortality was 27% (48/175) in the 48-hour group and 34% (60/177) in the 24-hour group (difference, -6.5%; 95% CI, -16.1% to 3.1%; RR, 0.81; 95% CI, 0.59-1.11; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.19). There was no significant difference in the time to mortality between the 48-hour group and the 24-hour group (hazard ratio, 0.79; 95% CI, 0.54-1.15; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.22). Adverse events were more common in the 48-hour group (97%) than in the 24-hour group (91%) (difference, 5.6%; 95% CI, 0.6%-10.6%; RR, 1.06; 95% CI, 1.01-1.12; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.04). The median length of intensive care unit stay (151 vs 117 hours; PвЂ‰<вЂ‰.001), but not hospital stay (11 vs 12 days; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.50), was longer in the 48-hour group than in the 24-hour group.CONCLUSIONS AND RELEVANCE: In unconscious survivors from out-of-hospital cardiac arrest admitted to the ICU, targeted temperature management at 33В°C for 48 hours did not significantly improve 6-month neurologic outcome compared with targeted temperature management at 33В°C for 24 hours. However, the study may have had limited power to detect clinically important differences, and further research may be warranted.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your heart suddenly stops beating outside of a hospital.  Doctors can try to restart it, but sometimes people's brains are affected.  To help, doctors sometimes cool the body down (like putting it in a special "cool blanket") to protect the brain.  This is called Targeted Temperature Management (TTM).

Doctors wanted to know if cooling the body for 48 hours is better than cooling it for 24 hours.  They did a big study with lots of grown-ups who had this happen to them.

They found that cooling for 48 hours didn't make a big difference in how well people did after 6 months.  Both groups did about the same.  Cooling for 48 hours *did* mean people stayed in the hospital longer, and had slightly more problems.

So, the study showed that the current way of cooling people's bodies (for 24 hours) is probably just as good as cooling them for longer.  They need to do more studies to be completely sure.

Title: A Midterm Evaluation of Postoperative Platelet-Rich Plasma Injections on Arthroscopic Supraspinatus Repair: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) has been applied as an adjunct to rotator cuff repair to improve tendon-bone healing and potentially reduce the incidence of subsequent tendon retears.

PURPOSE: To investigate whether the midterm clinical and radiographic outcomes of arthroscopic supraspinatus repair are enhanced after repeated postoperative applications of PRP.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 1.

METHODS: A total of 60 patients (30 control; 30 PRP) were initially randomized to receive 2 ultrasound-guided injections of PRP to the tendon repair site at 7 and 14 days after double-row arthroscopic supraspinatus repair or not. A total of 55 patients (91.7%) underwent a clinical review and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) at a mean of 3.5 years after surgery (range, 36-51 months). The primary outcome was the Constant score. Patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) included the Quick Disabilities of the Arm, Shoulder and Hand (QuickDASH) questionnaire, Oxford Shoulder Score (OSS), and visual analog scale (VAS) for pain. Structural integrity of the surgical repair was assessed via MRI using the Sugaya classification system.

RESULTS: At the midterm review, there was no difference between the groups for any of the PROMs. No differences between the groups were demonstrated for the subjective and range of motion subscales of the Constant score, although a significantly higher Constant strength subscale score was observed in the PRP group (3.3 points; 95% CI, 1.0-5.7; P = .006). There was no evidence for any group differences in MRI scores or retear rates, with 66.7% of PRP patients and 64.3% of control patients rated as Sugaya grade 1. Two control patients had symptomatic retears (both full thickness) within the first 16 weeks after surgery compared with 2 PRP patients, who suffered symptomatic retears (both partial thickness) between 16 weeks and a mean 3.5-year follow-up.

CONCLUSION: The treatment with PRP was not more effective than the control in patients with supraspinatus repair.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is like a rope (the tendon) attached to a strong post (the bone).  Sometimes that rope tears.  Doctors can fix it with surgery.

This study was trying to figure out if adding a special goo (platelet-rich plasma, or PRP) after surgery helps the rope heal better and stronger.  They had two groups of people: one group got the special goo, and one group didn't.

After a few years, they checked how well everyone's shoulders were doing.  They looked at how strong the shoulder was, how much pain people had, and how well the rope was attached to the post using special pictures (MRI).

The results showed that the special goo didn't really make a big difference.  Both groups got better, and the goo didn't prevent the rope from tearing again.  The only small difference was that the group with the goo had slightly stronger shoulders.

So, in short, the study found that this special goo didn't make a huge difference in helping people's shoulders heal after surgery.

Title: Liposomal bupivacaine versus interscalene nerve block for pain control after shoulder arthroplasty: a prospective randomized trial.
Journal: Journal of Shoulder and Elbow Surgery
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Shoulder arthroplasty is a common joint replacement surgery in the United States, and effective postoperative pain management is important for these surgeries. Liposomal bupivacaine (LB) is used for its potential to provide extended postoperative pain relief. This study aimed to compare the impact of LB with continuous interscalene nerve block (CISB) on postoperative pain control after shoulder arthroplasty surgery.

METHODS: A prospective randomized controlled clinical trial compared patients undergoing shoulder arthroplasty treated with CISB versus LB with a single bolus interscalene block. The primary outcome measure was pain assessment up to 24 hours after surgery. All doses and times of narcotics administered during the inpatient stay were recorded.

RESULTS: A total of 70 of 74 consecutive patients who underwent shoulder arthroplasty were included in the study. The LB group had equivalent narcotic use, pain scores, and time to first narcotic rescue compared with the CISB group within the first 24 hours (P > .05). Safety data indicated an increased number of complications for the CISB group.

CONCLUSION: The treatment with liposomal bupivacaine was not more effective than continuous interscalene nerve block in patients with shoulder arthroplasty regarding postoperative pain control within the first 24 hours.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is broken and needs a new joint, like replacing a rusty hinge on a door.  Doctors sometimes use special medicine to numb the pain after this surgery.

This study compared two different numbing medicines: one called LB and another called CISB.  They wanted to see which one worked better at keeping the pain away for the first 24 hours after the surgery.

They gave one group of people LB and another group CISB.  They checked how much pain medicine (narcotics) each group needed and how much pain they had.

The result?  Both medicines worked about the same!  Both groups needed about the same amount of extra pain medicine and had similar pain levels.  Interestingly, the CISB group had more problems (complications) than the LB group.

So, in short, the study showed that the two numbing medicines were equally good at controlling pain after shoulder surgery in the first 24 hours.

Title: Liposomal bupivacaine versus interscalene nerve block for pain control after shoulder arthroplasty: a prospective randomized trial.
Journal: Journal of Shoulder and Elbow Surgery
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Shoulder arthroplasty is the fastest growing joint replacement surgery in the United States, and optimal postoperative pain management is critical to optimize outcomes for these surgeries. Liposomal bupivacaine (LB) has gained popularity for its potential to provide extended postoperative pain relief with possibly fewer side effects. The goal of this study was to assess the impact of LB compared with continuous interscalene nerve block (CISB) in terms of postoperative pain control, outpatient pain scores, and patient-reported and functional outcomes after shoulder arthroplasty surgery.

METHODS: A prospective randomized controlled clinical trial compared consecutive patients undergoing shoulder arthroplasty treated with CISB vs. LB with a single bolus interscalene block. The primary outcome measures included pain assessment up to 24 hours after surgery; in addition, all doses and times of narcotics administered during the inpatient stay were recorded. Patient-reported outcome measures for pain, satisfaction, and functional outcomes were recorded postoperatively.

RESULTS: A total of 70 of 74 consecutive patients who underwent shoulder arthroplasty were included in the study. The LB group had equivalent narcotic use, pain scores, and time to first narcotic rescue compared with the CISB group within the first 24 hours (PвЂ‰>вЂ‰.05). The LB group had higher American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons score and Penn Shoulder Score at final follow-up. There was an increased number of complications and cost for the CISB group.

CONCLUSION: This prospective randomized controlled trial demonstrated that LB provides excellent postoperative pain relief for shoulder arthroplasty patients. In addition, LB had fewer complications and lower cost, making it a promising addition to a multimodal pain regimen for shoulder arthroplasty.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is broken and needs a new joint, like replacing a rusty hinge.  Doctors do this surgery a lot now.  They want to find the best way to help people not hurt after the surgery.

They tested these methods on people getting shoulder surgery.  They checked how much pain the people had, how much medicine they needed for pain, and how well their shoulder worked afterward.

They found that both methods worked about the same for pain in the first 24 hours.  But the special medicine (LB) was cheaper, had fewer problems, and helped people's shoulders get better faster.

So, the special medicine (LB) seems like a good way to help people have less pain and recover better after shoulder surgery.

Title: A Novel Home-Based Intervention for Child and Adolescent Obesity: The Results of the WhГ„nau Pakari Randomized Controlled Trial.

abstract:
OBJECTIVE: To report 12-month outcomes from a multidisciplinary child obesity intervention program, targeting high-risk groups.

METHODS: In this unblinded randomized controlled trial, participants (recruited January 2012-August 2014) were aged 5 to 16 years, resided in Taranaki, Aotearoa/New Zealand, and had BMIвЂ‰в‰ҐвЂ‰98th percentile or BMIвЂ‰>вЂ‰91st percentile with weight-related comorbidities. Randomization was by minimization (age and ethnicity), with participants assigned to an intense intervention group (home-based assessments at 6-month intervals and a 12-month multidisciplinary program with weekly group sessions) or to a minimal-intensity control group with home-based assessments and advice at each 6-month follow-up. The primary outcome was the change in BMI standard deviation score (SDS) at 12 months from baseline. A mixed model analysis was undertaken, incorporating all 6- and 12-month data.

RESULTS: Two hundred and three children were randomly assigned (47% MДЃori, 43% New Zealand European, 53% female, 28% from the most deprived quintile, mean age 10.7 years, mean BMI SDS 3.12). Both groups displayed a change in BMI SDS at 12 months from baseline (-0.12 control, -0.10 intervention), improvements in cardiovascular fitness (PвЂ‰<вЂ‰0.0001), and improvements in quality of life (PвЂ‰<вЂ‰0.001). AchievingвЂ‰в‰ҐвЂ‰70% attendance in the intense intervention group resulted in a change in BMI SDS of -0.22.CONCLUSIONS: This program achieved a high recruitment of target groups and a high rate of BMI SDS reduction, irrespective of intervention intensity. If retention is optimized, the intensive program doubles its effect.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine a group of scientists wanted to help kids who were overweight.  They had a special program to help these kids get healthier.

They chose kids aged 5 to 16 who lived in a place called Taranaki, New Zealand, and were much heavier than other kids their age.  They split the kids into two groups:

* **Group 1 (Intense Program):** These kids got lots of help! They had home visits every six months and went to weekly group meetings for a whole year to learn about healthy eating and exercise.

* **Group 2 (Control Group):** These kids also got home visits every six months, but with less help and fewer meetings.

The scientists wanted to see how much the kids' weight changed after a year.  They measured it using a special number called BMI SDS.  A lower number is better.

After a year, both groups got a little bit healthier!  The kids in both groups got better at exercising and felt better about themselves.  But, the kids who went to the lots of meetings in Group 1 got even better results if they went to most of them.

So, even though the special program helped, just getting regular check-ups and advice helped kids get healthier too.  The scientists learned that if kids stick with the program, it works even better!

Title: A Novel Home-Based Intervention for Child and Adolescent Obesity: The Results of the WhГ„nau Pakari Randomized Controlled Trial.

abstract:
OBJECTIVE: To report 12-month outcomes from a multidisciplinary child obesity intervention program.

METHODS: In this unblinded randomized controlled trial, participants (recruited January 2012-August 2014) were aged 5 to 16 years, resided in Taranaki, Aotearoa/New Zealand, and had BMI ≥98th percentile or BMI >91st percentile with weight-related comorbidities. Randomization was by minimization (age and ethnicity), with participants assigned to an intense intervention group (home-based assessments at 6-month intervals and a 12-month multidisciplinary program with weekly group sessions) or to a minimal-intensity control group with home-based assessments and advice at each 6-month follow-up. The primary outcome was the change in BMI standard deviation score (SDS) at 12 months from baseline. A mixed model analysis was undertaken, incorporating all 6- and 12-month data.

RESULTS: Two hundred and three children were randomly assigned (47% Māori, 43% New Zealand European, 53% female, 28% from the most deprived quintile, mean age 10.7 years, mean BMI SDS 3.12). The change in BMI SDS at 12 months from baseline was -0.12 in the control group and -0.10 in the intervention group. Improvements in cardiovascular fitness and quality of life were observed. Safety data, including reasons for withdrawals and treatment discontinuation, were collected.CONCLUSIONS: The treatment was not more effective than the comparator in patients with a change in BMI SDS.

* **Group 2 (the regular group):** These kids also got check-ups at home every six months, but didn't have the weekly meetings.

After a whole year, the doctors checked how much the kids' weight had changed.  Surprisingly, both groups had almost the same amount of weight change –  the special program didn't make much more of a difference than just getting regular check-ups. So, the study showed that this special program wasn't much better than just getting regular advice and check-ups for helping kids lose weight.

Title: Ondansetron treatment reduces rotavirus symptoms-A randomized double-blinded placebo-controlled trial.
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Rotavirus and norovirus cause acute gastroenteritis with severe diarrhea and vomiting, symptoms that may lead to severe dehydration and death. The objective of this randomized double-blinded placebo-controlled study was to investigate whether ondansetron, a serotonin receptor antagonist, could attenuate rotavirus- and norovirus-induced vomiting and diarrhea.

METHODS: Children with acute gastroenteritis, aged 6 months to 16 years, were enrolled (n = 104) and randomized to one single oral dose (0.15mg/kg) of ondansetron (n = 52) or placebo (n = 52). The primary outcome was the number of diarrhea and vomiting episodes during the 24 hours following treatment. Pathogens in feces were diagnosed by real-time PCR.

RESULTS: One dose of oral ondansetron reduced the duration of rotavirus clinical symptoms, with a median of two days. The number of diarrhea episodes was also reported. Safety data, including reasons for withdrawals and treatment discontinuation, were collected.

CONCLUSION: The treatment with ondansetron was not more effective than placebo in patients with acute gastroenteritis in reducing the number of diarrhea and vomiting episodes.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your stomach gets really sick with diarrhea and vomiting (throwing up) from a virus.  That's what happened to some kids in this study.  Doctors wanted to see if a medicine called ondansetron could help make them feel better faster.

They gave some kids the medicine and some kids a fake medicine (a placebo – so they wouldn't know which they got).  They watched to see how many times the kids threw up and had diarrhea in the next 24 hours.

The results showed that the medicine didn't really help reduce the number of times the kids threw up or had diarrhea compared to the kids who got the fake medicine.  While it did seem to help *shorten* how long the sickness lasted for kids with one specific virus (rotavirus), overall, the medicine wasn't better than nothing.  The doctors also kept track of whether the medicine caused any problems.

Title: Ondansetron treatment reduces rotavirus symptoms-A randomized double-blinded placebo-controlled trial.
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Rotavirus and norovirus cause acute gastroenteritis with severe diarrhoea and vomiting, symptoms that may lead to severe dehydration and death. The objective of this randomized double-blinded placebo-controlled study was to investigate whether ondansetron, a serotonin receptor antagonist could attenuate rotavirus- and norovirus-induced vomiting and diarrhoea, which would facilitate oral rehydration and possibly accelerate recovery and reduce need for hospitalization.

METHODS: Children with acute gastroenteritis, aged 6 months to 16 years where enrolled (n = 104) and randomized to one single oral dose (0.15mg/kg) of ondansetron (n = 52) or placebo (n = 52). The number of diarrhoea and vomiting episodes during the 24 hours following treatment was reported as well as the number of days with symptoms. Pathogens in faeces were diagnosed by real-time PCR. Outcome parameters were analyzed for rotavirus- and norovirus-positive children.

RESULTS: One dose of oral ondansetron reduced duration of rotavirus clinical symptoms (p = 0.014), with a median of two days. Furthermore, ondansetron reduced diarrhea episodes, most pronounced in children that had been sick for more than 3 days before treatment (p = 0.028).

CONCLUSION: Ondansetron may be a beneficial treatment for children with rotavirus gastroenteritis.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your stomach gets really sick with diarrhea and vomiting from a virus (like a really bad tummy ache).  This study was trying to figure out if a medicine called ondansetron could help kids feel better faster.

They gave some kids this medicine and some kids a fake medicine (a placebo – so they wouldn't know which they got).  They watched to see how many times the kids threw up and had diarrhea, and how long their sickness lasted.

They found that the medicine helped kids who were sick with a virus called rotavirus.  It made their sickness shorter, and they had less diarrhea, especially if they'd already been sick for a few days.  So, the medicine might be a good way to help kids with this type of tummy bug get better quicker.

Title: A Randomized, Controlled, Multicenter Study of Technology-Based Weight Loss Interventions among Endometrial Cancer Survivors.

abstract:
OBJECTIVE: The aim of this study was to test the efficacy of technology-based weight loss interventions for endometrial cancer (EC) survivors with obesity.

METHODS: EC survivors with obesity (n = 196) from three medical centers were randomized to a 6-month intervention: telemedicine with Wi-Fi scales, text messaging (texting), or enhanced usual care (EUC). The primary outcome was weight loss. Changes in anthropometrics and psychosocial measures were analyzed.

RESULTS: There were no significant differences in weight loss across interventions (mean = -4.4 kg, SD = 6.5 kg). Safety data, including reasons for withdrawals and treatment discontinuation, were not reported.CONCLUSIONS: The treatment interventions were not more effective than the comparator in patients with endometrial cancer survivors with obesity.

Imagine scientists wanted to see if using technology like apps and special scales could help women who've had endometrial cancer (a type of cancer) and are overweight lose weight.

They had almost 200 women try three different things for six months:

1. **Telemedicine with special scales:**  Doctors checked in with them online and they used scales that sent their weight to the doctors.
2. **Text messaging:** They got helpful messages on their phones.
3. **Usual care:** This group just got the normal care they'd usually get.

They measured how much weight everyone lost.  Guess what?  None of the special technology methods helped people lose more weight than just getting normal care.  Everyone lost about the same amount (a little less than 10 pounds on average).  The scientists didn't even say if anyone had problems with any of the methods.

So, the study showed that these techy ways to lose weight didn't work any better than usual care for these women.

OBJECTIVE: The aim of this study was to test the efficacy of technology-based weight loss interventions for endometrial cancer (EC) survivors with obesity.

METHODS: EC survivors with obesity (nвЂ‰=вЂ‰196) from three medical centers completed assessments for knowledge of obesity as a risk for EC and interest in weight management. RESULTS: One-third of survey participants lacked awareness that obesity increased the risk of EC, and 40% misclassified their body mass. EUC also produced weight loss, demonstrating a potential for beginning weight management with information on specific diet and exercise goals.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine a group of women who had endometrial cancer (a type of cancer) and were also overweight.  Doctors wanted to see if using technology like phones and internet could help them lose weight and feel better.

They divided these women into three groups:

1. **Telemedicine group:**  Got help from doctors online using video calls and special scales that sent their weight to the doctor.
2. **Texting group:** Got advice and support through text messages.
3. **Regular care group:**  Got the usual advice from their doctors about diet and exercise.

The study found that:

* Many women didn't know being overweight increases the risk of endometrial cancer.
* None of the groups lost a *lot* of weight, but they all lost *some*.
* The telemedicine group felt better physically and about their bodies, and had better sexual health.
* The regular care group exercised more.
* The texting group walked more than the telemedicine group.

Basically, using technology to help these women lose weight was helpful in some ways, but just getting regular advice from a doctor about diet and exercise also helped them lose weight.  It showed that there are different ways to help people lose weight and feel better.

Title: Behavioral Weight Loss Intervention for Migraine: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
Spin in Abstract Title/Results: Other - distract from negative trial by claiming need for future research
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Other
abstract:
Objective: The objective of this study was to test whether behavioral weight loss (BWL) intervention decreases headaches in women with comorbid migraine and overweight or obesity.

Methods: This randomized, single-blind trial allocated women 18 to 50 years old with 4 to 20 migraine days per month and a BMI = 25.0-49.9 kg/m2 to 16 weeks of BWL (n = 54), which targeted exercise and eating behaviors for weight loss, or to migraine education control (ME, n = 56), which delivered didactic instruction on migraine and treatments. Participants completed a 4-week smartphone headache diary at baseline, posttreatment (16-20 wk), and follow-up (32-36 wk). The primary outcome was posttreatment change in migraine days per month, analyzed via linear mixed effects models.

Results: Of 110 participants randomly assigned, 85 (78%) and 80 (73%) completed posttreatment and follow-up. Although the BWL group achieved greater weight loss (mean [95% CI] in kilograms) than the ME group at posttreatment (-3.8 [-2.5 to -5.0] vs. + 0.9 [-0.4 to 2.2], P < 0.001) and follow-up (-3.2 [-2.0 to -4.5] vs. + 1.1 [-0.2 to 2.4], P < 0.001), there were no significant group (BWL vs. ME) differences (mean [95% CI]) in migraine days per month at posttreatment (-3.0 [-2.0 to -4.0] vs. -4.0 [-2.9 to -5.0], P = 0.185) or follow-up (-3.8 [-2.7 to -4.8] vs. -4.4 [-3.4 to -5.5], P = 0.378).

Conclusions: Contrary to hypotheses, BWL and ME yielded similar, sustained reductions in migraine headaches. Future research should evaluate whether adding BWL to standard pharmacological and/or nonpharmacological migraine treatment approaches yields greater benefits.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine scientists wanted to see if losing weight could help women with headaches.  They had two groups of women with headaches who were also a little overweight.

One group (Group A) went on a special weight loss program that focused on eating better and exercising more.  The other group (Group B) learned about headaches and how to manage them.

Both groups kept track of how many headaches they had for a while.  After the program, Group A lost more weight than Group B.  But surprisingly, both groups had about the same number of headaches afterwards!  Even after more time passed, the number of headaches stayed about the same for both groups.

So, the scientists learned that even though losing weight is good, it didn't seem to make a big difference in the number of headaches these women had.  They think maybe losing weight *along with* other headache treatments might help more.

Imagine a science experiment to see if losing weight helps reduce headaches.

Title: Platelet-Rich Plasma Intra-articular Knee Injections Show No Superiority Versus Viscosupplementation: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injections have been proposed as a new conservative option for knee degeneration to provide symptomatic relief and delay surgical intervention. Although the current literature provides some evidence on the benefits of this technique compared with viscosupplementation, no studies have been performed to compare their long-term effects.

PURPOSE: To compare the long-term clinical outcomes provided by intra-articular injections of either PRP or hyaluronic acid (HA) to treat knee degenerative disease.

STUDY DESIGN: Randomized controlled trial; Level of evidence, 1.

METHODS: Patients with a history of chronic symptomatic knee degenerative changes and osteoarthritis (Kellgren-Lawrence grade 0-3) were enrolled: 192 patients were randomized to undergo 3 blinded weekly intra-articular injections of either PRP or HA. Patients were prospectively evaluated before the injection and then at 2, 6, 12, and 24 months and a mean of 64.3 months (SD, 7.8 months) of follow-up. Evaluation was based on International Knee Documentation Committee (IKDC) subjective (main outcome), EuroQol visual analog scale, and Tegner scores; 167 patients reached the final evaluation.

RESULTS: Both treatments were effective in improving knee functional status and symptoms over time: Mean В± SD IKDC subjective score improved significantly for both PRP and HA groups ( P < .0005) and remained stable over time up to 24 months (from 53.3 В± 14.3 to 67.3 В± 18.1 and from 50.3 В± 13.2 to 62.1 В± 20.8 for PRP and HA groups, respectively). At final evaluation, a significant IKDC reduction was observed in both treatment groups, with the PRP group still presenting significantly higher values compared with baseline: PRP 60.5 В± 19.0 ( P < .001 vs baseline), HA 55.7 В± 18.8 (not significant vs baseline). A comparative analysis showed no significant intergroup difference in any of the clinical scores at any follow-up point. The median duration of patient subjective perception of symptomatic relief was 9 months for HA and 12 months for PRP (not significant). The only significant difference was observed in the rate of reintervention at 24 months, which was significantly lower in the PRP group (22.6% vs 37.1%, P = .036).

CONCLUSION: Both treatments were effective in improving knee functional status and symptoms over time. PRP did not provide an overall superior clinical improvement compared with HA in terms of either symptomatic-functional improvement at different follow-up points or effect duration.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee hurts because it's getting old and worn down, like an old toy.  Doctors sometimes use special shots to help.  This study compared two shots: one with something called PRP (think of it like your body's own healing stuff) and another with hyaluronic acid (HA), which is a gooey substance that helps lubricate the knee.

They gave these shots to a bunch of adults with sore knees.  They checked how well their knees felt over a long time – almost five years!

Both shots helped! People's knees felt better and worked better after getting either shot.  But, surprisingly, the PRP shot didn't work *that* much better than the HA shot in the long run.  The only real difference was that fewer people who got the PRP shot needed more treatment later on.

So, both shots are good for helping sore knees, but neither one is clearly better than the other.

Imagine your knee is like a rusty hinge.  Doctors sometimes use special shots to help it work better.  This study compared two types of shots: one with something called PRP (think of it like extra-strong knee juice from your own blood) and another with HA (a type of lubricant).

Title: The use of exparel (liposomal bupivacaine) to manage postoperative pain in unilateral total knee arthroplasty patients.
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim equivalence/non-inferiority versus control for a (-) endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Local periarticular infiltration (PAI) analgesia has emerged as an important component of multimodal approaches to treat total knee arthroplasty postoperative pain. Liposomal bupivacaine may provide prolonged analgesic duration when injected into the surrounding tissues. The purpose of this study was to compare the analgesic efficacy and serum bupivacaine levels of a continuous femoral nerve block (CFNB) with bupivacaine to PAI with liposomal bupivacaine.

METHODS: Sixty-five patients undergoing primary unilateral total knee arthroplasty were randomized into 2 groups: (1) CFNB and PAI with bupivacaine (CFNB group) or (2) PAI with bupivacaine:liposomal bupivacaine mixture at the end of surgery (LB group). The primary outcome was pain intensity at maximum knee flexion 24В hours following surgery. Secondary outcomes included pain intensities at rest and movement at timed intervals and serum bupivacaine levels.

RESULTS: Patients in the CFNB group experienced lower pain scores at maximum knee flexion at 24В hours (7.91; 95% confidence interval, 7.19-8.61) compared to the LB group (8.95; 95% confidence interval, 8.42-9.48; PВ = .02). The mean peak serum bupivacaine level in the LB group up to 72В hours was 0.55 Ојg/mL versus 1.4 Ојg/mL for CFNB group (PВ = .0008) with one patient in the CFNB group exceeding the reported minimum serum bupivacaine threshold for toxicity.

CONCLUSION: While similar pain control was observed on the day of surgery for both groups, patients with a CFNB experienced lower pain intensities during maximum knee flexion at 24В hours. Total serum concentrations in LB group remained below the toxicity threshold over the study period.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your knee is hurt and needs a new joint (like a robot knee!).  Doctors use special medicine to help with the pain after surgery.

This study compared two different pain-killing methods:

* **Method 1 (CFNB):**  A shot of medicine near the nerve in the leg to numb the whole area.
* **Method 2 (LB):** A shot of a special, long-lasting pain medicine near the knee joint.

They gave 65 people with new knee joints one of these methods.  They checked how much pain the people felt at different times.

The study found that:

* On the day of surgery, both methods worked about the same.
* The next day, the people who got Method 1 (the nerve shot) had less pain when they bent their knee.
* The medicine levels in the blood were higher with Method 1, but still safe for almost everyone.

So, even though both methods helped with pain, the nerve shot (Method 1) seemed to work better for pain the day after surgery when bending the knee.

Imagine someone had surgery on their knee to replace the joint.  Doctors want to make sure they don't have too much pain afterward.

* **Group 1:** Got medicine injected near the knee (PAI) and also a special injection near the nerve in the leg (CFNB) to numb it.  Think of it like a double dose of pain relief near the knee.

The study wanted to see which method worked better at reducing pain 24 hours after surgery.  They also measured how much medicine was in the blood.

The results showed that the double dose method (Group 1) didn't actually work *that* much better than just the long-lasting medicine near the knee (Group 2).  The double dose method had more medicine in the blood, and one person even had a bit too much medicine in their blood.

Title: Platelet-rich plasma for arthroscopic repair of medium to large rotator cuff tears: a randomized controlled trial.
Journal: The American Journal of Sports Medicine
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: Claim benefit based on (+) secondary endpoint
abstract:
BACKGROUND: It is unclear whether leukocyte-poor (LP) or leukocyte-rich (LR) varieties of platelet-rich plasma (PRP) as an adjuvant to arthroscopic rotator cuff repair (ARCR) result in improved tendon healing rates.

PURPOSE: To perform a network meta-analysis of the randomized controlled trials in the literature to ascertain whether there is evidence to support the use of LP- or LR-PRP as an adjunct to ARCR.

METHODS: The literature search was based on the PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) guidelines. Randomized controlled trials comparing LP- or LR-PRP with a control alongside ARCR were included. The primary outcome was the rate of retear and/or incomplete tendon healing after fixation. Clinical outcomes were compared using a frequentist approach to network meta-analysis, with statistical analysis performed using R. The treatment options were ranked using the P-score.

RESULTS: There were 13 studies (868 patients) included, with 9 studies comparing LP-PRP with a control and 4 studies comparing LR-PRP with a control. LP-PRP was found to reduce the rate of retear and/or incomplete tendon healing after fixation. LP-PRP had the highest P-score for all treatment groups. LR-PRP did not result in significant improvements over the control group. Post hoc analysis revealed that LP-PRP did not lead to significant improvements over LR-PRP in any category.

CONCLUSION: The treatment LP-PRP was not more effective than LR-PRP in patients with arthroscopic rotator cuff repair on the rate of retear and/or incomplete tendon healing.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is hurt and needs a repair, like fixing a torn piece of a puzzle.  Doctors sometimes use something called PRP (like a special goo made from your own blood) to help the shoulder heal better after the repair.  This goo can be made in two ways: one with fewer white blood cells (LP-PRP) and one with more (LR-PRP).

Scientists wanted to know which type of goo is better at helping the shoulder heal. They looked at lots of studies where people got shoulder repairs and either LP-PRP, LR-PRP, or nothing extra.

They found that the goo with fewer white blood cells (LP-PRP) helped people heal better than not using any extra goo at all.  However, surprisingly, the goo with more white blood cells (LR-PRP) didn't help much more than doing nothing extra.  And, the best goo (LP-PRP) wasn't actually *that* much better than the other type of goo (LR-PRP).

So, in short:  One type of the special goo helped healing a little, but neither type was a super amazing miracle cure compared to the other.

abstract:
BACKGROUND: It is unclear whether leukocyte-poor (LP) or leukocyte-rich (LR) varieties of platelet-rich plasma (PRP) as an adjuvant to arthroscopic rotator cuff repair (ARCR) result in improved tendon healing rates.

PURPOSE: To perform a network meta-analysis of the randomized controlled trials in the literature to ascertain whether there is evidence to support the use of LP- or LR-PRP as an adjunct to ARCR.

METHODS: The literature search was based on the PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) guidelines. Randomized controlled trials comparing LP- or LR-PRP with a control alongside ARCR were included. Clinical outcomes, including retears and functional outcomes, were compared using a frequentist approach to network meta-analysis, with statistical analysis performed using R. The treatment options were ranked using the P-score.

RESULTS: There were 13 studies (868 patients) included, with 9 studies comparing LP-PRP with a control and 4 studies comparing LR-PRP with a control. LP-PRP was found to significantly reduce the rate of retear and/or incomplete tendon healing after fixation, even among medium-large tears; it also improved outcomes on the visual analog scale for pain, Constant score, and University of California Los Angeles score. LP-PRP had the highest P-score for all treatment groups. LR-PRP did not result in any significant improvements over the control group, except for visual analog scale score for pain. However, post hoc analysis revealed that LP-PRP did not lead to significant improvements over LR-PRP in any category.

CONCLUSION: The current study demonstrates that LP-PRP reduces the rate of retear and/or incomplete tendon healing after ARCR and improves patient-reported outcomes as compared with a control. However, it is still unclear whether LP-PRP improves the tendon healing rate when compared with LR-PRP.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your shoulder is hurt and needs a repair, like fixing a torn piece of a puzzle.  Doctors sometimes use something called PRP (like a special goo made from your own blood) to help the shoulder heal better after the repair.  This goo can be made in two ways: one with fewer white blood cells (LP-PRP) and one with more (LR-PRP).

Scientists wanted to know which type of goo is better. They looked at lots of studies where people got shoulder repairs and either LP-PRP, LR-PRP, or nothing extra.

They found that the goo with fewer white blood cells (LP-PRP) helped people's shoulders heal better and less likely to tear again.  It also helped reduce pain.  The goo with more white blood cells (LR-PRP) didn't really help much more than doing nothing extra.

However, they couldn't say for sure if the goo with fewer white blood cells was *definitely* better than the goo with more white blood cells.  More research is needed to be completely sure.

Title: Analysis of Gastric Physiology After Laparoscopic Sleeve Gastrectomy (LSG) With or Without Antral Preservation in Relation to Metabolic Response: a Randomised Study.

abstract:
BACKGROUND: Immunometabolism is the interaction between immune system and nutrient metabolism. Severe obesity is considered a state of meta-inflammation associated with obesity that influences the development of chronic-degenerative diseases.OBJECTIVE: We aimed to establish the immunometabolic differences in bariatric patients and to determine whether cellular immunity is associated with metabolic changes.METHODOLOGY: We conducted an observational study in patients who underwent laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy (LSG) or laparoscopic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (LRYGB). We explored the differences in the immunometabolic profile before and after surgery in the study group, by surgical technique, and we evaluated the changes in immunological variables as a function of metabolic variables with correlation analysis.

RESULTS: The follow-up rate was 88.7%. After the intervention, there were changes in cellular immunity, with a decrease in effector T lymphocytes (CD8+CD28-) and an increase in B lymphocytes, memory helper T cells, and cytotoxic T lymphocytes. LSG resulted in a greater decrease in (CD4+CD62-)вЂ‰T lymphocytes compared with LRYGB. Patients who underwent surgery with LRYGB presented greater clinical and metabolic improvements, as well as improvement of obesity-associated medical problems. Women who underwent LRYGB showed a greater reduction in fat-free mass compared with women who underwent LSG.

CONCLUSION: Bariatric surgery, mainly LRYGB, leads to immunometabolic changes and improves associated medical problems.
plain_language_summary:
Imagine your body is like a car.  It needs fuel (food) to run, and it has a defense system (immune system) to fight off germs.  Immunometabolism is how these two things work together.

Some people are very overweight (severely obese), and their bodies are kind of like a car that's always slightly on fire (meta-inflammation). This can lead to health problems later in life.

Scientists wanted to see if a special type of surgery (bariatric surgery, like making the stomach smaller) could help fix this "car fire." They studied people who had this surgery and looked at their immune systems and how their bodies used food before and after the surgery.

They found that the surgery *did* change the immune system.  Some types of immune cells went up, others went down. One type of surgery (LRYGB) seemed to work better than another (LSG) at fixing the "car fire" and improving people's health.

In short:  The study showed that surgery can help people who are very overweight by improving how their bodies fight off germs and use food.  One type of surgery worked better than another.

plain_language_summary:
Imagine your body has two teams:  the immune team (fighting off germs) and the energy team (using food for energy).  Immunometabolism is how these two teams work together.

Some people are very overweight, and that causes low-level inflammation (like a tiny bit of swelling all the time) throughout their bodies. This can lead to diseases later in life.

Scientists wanted to see if two types of weight-loss surgery (LSG and LRYGB – think of them as two different ways to shrink the stomach) could help these teams work better.  They studied people before and after surgery.

They found that both surgeries helped the immune team change, but LRYGB (one type of surgery) seemed to help people lose more weight and get healthier overall.  However, surprisingly, the study concluded that neither surgery was *significantly* better than the other at improving overall health.  It's a bit complicated, and more research is needed.
